title: Young Professionals: Learning to Learn: Reflections from ISDEIV 2025 and a Career in Science
authors: Moein Borghei
abstract: The city of Chengdu, home of giant pandas and spicy Sichuan cuisine, was the host to the electrical insulation community for the 31st International Symposium on Discharges and Electrical Insulation in Vacuum (ISDEIV) from September 21 to 26, 2025. The event, impeccably organized by Sichuan University (chaired by Shenli Jia) and technically sponsored by the IEEE Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation Society (DEIS) along with sponsorship from key industry leaders, brought together 288 researchers, engineers, and students from across Asia, Europe, and the Americas.

title: CF-LRDM: An Efficient Channel Fingerprint Construction Approach Assisted by Latent Residual-based Diffusion Model
authors: Wenya Fan, Zhenzhou Jin, Li You, Chenjie Xie, Xiqi Gao
abstract: Accurate acquisition of channel state information (CSI) for massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems is of paramount importance for next-generation wireless networks. An emerging approach termed channel fingerprint (CF) can store essential site-specific channel knowledge information within the target communication area, which is conducive to the acquisition of CSI. However, limited by hardware cost and storage overhead, most available CFs are coarse-grained, which fail to provide sufficient and precise channel-related knowledge. To tackle this challenge, we propose an efficient generative approach for CF construction, namely latent residual-based diffusion model (CF-LRDM). Specifically, different from traditional generative diffusion models (GDMs) with ill-posed generating targets and limited inference efficiency, the proposed CF-LRDM establishes a Markov chain between fine-grained and coarse-grained CFs via residual-shifting and can sample from a given coarse-grained CF to a fine-grained CF with only 15 sampling steps. Experimental results demonstrate superior performance of the proposed method against existing baselines.

title: IGBT Open-Circuit Fault Detection and Localization Method for Cascaded H-Bridge Stage of Power Electronic Transformer
authors: Tao Zheng, Chong Wang, Yufei Zhao
abstract: The rectifier stage of power electronic transformers (PETs) commonly employs either a cascaded H-bridge (CHB) or a modular multilevel converter (MMC) topology. Due to its high modularity, scalability, and ease of redundancy design, the CHB topology is widely adopted in PETs. However, the use of numerous power electronic devices in PETs increases the probability of faults. In this paper, a novel fault detection and localization method is proposed for the rapid identification of IGBT open-circuit faults in the CHB stages of PETs. A mathematical model of the CHB grid-side voltage is established, which contains abundant harmonic information. The harmonic characteristics of the grid-side voltage under different IGBT open-circuit faults are analyzed, revealing that the amplitude and phase of even-order carrier frequency harmonics depend on the location of the faulty H-bridges. Based on this characteristic, the fault region is identified. By adjusting the modulation scheme of specific H-bridges and analyzing the resulting variations in the grid-side voltage, multiple IGBT open-circuit faults can be accurately localized. The proposed method utilizes the characteristic harmonics of the H-bridge port theoretical voltage as a reference, thereby reducing fault-identification time and remaining unaffected by load conditions. Its effectiveness is validated through PSCAD/EMTDC simulations and hardware-in-the-loop experiments.

title: Detecting Abnormal Vehicle Behavior with a Diffusion-Transformer Generation Framework
authors: Zhiyu Wang, Linheng Li, Xu Qu, Bin Ran
abstract: With the rapid development of intelligent transportation systems (ITS), ensuring traffic safety has become a critical societal priority. Abnormal driving behavior detection has emerged as an essential approach to mitigate potential traffic risks, as even minor abnormal behaviors can lead to severe safety hazards through cascading effects under specific environmental conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an innovative framework for the detection of abnormal vehicle behavior, built on the deep integration of a diffusion model and the Transformer architecture. The proposed framework adopts a two-stage process: a generation stage, where a classifier-free denoising diffusion model reconstructs the normal trajectory distribution from Gaussian noise iteratively, and a comparison stage, where deviations between observed trajectories and generated normal trajectory distributions are quantified to detect anomalies. This method eliminates the need for hand-made thresholds, overcoming the subjectivity and rigidity of traditional anomaly detection approaches, and effectively addresses the severe class imbalance between normal and abnormal samples often observed in real-world traffic scenarios. In addition, extensive evaluations of real-world traffic datasets demonstrate the superiority of the framework, achieving a precision of 0.948, a precision of 0.317, a recall of 0.765, and an F1-score of 0.448, significantly exceeding state-of-the-art benchmarks. These results underscore the potential of the proposed framework as a robust foundation for proactive early-warning systems in ITS, contributing to safer and more efficient transportation networks.

title: An Explainable Multimodal Vision–Language Framework with Adaptive Mixture of Experts and Optimized Learning for Remote Sensing Image Captioning and Visual Question Answering
authors: M Balakrishna Mallapu, Deepthi Godavarthi
abstract: The provision of accurate and explainable captions and answers to visual questions about remote sensing images is essential in areas such as disaster management, land-use supervision, and military analysis, where system interpretability is a critical factor for decision-making. However, existing vision–language models, powered by transformers and multimodal encoders, often struggle to generalize across diverse scene types and lack transparent reasoning mechanisms, thereby limiting their applicability in critical domains. To address these challenges, thisresearch aims to develop a unified and explainable vision–language framework that improves both caption generation and visual question answering performance while ensuring interpretability. Although the current vision-language models using the transformer architecture, multimodal encoders, and attention-based decoders achieve promising results, the standardization of diverse scene types and transparent decision-making remain to be addressed. Therefore, this research presents the application of the explainable vision-language model with the incorporation of the Mixture of Experts-based Robustly bidirectional encoder representations from the transformers AutoEncoder (MoERAE) block to the caption generation task. The MoERAE model utilizes the dynamically routing mechanism for the routing of the multimodal features using different expert paths to capture the context-based representation of the different scenarios in remote sensing. The framework further incorporates an Attention-based Vision Transformer (AVT) for robust spatial feature extraction and GPT-4 for effective multimodal alignment and semantic reasoning. The fine-tuning of MoERAE is carried out by the Rabbit and Turtle Algorithm (RTA), which is an adaptive optimizer that ensures the optimal selection of experts by striking a balance between rapid exploration and convergence. Thus, the quality of the captions generated is very high. Furthermore, the Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) technique has been employed to ascertain the importance of specific visual and textual tokens in the generation of captions and answers, thus promoting transparency. The framework is tested and validated on several benchmark datasets such as UCM-Captions, Sydney-Captions, and RSICD. The metrics for evaluating the framework are also provided. The experimental results prove that the proposed framework performs better than existing ones by achieving a high of 99% and a cross-validation accuracy of 99%, thereby proving the robustness and generalization of the framework. The results also prove that the integration of multimodal alignment, adaptive expert routing, and explainability mechanisms into the framework significantly enhances its performance and interpretability for practical use cases in the field of remote sensing.

title: NASchecker: Automatically Identifying the Performance, Security, and Privacy Issues of NAS Devices
authors: Guangyue Ren, Jincheng Wang, Le Yu, Liping Han, Mingzhe Hu, Wei Chen, Tingting Liu, Xiapu Luo, Guozi Sun
abstract: Network attached storage (NAS) devices are widely deployed for personal data storage. However, their distributed architecture and limited inspection interfaces pose significant challenges for comprehensive performance, security, and privacy analysis. In this paper, we first establish a threat model for NAS ecosystems. Then, we present a systematic framework NASchecker for discovering performance optimization mechanisms, security threats, and privacy leakage in NAS devices. By analyzing traffic generated during varied file operations on crafted files, NASchecker infers implemented optimizations and identifies security flaws within the traffic (e.g., susceptibility to passive sniffing and replay attacks). NASchecker also integrates NAS-specific protocol fuzzing and firmware reverse engineering to uncover deep-seated command injection, memory corruption, and improper access control vulnerabilities. NASchecker compares personally identifiable information (PII) leaked in traffic against declarations in privacy policies to detect privacy compliance issues. We evaluated NASchecker on twelve commercial NAS devices. Our results reveal that none of the tested devices employ file compression or deduplication. From a security standpoint, ten devices are vulnerable to passive sniffing and seven to replay attacks. Moreover, seven devices are affected by command injection, four by memory corruption, and eleven by improper access control. From a privacy perspective, four devices leaked PIIs that were not disclosed in their respective privacy policies. After reporting the findings to the manufacturers, we have been acknowledged by several manufacturers, resulting in the assignment of 20 CVEs and 6 NVDB entries (16 of them are rated as high severity). These findings validate NASchecker’s effectiveness and underscore the urgent need for improved design and testing practices of NAS.

title: Collaborative Design of Conductor Plate and Switching Pattern for SiC Three-Level ANPC Inverter in PMSM Drives
authors: Guanzhou Ren, Guangtong Ma, Hong Xie, Hong Wan, Shuai Xu
abstract: In recent years, the silicon carbide (SiC) power devices have been widely applied in permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drives. However, their application in three-level inverters introduces critical design challenges, including low stray inductance busbar configurations and balanced thermal distribution. To overcome these challenges, this article presents a collaborative design of conductor plate and switching pattern for SiC three-level active neutral-point-clamped (ANPC) inverters. First, a four-layer laminated conductor plate is developed to minimize switching overshoot and reduce stray inductance. The conductor plate geometries are optimized for balanced thermal distribution. Then, to mitigate common-mode current and enhance dielectric reliability, a multimode space vector pulse width modulation (SVPWM) is proposed. Through the switching patterns optimization, the common-mode voltage and switching frequency are suppressed simultaneously. Additionally, the optimized switching pattern achieves precise neutral-point voltage balance by dynamically adjusting the dwell times of four trapezoidal-shaped vectors. The experimental verification with PMSM drive and resistive-inductive load demonstrates the efficiency and robustness of the designed configuration and the optimized modulation scheme.

title: CaMT-iTire: Context-aware Multi-Task Learning for intelligent Tire Framework
authors: Suwoong Heo, Hyewon Moon, Seunghyun Chung, Kwonwoo Lee, Sunghan You
abstract: Intelligent tire systems (ITS) enable direct sensing of tire and road interaction using sensors mounted inside the tire, providing information that is difficult to obtain from conventional vehicle-mounted sensors. However, acceleration signals measured from tire-mounted inertial measurement unit (IMU) sensors are influenced by multiple physically coupled factors, including vertical load, wheel alignment, and varying operating conditions. This coupling limits the robustness of conventional single-task estimators and multi-task learning approaches in real-world driving environments. In this paper, we propose CaMT-iTire, a context-aware multi-task learning framework designed to jointly estimate wheel load and wheel alignment parameters (toe and camber angles) from a single tire accelerometer sensor mounted on inner liner of a tire. The proposed framework combines a learnable Sinc-based filterbank with a condition-aware convex band-mixing layer to perform adaptive frequency-selective feature extraction from raw acceleration signals. In addition, a Progressive Layered Extraction (PLE) backbone with context-aware gating is introduced to mitigate inter-task interference by dynamically routing features according to operating conditions. Extensive experiments conducted on both controlled drum-test and real world on-road driving data demonstrate that the proposed CaMT-iTire framework consistently outperforms conventional single-task and standard multi-task learning baselines. The results show improved estimation accuracy and robustness, particularly across the operating conditions covered in our datasets, highlighting the effectiveness of the proposed context-aware multi-task learning approach for intelligent tire.

title: STPNet: A Dual-Channel Spatiotemporal Network for Reweighting-Based Smartphone Positioning in Complex Urban Environments
authors: Li Liu, Hua Chen, Jian Wang, Zhao Li, Ran Lu, Kai Liu
abstract: The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) provides absolute positioning for Internet of Things (IoT) devices, with smartphones being the most pervasive user terminals. However, in urban environments, non-line-of-sight (NLOS) interference severely degrades GNSS positioning on smartphones. Machine-learning (ML)-based NLOS mitigation has attracted increasing attention, but the existing methods often rely on single-level feature representations. Moreover, detected NLOS signals are commonly excluded or assigned relatively low weights, thus ignoring their contributions in the observation equation and leading to degraded positioning accuracy. To address these issues, this study proposes STPNet, a dual-channel spatiotemporal model for NLOS-aware observation reweighting in smartphone-based GNSS positioning. STPNet learns per-satellite spatial representations and multi-epoch temporal dependencies from smartphone raw GNSS observations, and the predicted outputs are then employed for pseudorange correction and observation reweighting. Experimental results show that STPNet achieves an NLOS classification accuracy of 92.7%, markedly outperforming baseline ML methods. In terms of horizontal and vertical positioning accuracy, the proposed method achieves improvements of 81.4% and 83.8%, respectively, compared with traditional filtering methods. Analysis of the model window length indicates that longer temporal sequences improve positioning accuracy but increase inference latency, with a window of 12–16 epochs exhibiting an effective trade-off for smartphone deployment. This method provides a robust and practical solution for smart mobility and real-time navigation in urban GNSS-challenged environments.

title: Multi-Scale Residual Denoising Network for Robust InSAR Phase Reconstruction
authors: Qi Zhang, Houjun Jiang, Teng Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) phase denoising is essential for deformation monitoring and topographic mapping, yet it remains challenging due to the inherent trade-off between denoising strength, structural preservation, and computational efficiency. Classical filters maintain phase integrity but oversmooth fine details, non-local methods deliver effective noise suppression at high computational cost, and existing deep learning approaches often alter low-SNR regions, introducing artifacts. To overcome these limitations, we propose the Multi-Scale Residual Denoising Network (MSRD-Net), a hierarchical phase reconstruction framework that integrates the nonlinear recovery capability of deep learning with the robustness of frequency-domain filtering. MSRD-Net decomposes interferometric phase into low-frequency and corresponding residual components, preserving dominant structures while progressively restoring fine-scale details. A self-boosting training strategy using real-world noise extracted from Sentinel-1 and TanDEM-X data further improves the network's robustness and generalization. Evaluations across four representative scenarios—earthquakes, landslides, rainforests, and urban areas—show that MSRD-Net achieves a balanced improvement in noise suppression, structural fidelity, and efficiency. Furthermore, its scalable design allows adaptive adjustment of denoising strength, while its iterative nature ensures reliable performance under extreme noise. These characteristics establish MSRD-Net as a practical and versatile solution for both large-scale operational monitoring and high-precision geoscience studies with InSAR.

title: A Physics Parameter-Guided Metalearning Framework for Temperature Prediction of Double-Sided Double-Glass TopCon Photovoltaic Modules
authors: Huajian Ding, Zhongyuan Su, Jun Wang
abstract: Aiming at the need for accurate prediction operational temperature of N-type TOPCon bifacial double-glass photovoltaic modules, this article proposes an integrated forecasting method combining physical principles with metalearning. A metalearner model based on a Stacking ensemble framework is constructed, which integrates predictions from a thermodynamic model and a dual-branch long short-term memory (LSTM) network through a dynamic weighting mechanism. Cross-validation strategies are employed to train deep neural networks, enabling multiscale feature integration and weight self-adjustment. Experimental results achieve a root mean square error (RMSE) of only 0.95°C, representing a 66% reduction compared to the physical model (RMSE = 2.81°C) and a 14% reduction compared to the LSTM model (RMSE = 1.11°C), substantially improving the model’s adaptability in nonlinear and dynamic scenarios.

title: Towards adaptive optimal personalization control of robotic hip exoskeleton assistance for individuals with paretic stroke_supp2-3685273.mp4
authors: Qiang Zhang
abstract: Neurological disorders, like stroke, can re duce the mobility and quality of life for millions of indi viduals across the world. As promising gait assistive de vices, powered lower-limb exoskeletons have gained wide attention for their potential to improve walking function for individuals with mobility impairments. However, due to the complex human-robot interaction, highly nonlinear dynamics modeling, and person-to-person variability, it is challenging to provide optimal customized assistance from these exoskeletons. In the current study, we showed that a reinforcement learning (RL)-based control framework pro vided adaptive and optimal personalized assistance across the hip joint to improve walking function for individuals with and without mobility deficits. We selected hip kinematic spatial gait symmetry as the control objective for five par ticipants with hemiparetic stroke, and found three optimal control parameters over an average of 201.7±36.1 sec. The kinematic spatial gait symmetry was defined as the joint excursion symmetry on both hip joints within the same gait cycle. By adding optimal assistance, we observed that the joint excursion during gait cycles at the affected hip joint was increased from 37.09° to 48.03° by 29.5% (p = 0.003) and the joint excursion gait symmetry was improved from 7.55% to-0.63% (p = 0.002) compared to transparent mode (no assistance). The findings suggest that the application of an RL-based assistance personalization control scheme to a wearable hip exoskeleton may positively impact gait rehabilitation training outcomes in a clinical population.

title: On the Study of Integrated Sensing and Communication: From an Information Timeliness Perspective
authors: Chao Jia, Zhongyuan Zhao, Like Sun, Tony Q. S. Quek
abstract: In integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, the timeliness performance is pivotal to support high-precision sensing and reliable high-rate data communication, particularly for real-time applications. However, there are two critical challenges in the research on the timeliness of ISAC systems: joint characterization of sensing-communication time-liness tradeoffs and unified resource allocation optimization. To address these challenges, a timeliness-centric analysis and optimization framework is proposed for ISAC systems. First, the age of information (AoI) of sensing tasks and the delay of communication tasks are analyzed to evaluate the timeliness performance of ISAC systems. Then, the closed-form expressions of the outage probabilities for sensing and communication tasks are derived, quantifying their reliability under dynamic channel conditions. Next, a joint sensing-and-communication time-allocation optimization problem is established to minimize the weighted sum of sensing AoI and communication delay. To tackle the non-linear integer programming problem, a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm is designed, which can be verified to converge to an optimal solution with polynomial computational complexity. Simulation results are presented to validate the accuracy of the analytical results, and demonstrate the optimality and performance gains of our proposed DP algorithm.

title: Multipath Component Power Delay Profile Based Joint Range and Doppler Estimation for AFDM-ISAC Systems
authors: Fangqing Xiao, Zunqi Li, Dirk Slock
abstract: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems combine sensing and communication functionalities within a unified framework, enhancing spectral efficiency and reducing costs by utilizing shared hardware components. This paper investigates multipath component power delay profile (MPCPDP)-based joint range and Doppler estimation for Affine Frequency Division Multiplexing (AFDM)-ISAC systems. The path resolvability of the equivalent channel in the AFDM system allows the recognition of Line-of-Sight (LoS) and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) paths within a single pilot symbol in fast time-varying channels. We develop a joint estimation model that leverages multipath Doppler shifts and delays information under the AFDM waveform. Utilizing the MPCPDP, we propose a novel ranging method that exploits the range-dependent magnitude of the MPCPDP across its delay spread by constructing a Nakagami-m statistical fading model for MPC channel fading and correlating the distribution parameters with propagation distance in AFDM systems. without requiring additional ranging-specific time synchronization beyond standard receiver coarse synchronization, since the method exploits relative delays and MPCPDP statistics. We also transform the nonlinear Doppler estimation problem into a bilinear estimation problem using a First-order Taylor expansion. Moreover, we introduce the Expectation Maximization algorithm to estimate the hyperparameters and leverage the Expectation Consistent algorithm to cope with high-dimensional integration challenges. Extensive numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of our MPCPDP-based joint range and Doppler estimation in ISAC systems.

title: Entropy Injection in Fused Market Networks: HSBM–MDL Quantification of News-Driven Structural Reconfiguration
authors: Jinze Yang, Kaira Sekiguchi, Yukio Ohsawa
abstract: Understanding how semantic information flow reorganizes market topology remains an open challenge in financial network analysis. Prior work typically emphasizes price correlations or sentiment polarity, but rarely quantifies the structural response of the market network to information inflow within a unified coding framework. We propose a fusion-based approach to measure topology-aware information impact. Specifically, we construct a Price Network GP from return co-movements and a Semantic Network GN from LLM-based text embeddings, and integrate them into a Fused Network GF . Using the Hierarchical Stochastic Block Model (HSBM) under the minimum description length principle, we estimate structural entropies for GF and GP (HF and HP), and define the marginal structural cost of information injection as ΔH = HF − HP. Empirically, using S&P 100 constituents over 2023–2025, we find that ΔH is consistently positive, indicating that semantic information inflow acts as an entropy injector relative to the price backbone. High-risk regimes (periods of elevated volatility) exhibit markedly larger ΔH together with a reorganization of the inferred hierarchy, characterized by deeper partitions and increased fine-grained communities, consistent with micro-fragmentation of market structure. At the macro level, normalized modularity ZQ declines on high-volatility days, suggesting weaker sectoral separability and intensified cross-community coupling. Finally, permutation-based counterfactual tests that disrupt semantic alignment show that the observed regime separation inΔH is not attributable to arbitrary mixing, supporting the interpretation that information shocks induce organized structural reconfiguration rather than random disorder. Overall, our framework provides a principled, topology-aware link between semantic information flow and market-wide risk states.

title: Contrastive Learning for Terrain Identification of Earthworm-Like Robots with Limited Labeled Proprioceptive Data
authors: Hongsen Pang, Hanzhuo Zhang, Yunfei Zheng, Hongbin Fang
abstract: Accurate terrain perception is essential for autonomous decision-making in earthworm-like robots, which navigate complex environments using retrograde peristaltic gaits. During locomotion, these robots generate proprioceptive feedback through ground interaction, making visionless terrain recognition possible. However, conventional learning-based methods typically rely on large labeled datasets, which are costly and impractical for field-deployed earthworm-like robots. In this study, we develop an inertial sensor system to capture the robot’s proprioceptive signals and propose a contrastive learning framework for efficient terrain identification with minimal supervision. The model employs data augmentation strategies—both strong and weak—to generate diverse views of the same data, facilitating robust feature extraction. A Temporal Convolutional Network and autoregressive Transformer are used to capture multi-scale temporal dependencies from raw IMU signals. To further enhance performance under limited labeled data, we introduce a semi-supervised fine-tuning stage, which generates pseudo-labels for unlabeled data and applies supervised contrastive loss to refine the learned representations. Extensive experiments across varied terrains and gaits show that our method consistently outperforms existing supervised and self-supervised baselines, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with minimal labeled data. This approach addresses a key challenge in proprioceptive terrain recognition and offers a scalable, efficient solution for real-time deployment in resource-constrained earthworm-like robotic platforms.

title: IRKAN: Wavelet-based Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks for Infrared Small Target Detection
authors: Peng Ding, Xiaolin Wei, Weihua He, Wuming Wu
abstract: Infrared small target detection is fundamentally challenged by the poor generalization of deep learning models across diverse sensors and imaging resolutions. To address this limitation, a novel framework named IRKAN is proposed, which reimagines the network’s core feature extraction mechanism. The proposed approach moves beyond conventional architectures by adopting the Kolmogorov-Arnold Network paradigm, replacing fixed, node-based activations with learnable functions on the network edges. This design endows the model with the intrinsic capability to learn optimal, data-driven transformations for complex infrared signals. To tailor this adaptive framework for the specific physics of infrared small target detection, wavelet basis functions are employed to parameterize these activations. This innovation infuses a powerful signal-processing prior into the network, leveraging the time-frequency localization properties of wavelets to function as a learnable matched filter, adept at isolating the faint, high-frequency signatures of targets from background clutter. By strategically embedding these specialized wavelet-parameterized Kolmogorov-Arnold Network modules into a U-Net backbone, IRKAN achieves strong performance on the public WideIRSTD-Full dataset. Evaluated over ten independent runs, it attains a mean normalized Intersection over Union of 68.89% and a low False Alarm Rate of 8.33 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−6</sup>, while maintaining computational efficiency (3.96M parameters). The synergy between the architectural flexibility of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks and the signal-aware inductive bias of wavelets provides a robust solution to the challenges of modern infrared small target detection. Zero-shot evaluations on the unseen NUDT-SIRSTD (512 × 512) and IRSTD-1K (256 × 256) datasets indicate that IRKAN possesses strong cross-resolution generalization capabilities, particularly under resolution shifts, without requiring fine-tuning.

title: A High Gain and Fully Metallic Ku-Band Antenna Design based on High-Order Mode Method
authors: Zhaoqi Bian, Yuanxi Cao, Fei Yang, Hengfeng Wang, Sen Yan
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a fully metallic high-gain antenna operating in Ku-band. A high-order mode (HOM) method is employed to calculate the dispersion curves of periodic structure accurately. This approach enables convenient acquisition of the radiation efficiency of slot antennas, and provide a guidance for slot array design. A single-ridge waveguide is adopted as the transmission waveguide of the slot array, which effectively expands the operating bandwidth and narrows the array element spacing, thus greatly improving the aperture efficiency. Additionally, to accommodate the small spacing caused by the ridge waveguide, a compact H-plane ridge waveguide T-junction with a matching slab is designed for the feeding network. This T-junction requires no complex ridge deformation or additional impedance matching lines, thus effectively saving the space of feeding network. The measured peak gain of the antenna prototype reaches 33.45 dBi, with an operating bandwidth of 7.3%. Its radiation efficiency and aperture efficiency reach 85.2% and 81.4%, respectively.

title: Sub-30 nm Silicon Nanopatterning: Toward Reduced Reflectance and Enhanced Sensitivity in CMOS Image Sensors
authors: ByoungGyu Kim, Yongkeun Lee
abstract: This work investigated how subwavelength silicon patterning and dielectric passivation influence the optical and electrical performance of advanced CMOS image sensors (CIS). Hole arrays with sidewall angles of 73°, 79°, and 86° were fabricated using industrial plasma-etch processes, with and without aluminum-oxide/hafnium-oxide passivation and antireflective (AR) coatings. Reflectance measurements from 360–1000 nm showed that etch geometry and dielectric filling substantially reduced Fresnel reflection, with the 79° profile achieving the lowest broadband reflectance and film-filled structures providing additional suppression in the blue–green region. Device-level evaluations on 0.64 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m-pixel CIS sensors demonstrated RGB sensitivity improvements of 0.7–3.3% under low illumination (23 lux, 30 ms), with no increase in crosstalk. These trends correlated closely with wafer-level reflectance, confirming reflectance as a reliable early predictor of device performance. Although certain etch conditions increased dark current due to plasma-induced damage, the aluminum-oxide/hafnium-oxide passivation stack effectively mitigated interface traps, enabling stable electrical behavior for optimized profiles. Overall, the results demonstrated that co-optimizing etch geometry, dielectric materials, and process conditions enhances both optical efficiency and electrical robustness, providing a manufacturable path toward higher-sensitivity CIS devices in submicron-pixel technology.[2025-0151]

title: High Gain Observer Based Simultaneous Identification of Stator Resistance and Rotor Speed for Sensorless Induction Motor Drives
authors: Lisi Tian, Lijuan Wang, Tiantuo He, Lei Xu, Yu Wang, Yujie Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: To address the issues of system instability and control performance degradation in sensorless induction motor drives caused by stator resistance mismatch under low-speed conditions, this paper proposes and investigates a dual-parameter simultaneous identification method based on an Adaptive High-Gain Observer (AHGO). By constructing an augmented system, the proposed method achieves real-time, synchronous online identification of both rotor speed and stator resistance. Consequently, it effectively mitigates the risk of system instability induced by the excessive parameter sensitivity inherent in conventional single-parameter identification strategies. Experimental results fully validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method under various complex low-speed operating conditions.

title: D2VAuth: Efficient Drone-to-Vehicle Authentication Using Blockchain and Hyperelliptic Curve Cryptography
authors: Zeeshan Ali Haider, Muhammad Fayaz, Inam Ullah, Muneera Altayeb, Fahad Alblehai, Amr Tolba
abstract: Integrating consumer-grade drones into the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) can extend coverage and enable low-latency services, but it also introduces authentication challenges due to high mobility, intermittent connectivity, resource constraints, and the reliance on centralized trusted authorities, which increases exposure to physical compromise and sophisticated attacks. Existing drone-assisted IoV authentication schemes are often computationally expensive, insufficiently scalable, or fragile under disconnections. To address these gaps, we propose drone-to-vehicle authentication (D2VAuth), a lightweight drone-to-vehicle authentication framework that combines Hyperelliptic Curve Cryptography (HECC) for energy-efficient key establishment with a permissioned blockchain using Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) for decentralized trust management, auditability, and revocation. D2VAuth supports offline and delayed authentication and resists common attacks, including Sybil, replay, cloning, Man-in-the-Middle (MITM), and Denial of Service (DoS). OMNeT++/Castalia simulations across six mobility and density scenarios demonstrate stable overhead, with per-node energy consumption of 32.82-32.86 mJ, single-session creation time of 5.1-135 ms, two-session creation time of 39.2-162 ms, and low packet loss of 1.8-4.1 under higher-density deployments. These results indicate that D2VAuth provides scalable, energy-efficient, and resilient authentication suitable for practical drone-assisted IoV applications such as logistics, surveillance, and secure vehicular networks.

title: A Constant Current and Constant Voltage Hybrid Wireless Battery Charging With Dual Frequency Data Transfer System
authors: Xiang Xu, Ping Jin, Yujing Guo, Junshun Wang, Gang Lei, Jianguo Zhu
abstract: To achieve constant current (CC) and constant voltage (CV) outputs in the power transmission terminal of the simultaneous wireless power and data transfer (SWPDT) system, and to address the research gap in existing data transmission ends regarding single-side dual-frequency parallel data streams, this article proposes a novel dual-channel SWPDT topology. In the power transmission channel, an LCC-CLC compensation structure with a switching circuit is introduced, which reduces the interference of power transmission on data transmission and enables the load to operate in a wide-range resonance under both CC and CV modes. In the data transmission channel, based on a dual-frequency resonance decoupling compensation (DRDC) network, the decoupling of parallel data branches are realized. Moreover, an LC parallel branch is utilized to suppress the interference between the power and the data transmission channels. Theoretical analyses are conducted on CC and CV output characteristics of the power channel topology and the dual-frequency transmission characteristics of the parallel data channel topology. Finally, an experimental prototype is built to verify the correctness and effectiveness of the proposed SWPDT topology.

title: LEO Satellites Cooperative Channel Estimation for Space-MIMO OFDM Uplink
authors: Zohre Mashayekh Bakhsh, Zhenyu Liu, Ke-Xin Li, Yi Ma, Rahim Tafazolli
abstract: This letter explores cooperative channel estimation for space-MIMO OFDM uplinks with multiple low-Earth-orbit (LEO) satellites. By leveraging spatial orthogonality across satellites, we mitigate pilot contamination in massive MIMO multi-user scenarios. To reduce inter-satellite link (ISL) feedback overhead, we propose a sensing-matrix-based scheme, where satellites forward only selected antenna signals to the master node (MN), preserving dominant signal energy. Simulation results show that cooperative satellites improve spatial decorrelation by 55% and reduce normalized mean square error (NMSE) by 83% compared to non-cooperative random pilot assignment. The proposed compression scheme effectively reduces ISL payload while maintaining channel estimation performance.

title: Domain Dropout-driven Prototype Enhancement Network for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Hyperspectral Change Detection
authors: Dehao Wang, Lina Xu, Yanni Dong, Ke Wu, Tao Chen, Yuxiang Zhang, Bo Du
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep learning has shown remarkable effectiveness in hyperspectral image change detection. Cross-domain few-shot learning has emerged as an effective solution to label scarcity in the target domain by exploiting labeled source domain data. However, existing methods often suffer from the interference of domain-sensitive channels and the instability of class prototypes. To address these issues, this article proposes a domain dropout-driven prototype enhancement network for cross-domain few-shot hyperspectral change detection. The method is built upon the metric-based meta-learning to learn transferable knowledge before adapting to new hyperspectral image change detection tasks. Specifically, a dual-branch domain dropout-driven feature extractor is constructed to adaptively dropout domain-sensitive channels, thereby extracting domain-invariant features to mitigate domain shift. Subsequently, a history-aware prototype enhancement module is designed to accumulate historical and current prototype information, thus improving the representativeness and stability of class prototypes. Finally, the trained model is transferred to the target domain to construct a change detection inference module under few-shot conditions. Experiments on several hyperspectral datasets illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm.

title: LMF-Net: Lightweight Multi-dimensional Feature Fusion Network for Floating Debris Detection on Water Surfaces
authors: Pengfei Shi, Hao Wu, Yang Gu, Yuanxue Xin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Detecting floating debris on water surfaces is essential for environmental protection but remains highly challenging due to low contrast, irregular shapes, and significant noise interference in complex aquatic environments. Existing end-to-end detectors based on the DEIM framework, while efficient, struggle to preserve fine-grained spatial details and effectively suppress noise under such conditions, leading to degraded feature discriminability and frequent missed detections. To address these limitations, we propose LMF-Net, a lightweight multi-dimensional feature fusion network that enhances representation learning through three synergistic innovations. First, the Spatial-Edge Fusion module employs a dual-stage multi-path architecture to collaboratively extract local texture details and global contour features, preserving critical edge information that standard convolutions tend to smooth out. Second, the Frequency-Decomposed Hybrid Attention module decouples features into high- and low-frequency components and adaptively fuses them, effectively suppressing water ripple noise while enhancing feature discriminability. Third, the Adaptive Weighted Downsampling module replaces static pooling with content-aware feature reorganization, mitigating information loss during multi-scale pyramid construction. Experimental results on our self-built FloatDataset demonstrate that, compared to the baseline, LMF-Net reduces parameters by 1.2% and FLOPs by 0.7% while achieving significant improvements of 3.5% in AP50 and 6.5% in AP50:95. On the public VisDrone2019 dataset, it outperforms state-of-the-art real-time detectors such as YOLOv8 and RT-DETR by 3.4% and 2.7% in AP50:95, respectively, demonstrating exceptional cross-domain generalization capability and superior performance.

title: Polarimetric Analysis and Dielectric Constant Retrieval of Lunar Regolith Using Mini-RF Compact-Pol SAR Data
authors: Liting Liang, Yunhua Zhang, Dong Li, Xiao Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Characterizing the dielectric properties of lunar regolith is crucial for revealing surface composition, assessing in-situ resource potential, and deciphering geological evolution of the Moon. The availability of near-global, high-resolution synthetic aperture radar (SAR) observations from Mini-RF has stimulated growing interest in retrieving the real part of the dielectric constant (ε′r) from its S-band compact-polarimetric (CP) data. Existing methods, however, suffer from model-data inconsistencies, limited physical interpretability, and excessive prior assumptions, making accurate and reliable ε′r estimation remain a significant challenge. This paper analyzes the CP scattering characteristics of the lunar regolith and develops a new decomposition technique to extract its dominant scattering contribution. Then, a parameterized, physically interpretable full-polarimetric scattering-matrix representation is established, according to which the scattering mechanism of the regolith is elucidated through experimental analysis, enabling accurate ε′r estimation. Using datasets from the Apollo 16, Apollo 17, and Chang’E-4 missions, the method is comprehensively validated, demonstrating superior estimation accuracy and improved physical consistency compared with existing techniques. These results highlight the potential of the proposed method for advancing future lunar exploration applications.

title: Cryogenic-Refined MOSFET Modeling for Oscillator, Frequency Divider, and Amplifier Designs Below 4 K
authors: Nikhil Jain, Wei-Yu Lin, Yu-Hsuan Huang, Min-Jui Lin, Bozhi Yin, Vladimir Stojanović, Jiun-Yun Li, Jun-Chau Chien
abstract: Capturing device characteristic changes at cryogenic temperatures is crucial for cryo-CMOS circuit designs. In this work, we present an isothermal cryogenic-refined modeling approach for CMOS transistors that is simple, low overhead, and easy to implement while offering the required accuracy for predicting circuit performance at the designated temperatures. Guided by die-level measurement data and circuit design principles, the model introduces corrections to only five critical parameters: threshold voltage, carrier mobility, elevated low-frequency flicker noise, dominant high-frequency shot noise, and subthreshold swing (SS). These refinements are implemented around the foundry-provided SPICE model, which is typically validated only down to about 200 K. With these adjustments, the proposed cryogenic-refined model achieves less than 5% error in both large-signal metrics (I–V characteristics) and small-signal parameters (e.g., transconductance) when compared with device measurements at deep-cryogenic temperatures. The methodology is validated in two advanced technologies: TSMC 40-nm CMOS and GlobalFoundries (GF) 45-nm RF-SOI. We further demonstrate its applicability in three representative RF circuits: a 30-GHz LC oscillator, a high-speed current-mode-logic (CML) frequency divider (FD), and a subthreshold Gb/s amplifier, all showing close agreement between simulated predictions and measurements performed at 4 and 2.5 K. We believe that the proposed approach is implementation-friendly and can significantly accelerate the development of cryo-CMOS integrated circuits.

title: Digital and Hybrid Precoding and RF Chain Selection Designs for Energy-Efficient Multi-User MIMO-OFDM ISAC Systems
authors: Po-Chun Kang, Ming-Chun Lee, Tzu-Chien Chiu, Ting-Yao Kuo, Ta-Sung Lee
abstract: Using multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) with orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has attracted considerable attention in recent years. While most existing works focus on improving MIMO-OFDM ISAC performance, the impact of transmit power and radio-frequency (RF) circuit power consumption on energy efficiency (EE) remains relatively underexplored. To address this gap, this paper investigates joint precoding and RF chain selection for multi-user MIMO-OFDM ISAC systems, and develops energy-efficient designs for both fully digital and hybrid precoding architectures through the joint optimization of precoding and RF-chain activation. Specifically, we first formulate a novel EE maximization problem subject to sensing performance constraints. Then, efficient optimization algorithms are proposed for both architectures, together with analyses of their computational complexity and convergence behavior. Building on the proposed approaches, spectral efficiency-power consumption tradeoff designs are also provided. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with existing schemes, the proposed approaches achieve significant improvements in the EE-sensing tradeoff for ISAC systems.

title: Enhancement and Regulation of Mono-Mode Bandwidth in a Slab Folded SIW
authors: Ken Ning, Hao Liu, Yan-Rong Zhu, Lin-Fei Gao, Xiyao Liu
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this article, a slab folded substrate integrated waveguide (SFSIW) is proposed for mono-mode bandwidth (MMB) enhancement. The SFSIW can be regarded as a folded substrate-integrated waveguide (FSIW) loaded with an air-via array perforated dielectric slab, where the distinct field distributions of the first two modes lead to a wider MMB than that of the FSIW. The propagation characteristics of the SFSIW are investigated and quasi-static formulas for estimating the MMB are derived. Two prototypes for exciting the first two modes of the SFSIW are designed and fabricated. The measured MMB of the SFSIW is around 11GHz, representing an 18% improvement over that of the FSIW. Owing to its wide MMB, compact structure, and low cost, the proposed SFSIW is well suited for integrated microwave components operating in the X-/Ku-bands for satellite communication, radar systems, and other broadband microwave front-end applications.

title: Harnessing Vision-Language Pretrained Models with Temporal-Aware Adaptation for Referring Video Object Segmentation
authors: Zikun Zhou, Wentao Xiong, Li Zhou, Xin Li, Zhenyu He, Yaowei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Referring Video Object Segmentation (RVOS) is a task that involves segmenting target objects in a video based on the given referring expressions. It is critical for video editing and analysis. The crux of RVOS is to model dense text-video relations to associate abstract linguistic concepts with dynamic visual contents at pixel-level. Most RVOS methods typically use vision and language models pretrained independently as backbones, mapping images and texts to uncoupled feature spaces. As a result, they must learn Vision-Language (VL) relation modeling from scratch. Vision-Language Pretrained (VLP) models have achieved remarkable success. Inspired by this, we propose to explore relation modeling for RVOS based on their aligned VL feature space. Nevertheless, transferring VLP models to RVOS is deceptively challenging, due to the gap between static image/region-level pretraining and dynamic pixel-level prediction. To bridge this gap, we introduce a framework named VLP-RVOS, which harnesses VLP models for RVOS through temporal-aware adaptation. We first propose temporal-aware prompt-tuning to adapt pretrained representations for pixel-level prediction and empower the vision encoder to model temporal contexts. We further customize a cube-frame attention mechanism for robust spatial-temporal reasoning. Besides, we propose to perform multi-stage VL relation modeling while and after feature extraction for comprehensive understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VLP-RVOS performs favorably against state-of-the-art algorithms and generalizes well. Our codes are available at https://github.com/xwt909090/VLP-RVOS.

title: A Measurement-Calibrated Vegetation Propagation Model via Leaf-Wood Mesh Reconstruction and Ray Tracing Inversion
authors: Zeyu Cui, Guozhen Xia, Xinyu Liao, Chengkai Liu, Shiyang Jiang, Yuqiang Zhang, Jia Shi, Chen Zhou, Zan Li
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Accurate radio propagation modeling through vegetation is challenged by highly irregular three-dimensional tree geometry and uncertain electromagnetic parameters, especially when scattering is non-negligible. This letter presents a measurement-calibrated vegetation propagation framework that integrates LiDAR-based tree mesh reconstruction with ray tracing (RT)-based parameter inversion. A handheld LiDAR scan is processed to separate woody components and leaf components, followed by leaf-cluster meshing and woody mesh construction to obtain a high-fidelity tree model. Deterministic RT serves as the forward simulator, while particle swarm optimization (PSO) calibrates a set of effective vegetation parameters, including relative permittivity, conductivity, thickness, scattering coefficient, and scattering-lobe width, by minimizing the mismatch between simulations and a small set of field measurements. Experiments at 2.4 GHz on a real tree show that calibration substantially improves RT accuracy across multiple receiver locations, reducing the received-power RMSE from 4.066 dB to 1.342 dB and the MAE from 3.157 dB to 1.097 dB, and consistently outperforming ITU-R, COST235, and Weissberger baselines.

title: A Multidimensional Assessment of Embodied Interaction in Large-Scale Full-Body Interactive Environments_supp1-3682920.pdf
authors: Sena Beste Ercan
abstract: As Mixed Reality (MR) systems evolve toward richer, full-body interactive experiences, understanding how spatial configurations shape user behavior becomes essential for designing usable, engaging, and cognitively coherent environments. This paper presents the design and evaluation of three MR mini-games developed to investigate key embodied interaction properties: (1) territoriality and social distance, (2) proprioception and kinaesthesia, and (3) navigation and wayfinding. Implemented across four differently scaled MR footprints, the games were tested with participants from three age groups, children, adolescents, and young adults, to assess how interaction space size influences bodily coordination, spatial negotiation, and social behavior. Using a multimodal analysis approach, we analyze behavioral patterns that emerge at different scales, revealing how users' interaction strategies change based on space. Our findings contribute to the human-centered design of MR systems by offering a behavioral lens on how scale influences user experience, embodiment, and interaction quality. The study informs future applications in education, social interaction, and rehabilitation, where adaptive MR design is critical for inclusive and effective engagement.

title: Characterizing Block Acknowledgment Window Reordering in Multi-Link Wi-Fi
authors: Rafael Oliveira, Duarte Raposo, Miguel Luís
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Wi-Fi has become a vital wireless technology across many sectors that require networks capable of handling reliable high-speed transmissions. The IEEE 802.11be standard, also known as Wi-Fi 7, further improves network performance by introducing Multi-Link Operation (MLO) with support for wireless link aggregation in the 2.4, 5 and 6 GHz. However, this feature increases the likelihood of out-of-order delivery, as Block Acknowledgment (BA) windows can experience stalls that, under burst loss, are associated with delay spikes at the application level. Because the MLO feature is relatively recent, this paper provides a detailed analysis of out-of-order delivery costs when switching from Single-Link Operation (SLO) to Simultaneous Transmit and Receive (STR) MLO with Wi-Fi 7. The tests are conducted in NS-3 with Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) and User Datagram Protocol (UDP) traffic in burst loss scenarios and with different network features, such as a larger BA window and Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access (OFDMA). The results show that STR MLO increases out-of-order delivery, leading to longer waiting times for frames inside the BA buffer at the receiver, with UDP traffic being more susceptible to these delays compared to TCP. However, MLO also offers greater flexibility in channel access, allowing for faster window repairs than SLO by steering retransmissions onto cleaner links. Nevertheless, the observations suggest that current BA coordination may be insufficient for asynchronous multi-link transmissions under burst loss, motivating further study of window repair policies for lossy multi-link Wi-Fi networks.

title: News from Japan: TEPCO Power Grid Builds Japan's First Extra-High-Voltage Digital Substation
authors: Yoshimichi Ohki
abstract: In Japan, overall electricity demand had remained almost constant, or even dropped slightly, for a long time until 2023, thanks to the progress of energy conservation measures. However, this situation is about to change drastically. This is because the progress in green transformation (GX) and digital transformation (DX) is driving an increase in large-scale electricity demand for data centers (DC), semiconductor factories, and the decarbonization of factories, including conversion to electric furnaces for steel production.

title: PACA: 3D Physical Adversarial Camouflage for Aircraft in Remote Sensing under Multi-View and Varying Weather Conditions
authors: Jinpeng Luo, Gang Liu, Min Dang, Zhaolu Zheng, Xulong Zhao, Nan Luo
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Most existing physical adversarial attack methods for remote sensing object detectors are optimized from a single nadir viewpoint, leading to significant performance degradation under multi-view observations. Moreover, most methods directly overlay adversarial textures onto images during digital training without adequately modeling the color distribution of complex environmental backgrounds, causing severe color inconsistency between adversarial textures and their surroundings. This reduces visual realism, training stability, and environmental adapt ability. To address these limitations, this paper proposes Physical full-coverage Adversarial Camouflage for Aircraft (PACA), a framework designed to achieve robust deception across multiple viewpoints and complex environmental conditions. PACA integrates differentiable neural rendering with an environment-aware texture optimization mechanism: a neural rendering module (NeuroCam) enables precise texture mapping and multi-view projection on 3D aircraft surfaces, while an environment-aware module (FIRNet) extracts and fuses environmental color features to generate visually consistent and physically realizable full coverage adversarial textures. Extensive experiments on the high fidelity simulated remote sensing dataset (RS-AirSim) and real world physical prototype tests demonstrate that the proposed method effectively degrades detection performance under multi view observations. Compared with traditional remote sensing camouflage strategies, PACA reduces the detection AP from 0.30–0.60 to below 0.02 in most white-box settings, and maintains strong attack effectiveness in real-world physical deployments, while remaining robust to viewpoint variations and complex weather conditions. Source code, dataset, and 3D simulation environment will be publicly available at our GitHub repository.

title: IEEE Standard for Geometry Point Cloud Compression
authors: N/A
abstract: A set of tools for efficient compression of geometry point clouds, along with the corresponding definitions of syntax, semantics, parsing, and decoding processes, including but not limited to prediction, transform, quantization, and entropy coding, is defined in this standard. This standard provides efficient coding tool sets for compression, decompression, processing, and packaging of point cloud data, which can significantly reduce the disk storage and communication bandwidth. The target applications and services include but are not limited to manufacturing, mapping, e-commerce, e-tourism, digital culture heritage.

title: Bio-Inspired Flower Robot for Emotional Human-Robot Interaction
authors: Qingyue Liu, Guojie Xu, Canrui Yang, Xunsheng Du, Igarashi Go, Hongqiang Wang
abstract: Mental health problems are common worldwide, but the available support systems and healthcare services are insufficient and inadequate. Social robots offer a promising solution to achieve social interaction and emotional connection. Nature-inspired architectural robotics can seamlessly blend into human daily life by reshaping future living environments. In this work, inspired by natural flowers, we designed a flower robot with a realistic morphology that simulates the opening-closing, rhythmic movements, and stem-bending movement. Building on the morphological design, we integrated a flex sensor into the petals to enable touch-induced tactile feedback, mimicking the touch-induced plant-like seismonastic movement. Furthermore, we incorporated voice interaction powered by a local large language model (LLM) to achieve adaptive intent understanding and personalized semantic engagement, thereby enhancing interaction flexibility compared to the repetitive tasks and rigid protocols of traditional social robots. Finally, the human-robot interaction (HRI) experiment demonstrated that the flower robot significantly reduced participants' negative emotions and provided positive social engagement. This work developed a bio-inspired flower robot, offering a promising pathway to social interaction and soothing presence.

title: FI-StackNet: An Optimized Feature-Interaction Stacking Network for Advanced Fraud Detection
authors: Mostafa Gamal, Hanin Ardah, Mohammed Aly
abstract: Credit card fraud detection remains a challenging problem due to extreme class imbalance, anonymized feature representations, and the absence of reliable sequential dependencies in widely used benchmark datasets. Although deep learning architectures have been extensively explored, their structural assumptions are often misaligned with non-sequential, PCA-transformed tabular data. To address these limitations, this paper proposes FI-StackNet, an interaction-aware stacking framework specifically designed for highly imbalanced tabular environments. The proposed method integrates heterogeneous base learners, including Random Forest, XGBoost, CatBoost, and Logistic Regression, and constructs a decision-level representation from their probabilistic outputs. Unlike conventional stacking approaches that rely on implicit or linear aggregation, FI-StackNet explicitly models pairwise interactions between base learner predictions, enabling the capture of complementary agreement, disagreement, and confidence patterns. This interaction-enhanced representation is then processed using a lightweight neural meta-learner, achieving a balance between expressive modeling and computational efficiency. Extensive experimental evaluation using stratified 5-fold cross-validation demonstrates that FI-StackNet achieves a superior F1-score of 86.63% with low variability (±0.35), outperforming strong ensemble baselines such as CatBoost (85.27% ± 0.45) and advanced deep learning models including CNN-BiLSTM (83.96% ± 1.78). Additional benchmarking against multiple deep learning architectures further confirms that FI-StackNet provides a more balanced and stable performance across evaluation metrics, particularly in highly imbalanced settings. Scalability analysis shows strong data efficiency, achieving competitive performance with limited training data and converging to optimal performance as data increases. Moreover, cost-sensitive evaluation on real transaction data indicates a 21.7% reduction in estimated financial loss compared to CatBoost, primarily due to improved control of false negatives. The framework also maintains low inference latency (4.8 ms per transaction), supporting real-time deployment. These results demonstrate that interaction-aware decision-level stacking represents a principled and effective advancement over conventional ensemble and deep learning approaches, offering a robust, stable, and cost-efficient solution for fraud detection in non-sequential tabular data.

title: A Control Architecture for Energy Management and Battery Integration in a Stand-Alone Heaving Wave Energy Converter
authors: Abdin Yousif Elamin, Tuanku Badzlin Hashfi, Omsalama M. Mohamed, Addy Wahyudie, Adha Imam Cahyadi, Mohamed Abdi Jama, Mohammad Shakeel Laghari, Ruhul Amin Khalil, Saad Mekhilef, Ahmed Abdrabou
abstract: This paper presents an integrated control framework for a stand-alone wave energy converter (WEC) system equipped with battery storage and load-side regulation. The core of the proposed system is a supervisory energy management system (EMS) that adaptively governs the operation by transitioning between reactive control, damping control, and power-shedding modes in response to wave conditions and the battery’s state of charge (SoC). To ensure efficient energy harvesting, a variable step-size maximum power point tracking (MPPT) algorithm is employed to dynamically tune the damping and stiffness coefficients of the power take-off (PTO) mechanism. The EMS operates alongside a battery-side controller responsible for regulating the DC bus voltage through controlled charging and discharging, thereby ensuring voltage stability under fluctuating sea states and load variations. A load-side controller guarantees balanced three-phase AC power delivery, maintaining sinusoidal voltage and current profiles during dynamic load conditions. The proposed control system is validated through detailed MATLAB/Simulink simulations and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) experiments. The findings demonstrates the EMS’s ability to manage power flow efficiently and maintaining safe SoC levels under high-energy conditions, while performing load disconnection during low-energy periods to prevent battery depletion. Both simulation and experimental outcomes demonstrate reliable voltage regulation, coordinated control mode switching, and effective system response to variable sea states and load demands.

title: Planar Transformer with Integrated Direct Liquid Cooling
authors: Fabian Groon, Julius Maximilian Placzek, Hamzeh Beiranvand, Marco Liserre
abstract: Planar transformers (PTs) are becoming increasingly popular due to the growing demand for compact medium frequency transformers in electric vehicles (EVs) and data centres. Reducing the size of such converters by increasing the power density of the PT remains the most significant challenge. In particular, thermal management of the internal printed circuit board (PCB) layers, which are difficult to access for cooling, poses the primary limitation. Therefore, this paper proposes three integrated direct liquid cooling methods for the PT layer stack. The first uses an additional hollow copper conductor as a cooling channel and the second uses a plastic spacer with a channel for coolant guidance. In these designs, the liquid flows inside the hollow copper conductor and, for the plastic spacer, comes into direct contact with the winding layers. Due to the nature of the copper conductor, additional losses will be induced. As current models cannot correctly predict losses in rectangular hollow conductors, a novel closed form solution has been developed to evaluate additional losses for efficiency and optimal placement. Additionally, the conductor thickness and channel height are optimized to achieve the best thermal performance. Then, a 1D thermal network is developed to estimate the possible temperature reduction of the proposed solutions. The proposed solutions are then compared, and the use of a hollow conductor as an active turn with cooling for high current capability is introduced as a third solution to reduce losses and the problem of current sharing in high current systems with many parallel layers. An experimental setup was built to validate the proposed cooling solutions and a temperature reduction of 54 % was achieved.

title: DHCNet: Dynamic Hierarchical Collaborative Learning for Change Detection in High-Resolution Remote Sensing Images
authors: Weilong Chen, Qing Ding, Fengyan Wang, Mingchang Wang, Kaiyuan Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: High-resolution remote sensing (HRRS) image change detection (CD) can effectively monitor dynamic changes of the earth’s surface. In recent years, deep learning-based RSCD methods have developed rapidly. However, they still have shortcomings in spatio-temporal feature interaction of images, multi-scale feature enhancement, and multi-level information fusion. Therefore, this paper proposes a dynamic hierarchical collaborative learning method (DHCNet) for accurate remote sensing image CD (RSCD). First, a dynamic hierarchical collaborative interaction module is designed. It improves the richness of bi-temporal multi-granularity features through layer-differentiated feature exchange and cross-temporal collaborative attention mechanism. Second, a multi-scale feature enhancement module is constructed to enhance DHCNet’s ability to represent the texture and semantic information of changed ground objects. Furthermore, a multi-level feature reconstruction module is proposed. It deeply fuses the enhanced multi-level features to reconstruct key change information and improve the model’s CD performance. Experimental results on the LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, CDD, Real-Wuhan, and S1GFloods datasets show that DHCNet’s CD outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, with F1 scores reaching 91.55%, 95.73%, 96.40%, 75.63%, and 96.36%, respectively. Comparative and ablation experiments confirm that DHCNet has significant advantages in CD of complex scenarios and fine-grained boundary representation. Our source codes are available at https://github.com/WBJIIKe/DHCNet.

title: A New NB-LDPC Code Construction Method with Arbitrary Generalized Girth
authors: Jaeil Lim, Jaewon Chung, Donghun Jeong, Daegeun Jee, Euicheol Lim
abstract: !Non-binary low density parity check (NB-LDPC) codes are known to have higher correction capability than their binary counterparts, low density parity check (LDPC) codes. The latest technique for analyzing NB-LDPC codes is to measure generalized girth. The generalized girth cannot be measured in a binary LDPC code, and can be considered an indicator for measuring the performance of NB-LDPC code. In this paper, we propose a new analysis method to represent NB-LDPC code as a graph. This graph is called a permutation graph. In this paper, we present and prove a reasoning for analyzing generalized girth using the permutation graph. This is a faster and simpler method than the existing generalized girth analysis method. The proposed code construction algorithm constructs NB-LDPC codes to have an arbitrary generalized girth length. Because the permutation graph-based analysis is applied, which is more effective than existing methods, the complexity of code construction is significantly reduced. This enables more efficient code construction. The decoding simulations show that the proposed method improves the correction performance of NB-LDPC codes compared to the existing state-of-the-art.

title: Spectral and Energy Efficiency Tradeoff for Pinching-Antenna Systems
authors: Zihao Zhou, Zhaolin Wang, Yuanwei Liu
abstract: The joint transmit and pinching beamforming design for spectral efficiency (SE) and energy efficiency (EE) tradeoff in pinching-antenna systems (PASS) is proposed, under practical channel and energy consumption models. In the single-user scenario, it is proved that the optimal pinching antenna (PA) positions are independent of the transmit beamforming. Based on this insight, a two-stage joint beamforming design is proposed. Specifically, in the first stage, a general PA placement framework is proposed for multi-waveguide systems. In the second stage, the closed-form solution for the optimal transmit beamformer is derived given the optimized PA positions. In the multi-user scenario, an alternating optimization (AO)-based joint beamforming design is proposed to balance the SE-EE performance while taking the quality-of-service (QoS) requirements into account. It is proved that the proposed AO-based algorithm is guaranteed to converge when no constraints are violated in PA placement subproblem. Numerical results demonstrate that: 1) the proposed algorithms effectively improve joint SE-EE performance; 2) PASS exhibits strong robustness against variations in the service area along the waveguide direction.

title: Hybrid Filters for High-Current Power Supplies Using Deadbeat and Repetitive Control
authors: Mohammad Wasiq, Cristina Terlizzi, Stefano Bifaretti, Alessandro Lampasi
abstract: The paper examines the challenges of supplying high current coils in magnetically confined fusion generators. Such systems use power electronic converters traditionally supplied by grid-connected thyristor-based interfaces. Due to their Megawatt-scale pulsed-power operations, a decoupling storage system is often used. Recently, supercapacitor-based storage systems emerged as a promising option. However, the harmonic distortions produced by the switching operation of power electronic converters require the use of passive filters. Due to the large currents (tens of kA) and low switching frequencies (around 1 kHz), their size and volume become a significant challenge. To reduce filter size, the paper proposes the inclusion of active filters on the DC supply side of high-current converters. Unlike conventional line-frequency applications, active filters must track higher frequency harmonics, thus a fast and accurate control system is needed. To this aim, a control approach combining deadbeat and repetitive algorithms, implemented on an industrial-grade microcontroller, is presented and validated through real time hardware in the loop tests. A volume and cost comparison with the traditional solution confirms the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: An Improved Swin Transformer-Based Method for Electric Spark Detection in Distribution Network Lines
authors: Yanneng Duan, Dingfan He, Zhengyu He, Xuexiang Zou, Dan Yue
abstract: Spark detection in distribution line videos remains challenging due to weak visual saliency, multi-scale variation, and complex environmental interference. To address these issues, this paper presents an enhanced Swin Transformer architecture for video-based recognition of electric sparks. The proposed model incorporates a Batch-Channel Normalization Transformer module to enhance sensitivity to small-scale targets while maintaining network stability. In addition, a Hybrid-Scale Multi-level Feature Pyramid module is introduced to strengthen multi-scale semantic representation through cross-layer feature fusion and atrous convolution. The resulting model achieves a favorable balance between detection accuracy and computational efficiency. Experimental results on a real-world transmission line dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms the original Swin-T baseline by 2.2% in accuracy.

title: BO-CLAHE-Based Contrast Optimization for Tuberculosis Classification Using EfficientNet Variants and Their Ensembles
authors: Syeda Meraj, Asadullah Shah, Ahsiah Ismail, Tengku MT Sembok, Syed Aftab
abstract: Tuberculosis (TB) detection from chest X-ray (CXR) images is widely used for large-scale screening; however, variations in image contrast across datasets introduce domain shift that degrades the reliability and generalizability of deep learning models. Existing approaches typically rely on fixed or manually tuned preprocessing pipelines, which fail to adapt to heterogeneous imaging conditions. To address this limitation, this study proposes a Bayesian Optimization–driven Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization framework (BO-CLAHE) that automatically learns dataset-specific contrast enhancement parameters. The optimized preprocessing is integrated with EfficientNet variants (B0–B4) and multiple ensemble strategies, including soft voting, hard voting, weighted voting, and stacked generalization. Experiments are conducted on three benchmark datasets: Montgomery County (MC), Shenzhen (SH), and a combined (MC&SH) dataset, using a stratified 70:15:15 training, validation, and testing protocol with class balancing applied to the training set. Results show that BO-CLAHE enhances feature discriminability and stabilizes model performance across datasetswith varying characteristics.While smaller datasets such asMC exhibit greater sensitivity variability, larger and more diverse datasets such as SH achieve consistently high performance, with EfficientNet-B3 reaching an AUC of 0.962. On the combined dataset, which introduces cross-domain heterogeneity, higher-capacity models such as EfficientNet-B4 demonstrate improved robustness (AUC = 0.933). Ensemble learning further improves classification stability, with stacked ensemble achieving the best overall performance, reaching up to 90% accuracy and AUC values of approximately 0.94–0.965 across datasets. These findings demonstrate that dataset-adaptive contrast optimization is critical for mitigating domain shift in CXR analysis. The proposed BO-CLAHE framework, combined with deep ensembles, provides a robust and scalable solution for consistent TB detection across heterogeneous imaging environments.

title: Erratum to “magnetodynamic H-$\phi$ Formulation for Improving the Convergence and Speed of Numerical Simulations of Superconducting Materials”
authors: Alexandre Arsenault, Bruno de Sousa Alves, Gregory Giard, Frédéric Sirois
abstract: This short erratum is a slight correction to the paper entitled “Magnetodynamic H-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\phi$</tex-math></inline-formula> formulation for improving the convergence and speed of numerical simulations of superconducting materials”, published in IEEE Transactions on applied superconductivity. Herein, the strong form implemented in the air is amended in order to have a more accurate description of the physics.

title: Autoregressive Stochastic Clock Jitter Compensation in Analog-to-Digital Converters
authors: Daniele Gerosa, Rui Hou, Vimar Björk, Ulf Gustavsson, Thomas Eriksson
abstract:
This paper addresses the mathematical modeling and compensation of stochastic discrete-time clock jitter in analog-to-digital converters (ADCs). We model the stochastic clock jitter as a first-order autoregressive (AR(1)) process, and we propose two novel, computationally efficient, pilot-assisted dejittering algorithms for baseband signals: one based on solving a sequence of weighted least-squares problems, and another that exploits the correlated jitter structure via a Kalman filterbased routine.We also propose a conditional maximum-likelihood estimator for the autoregressive parameters, enabling nearoptimal Kalman-filter performance even when such parameters vary over time. We further provide a mathematical analysis of the induced linearization errors, and we complement the theory with synthetic simulations to evaluate the proposed techniques across different scenarios.

The proposed techniques are shown to yield a 1 – 15 dB improvement in signal-to-noise-and-distortion ratio (SINADR) and 0.02 – 1.6 dB in symbol error vector magnitude (EVM), depending on impairment severity and pilot density. The Kalman smoother generally provides superior performance by leveraging additional temporal information.

title: LSPC-LA: Local Structure Preserving Clustering with Learnable Anchors
authors: Haonan Xin, Haoming Chen, Zhezheng Hao, Danyang Wu, Rong Wang, Feiping Nie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: K-means algorithm divides samples into c classes based on their structural characteristics. However, due to the non convex nature of the clustering problem, algorithms are prone to converge to poor local minima. To address the aforementioned issues, we propose the Local Structure Preserving Clustering with Learnable Anchors (LSPC-LA) method. We assume that with a well-designed anchor selection strategy, samples near the same anchor tend to belong to the same cluster, which reveal high confidence Must-Link local structural information for clustering. Based on this observation, we first construct an anchor-based bipartite graph, transforming the sample clustering problem into anchor clustering problem by local structural information, thus reducing the solution space and minimizing the risk of poor local minima. Then we create an anchor guiding matrix to allow anchors to learn the sample structure, improving clustering performance. Subsequently, an alternating iterative algorithm is proposed to optimize the LSPC-LA model. Finally, extensive experiments demonstrate the accuracy of the local structural information and the effectiveness of LSPC-LA.
