title: Power Consumption and Energy Efficiency of Mid-Band XL-MIMO: Modeling, Scaling Laws, and Performance Insights
authors: Jiachen Tian, Yu Han, Xiao Li, Shi Jin, Chao-Kai Wen
abstract: Mid-band extra-large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (XL-MIMO), emerging as a critical enabler for future communication systems, is expected to deliver significantly higher throughput by leveraging the extended bandwidth and enlarged antenna aperture. However, power consumption remains a significant concern due to the expanded system dimension, underscoring the need for thorough investigations into efficient system design and deployment. To this end, an in-depth study is conducted on mid-band XL-MIMO systems. Specifically, a comprehensive power consumption model is proposed, encompassing the power consumption of major hardware components and signal processing procedures, while capturing the influence of key system parameters. Considering typical near-field propagation characteristics, closed-form approximations of throughput are derived, providing an analytical framework for assessing energy efficiency (EE). Based on the proposed framework, the scaling law of EE with respect to key system configurations is derived, offering valuable insights for system design. Subsequently, extensions and comparisons are conducted among representative multi-antenna technologies, demonstrating the superiority of mid-band XL-MIMO in EE. Extensive numerical results not only verify the tightness of the throughput analysis but also validate the EE evaluations, unveiling the potential of energy-efficient mid-band XL-MIMO systems.

title: Learning Physics-Aware Sensorimotor Model With Visual–Tactile Sensing for DLO Manipulation_supp2-3678745.pdf
authors: Kun Qian
abstract: Robotic manipulation of deformable linear objects (DLOs)—including electrical cables, wires, and hoses—is prevalent in industrial applications. Achieving precise shape control in these tasks hinges on learning a reliable sensorimotor model that maps robot motion to resulting deformations. However, developing such models remains challenging, especially for DLOs with unknown or variable physical properties. To address this issue, we propose a physics-aware deformable linear object shaping (PA-DLOS) framework, which facilitates accurate shape control across a range of unseen DLOs. Specifically, the proposed neural network-based global DLO deformation model, trained in simulation, is conditioned on physics embeddings estimated in real time through a multimodal model that integrates visual, tactile, and proprioceptive inputs. On this basis, precise shape servoing of unseen DLOs can be achieved via a dual-stage online optimization strategy, which modulates the simulation-trained deformation model according to online visual and tactile feedback. Results from extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that PA-DLOS outperforms representative methods in both DLO deformation modeling and shape control. Notably, despite being trained solely in simulation, our framework achieves a 100% success rate, the highest tracking accuracy, and the fastest convergence in real-world shape servoing tasks.

title: Retrieval of Ocean Surface and Atmospheric Parameters from K- and Ka-Band Data Using a Physics-Informed Coupled Neural Network
authors: Peng Mao, Xiaobin Yin, Ning Wang, Yan Li, Qing Xu, Qingtao Song, Xingwei Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Sea surface temperature (SST), sea surface wind speed (SSW), and total column water vapor (WV) are critical parameters for understanding ocean-atmosphere interactions. While conventional microwave retrieval methods typically rely on C- and X-band channels, this study proposes a Physics-Informed Coupled Neural Network (PIC-NN) designed to jointly retrieve all three parameters using only K- and Ka-band brightness temperatures. The model incorporates two key components: bidirectional coupling connections, which explicitly encode known physical interactions among SST, SSW, and WV, and an adaptive scaling factor loss function that balances the multi-task learning process. Validated against ERA5 reanalysis data and multiple in-situ datasets, PIC-NN achieves SST retrieval accuracy comparable to that of the operational AMSR-2 product (which uses lower frequencies). Moreover, it significantly outperforms AMSR-2 in retrieving SSW and WV, achieving STD reductions of 19.0% and 23.7%, respectively. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each model component, and comparisons with traditional methods highlight the importance of modeling nonlinearity and physical coupling. Further validated on an in-orbit high-frequency payload, this work demonstrates the robust generalization of multi-parameter retrievals using high-frequency bands alone. Under potential constraints of low-frequency spectrum scarcity or specific payload designs restricted to high frequencies, this approach provides a viable solution for accurately extracting key ocean-atmosphere parameters.

title: An All-Fiber MMI Based Transmitter Head for Performance Improvement over Turbulent FSO-FC Channels
authors: Champalal Lalani, Aashish Mathur, Nitin Bhatia
abstract: This letter presents performance improvement in a free-space optical-fiber converged (FSO-FC) communication system under varying turbulence conditions using an all-fiber multimode interference head connected to a single mode fiber. The transmitter head is used for the generation of fundamental (LG00 )-alike and first higher order Laguerre-Gaussian (LG01 )-alike optical beams in free-space. The FSO channel is statistically characterized under different levels of turbulence, in terms of the probability density functions, for both the beams. We show that the transmitter head generating LG01-alike optical beam shows average bit error rate improvement as compared to LG00-alike beam under similar operating conditions. This study demonstrates the feasibility and robustness of fiber based transmitter heads for performance enhancement of the FSO-FC communication systems under varying turbulence conditions.

title: DSSM: Dynamic Streaming Pipeline with Spatiotemporal Model toward Remote Sensing Object Detection
authors: Shilong Jing, Yuchen Zhao, Zhuoxian Li, Xiangzhi Li, Xianda Xu, Hechong Wang, Hengyi Lv, Ming Sun, Yisa Zhang, Yang Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In the domain of visual perception tasks for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) remote sensing, conventional object detection algorithms suffer severe challenges arise from drastic variations in perspective and complex backgrounds, proving particularly problematic for the identification of occluded and fast-motion targets. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel dynamic streaming architecture that encompasses the following three solution approaches: 1) We have established a revolutionary Dual-Stage Streaming data pipeline (e.g., DSS Dataloader, DSS Sampler, and DSS Transform) to perceive target locations with more enriched spatiotemporal states, leveraging spatial pipeline to expedite rapid convergence on static representations, and applying temporal pipeline to facilitate fine-grained refinement by inject dynamic spatiotemporal details; 2) We have devised a novel selective memory module (SMM) that utilizes decoupled gating units to adaptively encode and store critical spatiotemporal features pertinent to the target from historical frame sequences, while furnishing abundant contextual priors and endows the model with the capacity to capture long-range temporal dependencies; 3) We have proposed a spatiotemporal loss function designed to constrain and guide the streaming model through state smoothing constraint, motion response constraint, and gating conflict penalty, thereby improving the localization performance of the model. Moreover, we introduce a comprehensively revised version of the VisDrone-VID dataset prompted by the identification of numerous instances of disorganized bounding box annotations and erroneous labels within the original compilation. Finally, extensive experiments demonstrate that the Dynamic Streaming Pipeline with Spatiotemporal Model (DSSM) outperforms all other state-of-the-art detection methods, achieving 14.4%, 77.6% and 58.4% mAP@0.5:0.95 on the VisDrone-VID, EMRS-Frame and EMRS-Event dataset. The updated VisDrone-VID dataset and our code are realeased at https://github.com/Jushl/DSSM.

title: A Wearable Shoulder Skin-Deformation Device Generating Illusory Forces for Posture Correction
authors: Kohei Okuda, Takuto Nakamura, Hideaki Kuzuoka, Kohei Yamakoshi, Yusuke Suzuki
abstract: The hanger reflex, in which skin shear deformation elicits a directional force sensation, provides a basis for wearable haptic guidance on the shoulders. In this study, we employ a wearable haptic device that uses four pneumatic airbags to apply controlled skin-shear deformation to both shoulders. Previous work on the hanger reflex has demonstrated that shear deformation of the skin can elicit directional force sensations, and such illusory forces are reliably perceived at the shoulder. However, the strength of these perceived forces and their potential influence on upper-body posture have not yet been quantitatively examined. To enable future applications in posture correction during manual or visually demanding tasks, the present study investigates both the perceived force magnitude and the postural effects elicited by shoulder skin-shear deformation. In Experiment 1, we quantified the magnitude of the illusory forces using a psychophysical comparison between forces elicited by skin-shear deformation and real traction forces. The device generated substantially larger perceived forces than typical illusion-based haptic methods, and the relationship between pressure and perceived force was well approximated by a linear model within the tested range. In Experiment 2, we evaluated how these deformation-elicited forces affect upper-body posture. We measured forward-backward trunk inclination under varying initial postures and stimulation directions. Consistent with the perceptual findings, the device produced consistent posture adjustments, particularly in the backward direction, with significant effects of initial posture and stimulus intensity on angular changes. Together, these findings demonstrate that shoulder hanger-reflex device provides quantifiable force sensations while also inducing measurable posture modification. The results offer essential information for individualized calibration and control-parameter design in wearable posture-support and rehabilitation systems, and they provide a foundation for future integration into ergonomic and assistive applications.

title: Embedded Artificial Intelligence for Predictive Maintenance of Jet Engines A Hardware-Aware Deployment on FPGA-Based Edge Platforms
authors: Giorgio Ubbriaco, Stefania Perri
abstract: The advancements of artificial intelligence and embedded platforms have enabled the development of cutting-edge solutions for predictive maintenance in the aerospace sector. Nowadays, failure data for the mechanical components of aircraft engines are rather limited. Specifically, the information preceding failures is lacking, as it is difficult and expensive to acquire. To overcome this difficulty and facilitate the development of methodologies based on data-driven approaches for detecting temporal patterns, tools have been developed to simulate the temporal evolution of these components until their failure. This paper presents a new methodology to enhance the throughput inference of prediction models for estimating the Remaining Useful Life of jet engines using a set of simulated data. The proposed models match or outperform most comparison algorithms, demonstrating significant results despite their simpler design and implementation. Subsequently, the models are quantized and compiled for execution on a Deep-learning Processing Unit. While these optimization and deployment steps introduce a slight loss in accuracy, they enable low energy consumption by leveraging the efficiency of the hardware platform. These improvements are crucial for critical embedded systems, such as those required in aerospace applications.

title: Seamless Bridging Between Secret Shares and HE Ciphertexts for IoT Data Processing
authors: Yufei Zhou, Peijia Zheng
abstract: The rise of IoT devices in daily life has led to massive amounts of sensitive data. Efficient and secure storage and processing of this data is key to unlocking IoT’s potential. Although homomorphic encryption (HE) ensures privacy and supports efficient and complex private data processing algorithms, its high computational and communication costs during encryption pose challenges for resource-limited IoT devices. We propose a novel framework to reduce the encryption and upload burden on IoT devices by enabling seamless conversion from lightweight secret shares to HE ciphertexts under the semi-honest model. Our design introduces a new secret sharing scheme and leverages efficient pseudorandom functions (PRFs) combined with private-key HE ciphertexts to improve reconstruction efficiency. After computation, results can be converted back into secret shares, supporting bidirectional transformation between HE ciphertexts and secret shares. Unlike existing approaches, our method eliminates reliance on specific HE parameters, allowing better integration with current HE-based privacy-preserving protocols. It also offers a more suitable solution for long-term storage of sensitive data, such as health records and images.

title: ObliviGate: Towards Architecture-Oblivious Privacy-Preserving Inference for Malicious Security
authors: Nianyun Song, Fuyi Wang, Rongfang Bie, Yu Guo, Leo Yu Zhang, Xiaohua Jia
abstract: Neural network inference is increasingly outsourced to cloud platforms, offering scalable computation with minimal local overhead. To mitigate privacy risks, secure multi-party computation (MPC) is commonly used to protect model parameters and user inputs. However, most MPC-based approaches assume that model architectures are publicly known, threatening intellectual property and facilitating various privacy attacks. In this work, we present ObliviGate, a novel scheme for architecture-oblivious, privacy-preserving inference built on a fully outsourced two-party computation (2PC) setting. ObliviGate introduces a principled two-level obfuscation mechanism: intra-layer obfuscation injects dummy neurons to conceal layer configurations, while inter-layer obfuscation inserts dummy edges with a secure gating mechanism to hide network topology. We further leverage differential privacy (DP) to guide dummy operation allocation, providing formal privacy guarantees while maintaining model functionality with negligible accuracy degradation. To ensure robustness against malicious cloud providers, we introduce an optimized malicious-secure inference protocol based on function secret sharing (FSS) with preprocessing, achieving strong verifiability with low online latency. Extensive theoretical analysis and experiments across multiple neural network architectures show that ObliviGate delivers <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.1\times \sim 14.8\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> speedups over prior studies, making it a practical, scalable solution for real-world outsourced inference.

title: Polarization-Aware DoA Detection Relying on a Single Rydberg Atomic Receiver
authors: Yuanbin Chen, Chau Yuen, Darmindra Arumugam, Chong Meng Samson See, Mérouane Debbah, Lajos Hanzo
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: A polarization-aware direction-of-arrival (DoA) detection scheme is conceived that leverages the intrinsic vector sensitivity of a single Rydberg atomic vapor cell to achieve quantum-enhanced angle resolution. Our core idea lies in the fact that the vector nature of an electromagnetic wave is uniquely determined by its orthogonal electric and magnetic field components, both of which can be retrieved by a single Rydberg atomic receiver via electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT)- based spectroscopy. To be specific, in the presence of a static magnetic bias field that defines a stable quantization axis, a pair of sequential EIT measurements is carried out in the same vapor cell. Firstly, the electric-field polarization angle is extracted from the Zeeman-resolved EIT spectrum associated with an electricdipole transition driven by the radio frequency (RF) field. Within the same experimental cycle, the RF field is then retuned to a magnetic-dipole resonance, producing Zeeman-resolved EIT peaks for decoding the RF magnetic-field orientation. This scheme exhibits a dual yet independent sensitivity on both angles, allowing for precise DoA reconstruction without the need for spatial diversity or phase referencing. Building on this foundation, we derive the quantum Fisher-information matrix (QFIM) and obtain a closed-form quantum Cramér-Rao bound (QCRB) for the joint estimation of polarization and orientation angles. Finally, simulation results spanning various quantum parameters validate the proposed approach and identify optimal operating regimes. With appropriately chosen polarization and magnetic-field geometries, a single vapor cell is expected to achieve sub-0.1° angle resolution at moderate RF-field driving strengths.

title: An Adaptive interval-constrained Tucker Decomposi-tion for Efficient Multilevel Fast Multipole Algorithm
authors: Haohui Ge, Yang Liu, Sheng Sun, Hanyu Li, Haijing Zhou
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: To enhance far-field tensor storage efficiency and computational performance in the multilevel fast multipole algorithm (MLFMA), we propose (i) an adaptive interval-constrained Tucker decomposition that reformulates rank selection under an error interval ϵ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">lower</sub> [ϵ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">lower</sub>, ϵ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">lower</sub>] to avoid overly conservative truncation and eliminate the manual tolerance adjustments, and (ii) a slice precomputation strategy that converts runtime Tucker contractions into matrix–vector products without repeated reshaping overhead to accelerate Tucker-compressed tensor–vector multiplication. Across five PEC-sphere cases, the method achieves 8.0–11.5:1 compression under 0.80-1.00 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-3</sup> tensor-level relative error and delivers 2.3–6.3× speedup in aggregation and 4.9–7.9× speedup in disaggregation.

title: When Steganography Meets Semantic Communication: An Authorizable Data Transmission Paradigm
authors: Boyu Han, Gaolei Li, Xiao Yang, Jun Wu, Jianhua Li, Yuchen Liu
abstract: Recent advances in Semantic Communication (SemCom) have integrated semantically aware encoding and channel adaptation to enhance wireless transmission efficiency. In SemCom, covert communication is extensively adopted to secure data transmission, but current methodologies remain constrained by signal-level obfuscation strategies that rely on bit-level manipulations. Consequently, they are loosely coupled with semantic feature learning and often face a rigid trade-off between covert robustness and undetectability in practice. To address these limitations, we fuse steganography with SemCom to propose an authorizable SemCom framework, embedding the true transmission data within cover inputs for covert delivery. The integration focuses on achieving highly covert secret transmission within the semantic communication architecture. In our framework, the private transmitter embeds true (i.e., secret) data into cover inputs using a secret hiding module, then applies semantic encoding for transmission. The receiving ends ensure covert data accessibility in multi-client scenarios: authorized users with the secret decoding module can fully semantically restore the true data, whereas unauthorized users solely reconstruct the apparent cover details and stay unaware of the hidden data. Moreover, to realize the above functionalities, the proposed SemCom system leverages a dual-objective joint training strategy, simultaneously learning shared knowledge of stealthy transmission and preserving the normal capability for regular data. Empirical results prove that our framework attains high-fidelity SemCom transmission for secret (PSNR $\sim 31$ dB, SSIM $\gt 91$%, and LPIPS $\sim 0.04$), and holds robustness under multiple adaptive and adversarial attack scenarios. Meanwhile, the embedded true data manifests no perceptible visual artifacts, and SemCom regular input efficacy is consistently preserved.

title: Multi-illumination Nail Analysis with Uncertainty-Aware Deep Learning: A Comprehensive Approach to Automated Fungal Detection
authors: Zenab Bosheah, Dorottya Keresztes, Péter Lõdár, Péter Gaszner, Júlia Szebényi, Eszter Marx, Zoltán Jánki, Rolland Gyulai, Vilmos Bilicki
abstract: This study presents a comprehensive approach to nail condition classification using multi-illumination imaging and uncertainty-aware deep learning.We developed a diagnostic system capturing nail images under ten lighting conditions (normal and polarized versions of white, UV, red, green, and blue light) and classifying them into three categories: normal, fungal infections, and other pathologies. Our Conv-NeXt-based neural network architecture demonstrated exceptional performance for fungal detection, achieving accuracies ranging from 93.63% to 96.31% across the various illumination modes. Multi-illumination analysis revealed distinctive signatures, with white light channels demonstrating highest importance (32–33% of attention weights) and red light showing specific utility for fungal detection (11.1% vs. 5–7% for other conditions). Spatial analysis through Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) identified that models focus primarily on nail edges when detecting fungal infections, with quantitative grid analysis showing edge regions contributing approximately 3 times more to classification decisions than central regions. Bayesian ensembles provided reliable uncertainty: predictive entropy discriminates correct vs. incorrect decisions (AUROC 0.9429), with strong calibration for the fungus classifier (Brier 0.040, ECE 0.032). t-SNE visualization demonstrated clear feature space separation, with fungal infections showing the most cohesive clustering corresponding to their distinctive illumination-spatial patterns. These convergent findings support clinical examination optimization: prioritizing nail edges under white light, with supplementary red light for suspected fungal cases, represents a promising strategy for future clinical validation.

title: Using Dynamic Safety Margins as Control Barrier Functions
authors: Victor Freire, Marco M. Nicotra
abstract: This paper presents an approach to design control barrier functions (CBFs) for arbitrary state and input constraints using tools from the reference governor literature. In particular, it is shown that dynamic safety margins (DSMs) are CBFs for an augmented system obtained by concatenating the state with a virtual reference. The proposed approach is agnostic to the relative degree and can handle multiple state and input constraints using the control-sharing property of CBFs. The construction of CBFs using Lyapunov-based DSMs is then investigated in further detail. Numerical simulations show that the method outperforms existing DSM-based approaches, while also guaranteeing safety and persistent feasibility of the associated optimization program.

title: Genetic Optimization of ±800 kV GIL Spacers for Metal Particle Expulsion
authors: Hucheng Liang, Weiwei Li, Boxue Du, Jia’nan Dong, Wencheng Yang, Tonglei Cheng, Hang Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Metal particle deposition and electric field distortion are key factors contributing to surface flashover on DC-GIL spacers. This paper presents a genetic algorithm (GA)-based optimization method for ±800 kV basin spacers, which simultaneously considers surface electric field, mechanical stress, and endows the spacers with the ability to actively repel metal particles. Four basin spacers with varying slopes are developed. In the initial designs, metal particles tend to move toward and deposit on the spacer surfaces, primarily due to attractive forces generated by the axial electric field. After optimization, the axial electric field around the spacers is reversed and strengthened, causing particles released from the high-voltage or ground electrodes to be repelled away from the spacers. For spherical metal particles, their trajectories span the entire insulation gap and are alternately influenced by attractive and repulsive forces. The larger the slope of the spacer, the more likely spherical particles are to collide with or deposit on the convex surface. In contrast, linear metal particles exhibit firefly-like motion near the high-voltage conductor, where the repelling electric field continuously prevents their deposition on the spacers. Results of this study are expected to provide guidance for the insulation design and safe operation of DC-GIL.

title: A 32-Gb/s Continuous Time Linear Equalizer With Programmable Active Inductors Technique for Wide Channel Loss Compensation
authors: Liu Wang, Chang Liu, Xiaoguang Liu, Zexin Su, Xiao Ma, Sheng Hu, Yao Liu, Franco Maloberti, Bo Li
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a 32-Gb/s continuous-time linear equalizer (CTLE) with programmable active inductors (PAIs) for wide channel loss compensation. The proposed CTLE innovatively resolves the boost gain tuning range limitation of conventional source-degenerated capacitor tuning caused by parasitic capacitance via PAI adjustment, and introduces a two-stage cascaded PAIs topology to overcome PAIs’ resistor physical size constraints, enabling a wide tuning range with wideband. Additionally, a cascaded frequency-alignment stage stabilizes the peaking frequency across all boost gain levels. The chip was fabricated in a 65-nm process. Measurements demonstrate that the chip achieves a 5–12.5 dB wide boost gain tuning range with 0.42-dB steps, a peaking frequency offset within ±10%, up to 17.3 dB of channel loss compensation at 16 GHz, and a 0.48-UI horizontal eye margin at 32 Gb/s (PRBS31 pattern, BER <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lt 10^{-12}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>).

title: Optimized Triple Phase Shift Variable Frequency Modulation for Single Stage SR-DAB based DC-AC Converters with Wide ZVS Range Operation
authors: Fangyong Wang, Junyang Bao, Yan Mao, Bangyin Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The single-stage series-resonant dual-active-bridge (SR-DAB) DC-AC converter, serving as a critical interface connecting DC sources and the AC grid, is often constrained by a narrow zero-voltage switching (ZVS) range and severe circulating current losses, while also requiring the capability to handle varying grid voltages and load fluctuations. To mitigate these limitations, this paper proposes a real-time, analytical Triple-Phase-Shift Variable Frequency Modulation (TPSVFM) strategy. This optimized scheme employs a real-time computational framework that synergizes a simplified analytical model with high-fidelity correction, thereby balancing computational feasibility with control precision. Additionally, a complementary Filtering Compensation Feedforward (FCF) strategy is introduced to suppress output current distortion and optimize system efficiency across the entire operating range. The proposed methodology ensures high flexibility and efficiency across the entire active and reactive power plane, enabling seamless bidirectional power transfer without complex auxiliary controls. Validation using a 500-W experimental prototype confirms the efficacy of the proposed approach, demonstrating a peak efficiency of 98.3%.

title: Prioritizing Feature Requests from App Reviews in Agile Mobile Software Development: A Mixed-Methods Empirical Study of Practitioners’ Perspectives
authors: Mansour Albidah, Noureddine Abbadeni, Khalid Almutib
abstract: Agile mobile software development increasingly relies on user feedback from app store reviews to inform requirements and feature request prioritization. While prior research has explored requirement elicitation and classification from user reviews, limited empirical evidence exists on how practitioners prioritize feature requests derived from such feedback in real-world agile mobile development contexts. This paper presents a mixed-methods empirical study combining a large-scale survey of 169 practitioners and semi-structured interviews with 12 practitioners to examine current practices, decision criteria, and challenges in prioritizing feature requests from app reviews. The study investigates (i) the factors influencing prioritization decisions, (ii) commonly used prioritization strategies, and (iii) how different types of feature requests are handled in practice. The results indicate that business value, implementation cost, and technical risk are the most influential prioritization factors, while contextual elements such as app store platform, organizational context, and regulatory constraints further shape decision-making. Ranking-based and classification-based components are frequently used in combination, and enhancement-related feature requests are typically addressed before other types. Although practitioners report interest in automation and machine learning support, concerns related to data quality, contextual interpretation, and trust limit their current adoption. These findings provide empirical insights into how app review feedback is translated into prioritization decisions and offer guidance for designing context-aware prioritization strategies in agile mobile software development.

title: DistShield: Distribution Preserving Model Obfuscation for Real-Time TEE-Shielded Secure Inference on IoT devices
authors: Qinglin Song, Gaojian Xiong, Yu Sun
abstract: While on-device inference avoids network latency and private data uploading in IoT, the risk of model thefts has raised serious concern. As a solution, state-of-the-art approach obfuscates critical parameters and shields de-obfuscate keys in TEE, ensuring model confidentiality with minimal overhead. However, we reveal that existing methods lead to anomalous clustering in distribution, undermining the anonymity of protected parameters. Based on this vulnerability, we demonstrate a model stealing attack which could recover over 97% of the model performance without any queries or training, severely compromising the model security. Additionally, existing methods struggle to scale for large language models. To address these challenges, we propose DistShield, which leverages distribution preserving obfuscation to generate obfuscated parameters with no anomaly. Moreover, to minimize the inference latency and adapt to large language models, iterative weight pruning is tailored to precisely narrow down the range of critical parameters. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves robust model security by protecting only 0.013% of the parameters, leading to a 10× reduction in model stealing attack accuracy, with only 11% additional TEE computation latency introduced. DistShield provides promising obfuscation scheme against model thefts on edge, while maintaining real-time inference capabilities.

title: Prompt-Free Efficient Adaptation of Segment Anything Model for Remote Sensing Landslide Detection
authors: Zuolei Li, Xingyu Gao, Zhenyu Chen, Yawen Duan, Fei Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Landslides pose serious risks to human safety and infrastructure, making accurate and timely detection essential for disaster assessment. Although deep learning has advanced landslide recognition from remote sensing imagery, existing methods typically rely on task-specific designs, which limit their generalization ability across diverse geographic environments. The Segment Anything Model (SAM) offers strong generalization and zero-shot segmentation ability, yet its dependence on manually provided prompts and limited suitability for remote sensing imagery constrain its effectiveness in landslide detection. To address these limitations, we propose PF-SAM, a prompt-free and parameter-efficient adaptation framework tailored for landslide segmentation. PF-SAM eliminates manual prompts by autonomously constructing the prompt embeddings that drive SAM’s segmentation process and adapting SAM to landslide remote sensing data. Specifically, we first propose the CNN-Token Adapter, which extracts multi-scale CNN features and transforms them into prompt tokens, providing SAM with localized geometric and textural cues essential for detecting landslides of diverse shapes and sizes. Then, we propose the Global Prompt Generation Mechanism (GPGM) that further enhances segmentation: the Dense Prompt Generation Module (DPGM) generates the coarse mask as the dense prompt to guide SAM toward the correct landslide regions, while the Sparse Prompt Generation Module (SPGM) selects high-confidence embeddings to generate sparse prompts that enhance the precise localization of landslide regions. Extensive experiments on multiple landslide datasets show that PF-SAM achieves state-of-the-art performance, substantially surpassing existing methods in detection accuracy and robustness.

title: HITE: A Hierarchical Intrinsically Interpretable Trust Evaluation Framework for Industrial IoT
authors: Meng Zhang, Yuliang Cheng, Changyu Xi, Hangyu Wang, Tong Cui
abstract: With the rapid evolution of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), dynamic trust evaluation for substantial industrial devices has emerged as a critical defense line for IIoT security. Although Deep Learning (DL) models are widely adopted due to their superior performance, their inherent “black-box” nature causes severe feature entanglement, hindering the establishment of trustworthy decision-making grounds. To mitigate this opacity, Explainable AI (XAI) paradigms—primarily Post-hoc Explanation and Intrinsic Interpretability—have been introduced. However, existing approaches face a critical dilemma: post-hoc methods merely approximate model behaviors via input perturbations, often suffering from low fidelity and approximation errors in high-dimensional IIoT scenarios; traditional intrinsic models guarantee transparency but often fail to capture complex nonlinear patterns, leading to suboptimal detection accuracy. To reconcile this trade-off, we propose HITE, a Hierarchical Intrinsically Interpretable Trust Evaluation framework. Uniquely, this framework leverages the additive structure of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) to achieve explicit additive decomposition of raw features. By synergizing with structured decision trees, HITE constructs a hybrid reasoning chain characterized by transparent representation and explicit decision logic. It provides end-to-end traceable explanations across four dimensions: feature mapping, representation attribution, decision path, and outcome scoring. Experiments conducted on two real-world industrial benchmarks, X-IIoTID and ToN-IoT, demonstrate that HITE achieves superior evaluation performance, attaining accuracies of 99.31% and 98.96%, respectively. Crucially, HITE outperforms mainstream explanation methods in terms of faithfulness and stability, realizing a critical paradigm shift from “Numerical Prediction” to “Trusted Reasoning”.

title: LPM-Aug: Latent Pathology-Informed Multimodal Augmentation for Generalized Cognitive Decline Detection Via Speech
authors: Xiaobao Wang, Bin Wen, Xiao Wei, Yuqin Lin, Kai Li, Yaru Cao, Tianrui Wang, Mingyang Gu, Yu Jiang, Jinyu Li, Longbiao Wang, Jianwu Dang
abstract: Speech-based cognitive decline detection is a promising non-invasive screening approach, yet its practical application remains challenging, particularly in modeling the complex affective-behavioral shifts associated with cognitive impairment in low-resource settings. Existing methods frequently exhibit unintended reliance on non-pathological biases, including speaker identity, transcription errors, and model-induced artifacts, which leads to shortcuts that are specific to particular datasets and tasks rather than stable pathology-focused representations. In this work, we propose LPM-Aug, a latent pathology-informed multimodal augmentation framework that aims to weaken the influence of such unintended factors while preserving disease-related acoustic and linguistic cues. Rather than indiscriminately increasing data diversity, the proposed approach encourages pathological-focused representation learning across audio and text modalities and incorporates complementary pathological cues to enhance speech-based modeling of cognitive decline. Experiments on three public datasets covering different languages and cognitive stages demonstrate that LPM-Aug consistently improves robustness and generalization compared with unimodal and non-augmented baselines.

title: Multi-Carrier Modulation: An Evolution from Time-Frequency Domain to Delay-Doppler Domain
authors: Hai Lin, Jinhong Yuan, Wei Yu, Jingxian Wu, Lajos Hanzo
abstract: The recently proposed orthogonal delay-Doppler division multiplexing (ODDM) modulation, which is a delay-Doppler (DD) domain multi-carrier (DDMC) modulation scheme based on the DD domain orthogonal pulse (DDOP), is studied. We first revisit the linear time-varying (LTV) channel model for the wireless channel, and review the conventional multi-carrier (MC) modulation schemes and their design guidelines for both linear time-invariant (LTI) and LTV channels. We then focus on the representation of the LTV channel in an equivalent sampled DD (ESDD) domain, and propose an impulse-function-based transmission strategy for the ESDD channel. Next, we take an in-depth look into the DDOP and show that it achieves orthogonality with respect to the fine time and frequency resolutions in the ESDD domain thus behaves like an impulse function. This allows us to unveil the unique input-output relation of the resultant ODDM modulation over the ESDD channel. We point out that the conventional MC modulation design guidelines based on the Weyl-Heisenberg (WH) frame theory can be relaxed without compromising its orthogonality or violating the WH frame theory. More specifically, for a practical communication system with bandwidth and duration constraints, MC modulation signals can be designed considering so-called local or sufficient (bi)orthogonality, which refers to the (bi)orthogonality among a WH subset for the MC signal within a specific bandwidth and duration. This is different from the conventional MC modulation waveform design guidelines (such as for orthogonal frequency division multiplexing and orthogonal time frequency space) based on the global (bi)orthogonality, which is the (bi)orthogonality among a WH full set corresponding to the MC signal occupying the entire TF domain. This novel design guideline could potentially open up opportunities for developing future waveforms required by new applications such as communication systems associated with high delay and/or Doppler shifts, as well as integrated sensing and communications.

title: A Synchronization Solution for Bi-static ISAC under NLOS with Rich Multipaths
authors: Shengli Ding, Baolong Chen, Yannan Yuan, Junjie Tan, Dajie Jiang, I Chih-Lin, Daqing Zhang
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is expected to play a prominent role in 6G. Avoiding full duplex transceivers, bi-static sensing is free from self-interference and able to leverage ubiquitous network devices, thus considered an indispensable scenario of ISAC. However, bi-static sensing must resolve the non-ideal synchronization of the transceiver nodes. Such timing offset (TO) remains a challenging issue, especially in the non-line-of-sight (NLOS) condition with rich multipaths. This article makes the best use of all multipaths, deriving the cyclic shift relation between the delay spectrums measured at the two sides of transceivers to estimate the TO. Based on this theoretical analysis, an algorithm called enhanced round-trip measurement (eRTM) is developed to mitigate the TO. Specifically, the cyclic cross-correlation between the amplitudes of the two normalized delay spectrums measured at the two sides of the transceivers respectively is conducted to estimate the TO, which is then used for TO mitigation. Extensive simulations have verified the advantages of the eRTM algorithm, especially in NLOS conditions and with low signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs). In addition, field tests conducted with a bi-static ISAC prototype system and eRTM algorithm, have achieved centimeter-level positioning accuracy even in NLOS conditions with rich multipaths, confirming the validities of our theoretical analysis as well as the proposed algorithm.

title: Magnetically Actuated Composite Vocal Fold Phantom for CT/TA Muscle Mimicry
authors: Onur Tavli, Kuter Erdil, Alp Kayra Dagistanli, Daghan Gokdel, Onur Ferhanoglu
abstract: We present a magnetically actuated vocal fold–phantom phonation system capable of reproducing longitudinal tension modulation to mimic cricothyroid (CT) and thyroarytenoid (TA) muscle activation. We first formulate the electromagnetic force model and describe howthe resulting artificial muscle activation alters longitudinal stiffness, transverse stiffness, and vibratory behavior. Vocal fold phantoms are fabricated using RTV silicone mixed with Fe powder and cast in 3D-printed molds. Leveraging gravity during curing, Fe powder are concentrated near the transverse surface closest to the actuation coil, enhancing magnetic responsiveness while maintaining tissue-like mechanical properties. Finite-element simulations are used to investigate how longitudinal loading modifies vibratory modes and dynamics. Experimentally, laser Doppler vibrometry (LDV) and knife-edge optical sensing validate the analytical and simulation results. Magnetic actuation enabled longitudinal elongations up to 2.0 mm, providing a tunable phonation frequency range of 127–191 Hz, consistent with simulation. Repeatability testing over 10 hours revealed only ±95 μm standard deviation in elongation, demonstrating the reliability and stability of both the actuator and the phantom. These results establish a tunable, muscle-mimicking platform that can facilitate controlled investigations of tension-dependent phonatory mechanics and support future laryngological research.

title: MIMO Extension of ATSC 3.0: System Design
authors: Sungjun Ahn, Bo-Mi Lim, Sunhyoung Kwon, Sung-Ik Park
abstract: This paper presents and proposes the system design and features pertaining to ATSC 3.0 MIMO Extension. The scope is the physical layer and closely related aspects, covering technology newly specified in A/322 (physical layer protocol), A/324 (related signaling and instructions processed in broadcast gateway), and A/327 (implementation and deployment guidelines). We detail the definition and baseline clarifications, processing and procedure descriptions, signaling parameters, and configuration constraints, together with the design rationale.

title: A GPU-enabled multi-frame reconstruction for 3D electrical tomography using Tensor cores
authors: Varun Kumar Tiwari, Mahmoud Meribout
abstract: Electrical tomography (ET) has emerged as a sustainable and non-invasive imaging technique, offering nonionizing and non-radioactive solutions for various applications. Advances in hardware systems have enabled high-speed data acquisition and image reconstruction, but the computational demands of 3D ET systems with large electrode arrays and complex finite element models present significant challenges for real-time deployment, particularly in resource-constrained edge environments. This paper proposes a GPU-enabled Tensor Core-based reconstruction strategy for designing high-speed ET systems suitable for Internet-of-Things and embedded platforms. By leveraging Tensor Core units and employing a multi-frame reconstruction technique, the proposed approach demonstrates substantial performance improvements over traditional methods. Five different algorithms, encompassing both non-iterative and iterative approaches, were implemented to validate the benefits of this strategy. Experimental results on an embedded GPU achieved a speed gain of over 13 times compared to a general-purpose computer, and over four times when using Tensor Cores instead of CUDA cores alone. The system achieved frame rates of up to 3048 frames per second for a 32-electrode setup with 2 million mesh elements, highlighting its potential to meet the computational demands of sustainable, efficient, real-time, and portable ET systems.

title: An Adaptive Inverse-Free Robust DZNN for Solving Future Linear Equation and Inequality Systems With Manipulator Application
authors: Binbin Qiu, Zijian Huang, Chao Zhong, Jinjin Guo, Yu Han
abstract: Future linear equation and inequality systems (FLEISs) are ubiquitous in dynamic engineering applications, yet solving them efficiently remains a challenge. Most existing models suffer from a fundamental tradeoff: they either grapple with the heavy computational complexity of matrix inversion or lack sufficient robustness against various noises while pursuing fast convergence. To overcome these limitations, this article proposes a novel adaptive inverse-free robust discretized zeroing neural network (AIFR-DZNN) model. The core innovation lies in the synergistic fusion of an inverse-free architecture with adaptive and robust mechanisms. Specifically, an inverse-free substitution strategy is employed to circumvent the matrix inversion bottleneck, thereby significantly reducing the computational complexity. Simultaneously, an adaptive convergence parameter and an error-summation term are seamlessly embedded to accelerate the convergence rate and enhance the robustness under noisy conditions. Rigorous theoretical analyses guarantee the stability and convergence of the proposed AIFR-DZNN model. Furthermore, comparative numerical experiments demonstrate its superior performance. Ultimately, the proposed AIFR-DZNN model is successfully applied to the path-tracking control of robotic manipulators, validating its efficacy and practicality in real-world manipulator applications.

title: Language-Guided Adaptive Spatial-Frequency-Aware Network for Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Chunpeng Wu, Shuli Cheng, Liejun Wang, Yankang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing change detection (RSCD) plays a crucial role in urban planning, disaster assessment, and ecological monitoring. However, the inherent complexity of remotely sensed images and the interference of non-targeted changes, such as illumination changes and ground renovation, often lead to the problem of misdetection of change areas by models. Moreover, many real-world changes occur at the semantic level rather than through explicit geometric or structural differences, making them difficult to distinguish based solely on visual appearance. Although deep learning-based methods have achieved significant progress in this field, most existing approaches struggle to effectively balance and address the complexities mentioned above due to insufficient semantic guidance. To this end, we propose a Language-Guided Adaptive Spatial-Frequency-Aware Network (LGSFNet) to enhance the model’s semantic guidance and discrimination ability for changing regions by introducing textual high-level semantic information at low cost. In this approach, textual prompts guide the model to focus more on change-related regions, thereby learning more discriminative features. Through disentangled fusion, this method provides a new perspective for traditional unimodal RSCD to utilize the high-level semantic information of text. Furthermore, we design a lightweight Dual-Domain Collaborative Sparse Attention mechanism in both spatial and frequency domains, which mimics human vision by adaptively emphasizing salient information. Combined with wavelet transforms, this mechanism further enhances the model’s sensitivity to complex change patterns, improving both discriminative power and robustness. Extensive experiments conducted on three widely used benchmarks—LEVIR, BCDD, and SYSU—demonstrate that LGSFNet achieves competitive performance compared with state-of-the-art methods.

title: Egocentric Online Action Segmentation via Evidential Temporal Contextualization
authors: Xun Jiang, Xing Xu, Zhangye Han, Zheng Wang, Jingkuan Song, Heng Tao Shen
abstract: The Egocentric Online Action Segmentation (EOAS) task aims to temporally segment a streaming egocentric video into a set of actions. Due to the first-person perspective views and online constraints, the EOAS task raised two intrinsic difficulties: (1) The shot movements could impact the egocentric vision and make it difficult to observe overall activities. (2) The visual signals are processed in real-time, requiring models to understand the observed visual frame without any post-contextual cues after the observed frame. To address these two problems, we proposed a novel EOAS approach named Evidential Temporal Contextualization (ETC), which consists of two key modules: (1) Context-Aggregated TRansformer that predicts and aggregates potential post context to enhance representation of frame-level features, effectively addressing the insufficient temporal contextualization in streaming videos. (2) Evidential Action Contextualization that leverages evidential deep learning to quantify the uncertainty in frame-level classification and dynamically adjust the model training and inference process accordingly. Extensive experiments on three public EOAS benchmark datasets, i.e., GTEA, EgoProceL, and EgoPER, demonstrate our proposed ETC approach outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, we also conduct more ablation studies and generalize our proposed ETC method to exocentric videos, furtherly prove its superiority. Our code is available at https://github.com/XunCHN/ETC.

title: Eight-Switch Nine-Level Common-ground Inverter with Four-fold Voltage Gain for PV Systems
authors: Felipe B. Grigoletto, Sound Cedieu, Kelwin Silveira, Sze Sing Lee, Yam P. Siwakoti
abstract: Transformerless inverters based on the common-ground (CG) concept have attracted increasing attention in grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) systems due to their ability to suppress leakage current by enforcing a constant common-mode voltage. This paper proposes a nine-level common-ground transformerless inverter employing only eight power switches, three diodes, and four capacitors. The proposed topology achieves a quadruple voltage gain within a single-stage conversion structure, enabling the direct interface of low-voltage PV sources without additional dc-dc stages. In addition to leakage current mitigation, the multilevel configuration reduces output voltage dv/dt and improves waveform quality, while a small input LC filter effectively smooths the dc-side current without introducing significant complexity. The inverter is designed for operation under both conventional low-frequency grids (60 Hz) and high-frequency ac microgrids (400 Hz), allowing a reduction in passive component size and enhanced power density. The operating principles, modulation strategy, design guidelines, and closed-loop control are presented in detail. The effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed inverter are validated through comprehensive simulation and experimental results.

title: A Compact Hybrid-Ring Circuit With Signal-Routing for Simultaneous 0° and 180° Phase-Difference Outputs
authors: Chieh Lee, Ching-Wen Tang
abstract: A compact hybrid-ring circuit with signal routing capability and simultaneous 0°/180° phase-difference outputs is proposed. The proposed topology integrates a power divider and a miniaturized 180° hybrid into a unified ring-based structure, enabling dual-phase signal generation within a compact layout. To achieve wideband phase inversion while maintaining size reduction, a slotted transmission line is incorporated into the hybrid section. Compared with conventional hybrid circuits that typically support only a single-phase operation, the proposed design provides enhanced functionality with controllable phase states and flexible signal routing. A prototype is designed, fabricated, and experimentally characterized. The measured results show good agreement with full-wave simulations, confirming the validity and effectiveness of the proposed design.
