title: Impact of Modular Non-Metallic Cryostats in the Performance of Superconducting AC Windings
authors: Luís F. D. Bucho, Inês S. P. Peixoto, Pedro P. C. Bhagubai, Silvio Vaschetto, P. J. Costa Branco, João F. P. Fernandes
abstract: For electrical machines with AC High-Temperature (HTS) Superconducting windings, where HTS tapes experience AC currents or magnetic fields, metallic cryostats may not be suitable. For these machines, non-conductive non-magnetic materials are required to avoid induced currents and impact on the magnetic field generated by AC HTS windings. Non-metallic cryostats may present an alternative solution; however, these are typically worse insulators, which results in additional heat leakage through the cryostat walls. Following this research, this work investigates the impact of modular non-metallic cryostats for HTS AC windings on electrical machine performance. Particularly, the impact of heat leakage and the required LN<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> flow cooling rate to avoid local HTS tape quenching is analyzed. A laboratory mock-up of a modular non-metallic cryostat based on Polyurethane (PUR) was developed and tested experimentally to cool an HTS tape in different conditions. Experimental tests were made to determine the insulation performance of PUR cryostats and the maximum tape current as a function of the LN<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> flow rate. Lumped parameter and finite element thermal models were developed to support the findings. Finally, the performance of an axial flux HTS machine, considering both metallic and non-metallic cryostats, is compared on the basis of finite element simulations.

title: Non-contact Respiratory Measurement of Wombats Using Millimeter-Wave Radar
authors: Marina Murakami, Ryoko Iwase, Chiemi Iba, Daisuke Ogura, Takuya Sakamoto
abstract: This study demonstrates the feasibility of radar-based non-contact respiratory monitoring for wombats. Two measurement experiments were conducted in June and December 2024 using 79-GHz millimeter-wave radar systems to monitor the respiration of two wombats. To estimate the respiratory interval, we used a method based on summing harmonic components in the autocorrelation function, capturing the quasi-periodic displacement of the body surface caused by respiration. Estimation accuracy was evaluated through simultaneous measurements from different angles using two radar units. The respiratory interval and respiratory rate were measured with errors of 47.4 ms (2.44%) and 0.81 bpm (2.21%), respectively. We also discuss the differences in respiratory rates between the two wombats, as well as seasonal variations between June and December. The results support the potential application of this method to non contact health monitoring of wombats.

title: Photocurable Polysiloxane-Based Microfluidic Chip for Convenient Sample Partitioning and Digital Nucleic Acid Assay_supp3-3682335.mp4
authors: Tao Zhang
abstract: Digital nucleic acid assay, capable of absolute quantification with high accuracy, shows promising potentials in various fields. Among others, chamber-based digital assay (cDA) is one of the most important techniques. However, challenges in sample partitioning often cause a cDA chip to suffer from complicated design/fabrication or tedious surface modification. While the polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-based chip can partially solve this problem, its slow thermal curing process forms a serious obstacle in chip manufacturing. To address these issues, we herein propose a novel photocurable polysiloxane-based cDA chip (pcDA), which enables not only simple, rapid fabrication, but also convenient sample partitioning under either negative or positive pressure. The chip molding process can be completed in less than 1 minute, and sample partitioning in several minutes. Moreover, both the isothermal method (e.g., digital recombinase polymerase amplification, dRPA) and digital polymerase chain reaction (dPCR) have been achieved, with the proof-of-concept demonstration of E.coli. and S.aureus. detection showing satisfactory results, which features a relatively wide dynamic range of 0-5000 copies/μL and high sensitivity at 1 copy/μL level. It is therefore reasonably believed that the proposed pcDA may well address the current issues of cDA technique, providing a new and viable platform for precise digital nucleic acid assay.

title: Relevance-Guided Audio Visual Fusion for Video Saliency Prediction
authors: Li Yu, Xuanzhe Sun, Pan Gao, Moncef Gabbouj
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Audio data, often synchronized with video frames, plays a crucial role in guiding the audience's visual attention. Incorporating audio information into video saliency prediction task can enhance the prediction of human visual behavior. However, existing audio-visual saliency prediction methods often directly fuse audio and visual features, without evaluating the semantic relevance between them. Fusing visual features with semantically irrelevant audio (e.g., background music) will introduce noise and degrade prediction performance. To address this issue, we propose a novel Audio-Visual Relevance-guided Saliency Prediction network (AVRSP). Specifically, the Relevance-guided Audio-Visual Feature Fusion module (RAVF) dynamically adjusts the retention of audio features based on the semantic relevance between audio and visual elements, thereby refining the fusion process with visual features. Furthermore, the Multi-scale feature Synergy (MS) module integrates visual features from different encoding stages, enhancing the network's ability to represent objects at various scales. The Multi-scale Regulator Gate (MRG) transfers crucial fusion information to visual features, thus optimizing the utilization of multi-scale visual features. Extensive experiments on six audio-visual eye movement datasets demonstrate that the proposed AVRSP network achieves superior performance to recent methods, with improvements of 1.93%, 3.05%, and 3.81% in SIM, CC, and NSS respectively.

title: Mitigating Temporal Domain Disparities for Cross-Temporal Remote Sensing Semantic Change Detection Using Vector Parcel Data and Contrastive Learning
authors: Shengli Wang, Nanshan Zheng, Yihu Zhu, Wei Liu, Ming Hao, Erzhu Li, Yilin Kuang, Yi Jiang, Lianpeng Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Most existing change detection (CD) methods assume that multi-temporal remote sensing images are acquired under similar seasonal conditions. When this assumption is violated, significant phenological variations introduce large appearance discrepancies that severely degrade detection accuracy. Moreover, most deep-learning-based CD approaches rely solely on raster imagery and neglect historical vector parcel data, resulting in limited training samples and poor generalization in real-world applications. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a vector-guided cross-temporal semantic change detection framework that mitigates temporal appearance discrepancies through contrastive feature alignment. A boundary-preserved superpixel segmentation and vector parcel-constrained adaptive cropping strategy is employed to automatically generate vector-aligned training samples, followed by a category-constrained clustering scheme to suppress label noise. A hybrid cross-temporal learning framework is then designed to reduce feature-level discrepancies between temporally mismatched images using Fourier-enhanced representations and contrastive supervision. Semantic changes are finally identified at the vector parcel level through rule-based reasoning. Experiments on two real-world regional datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves robust parcel-level semantic change detection under significant seasonal variations, outperforming representative state-of-the-art approaches and exhibiting strong generalization capability.

title: IEEE Standard for Local and Metropolitan Area Networks--Frame Replication and Elimination for Reliability Corrigendum 1: Technical and Editorial Corrections
authors: N/A
abstract: This corrigendum to IEEE Std 802.1CB™-2017 provides technical and editorial corrections.

title: Self-ensemble Learning Based on Energy Attention and Dual Branch Guidance for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Wenjie Li, Ronghua Shang, Jingyi Ding, Jie Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Due to the complex spatial heterogeneity and spectral heterogeneity of hyperspectral images, applying the self-ensembling learning with single noise perturbation in hyperspectral image classification is challenging. Therefore, this paper proposes a self-ensemble learning approach based on energy attention and dual branch guidance (EDSEL) for hyperspectral image classification. Firstly, this paper designs a neural network based on energy attention to extract spatial and spectral features of hyperspectral images. The spectral and spatial attention is optimized through the energy function between pixels in the spectral and spatial dimensions, to preserve multi-scale spatial structure information and obtain discriminative features of ground objects with complex spatial distributions. Secondly, EDSEL uses a teacher-assisted loss function for the training process of labeled samples. The classification loss function incorporates the alignment information between the teacher and student networks. This process assists the student network in learning from the teacher network’s knowledge and in extracting features that distinguish the labeled samples. Finally, EDSEL designs a dual branch guidance strategy for consistency learning of unlabeled samples. Two branches use static and temporal noise to help the network obtain consistent representations under different noise conditions, learning the essential spatial-spectral features and enhancing the classification performance. The experimental results on three hyperspectral image datasets show that the EDSEL performs better than the seven advanced algorithms.

title: Domain-Adapted SAM Meets Unsupervised Learning: A Novel Solution for Farmland Change Detection in High-Resolution Imagery
authors: Qiang Zhang, Yi Zhang, Zhicheng Wei, Sai Zhong, Yaxiong Chen, Guirong Feng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Farmland change detection is crucial for ensuring food security and sustainable agricultural management. While deep learning has shown promise, its application is severely hampered by the scarcity of high-quality labeled data in practical agricultural scenarios. To address the challenges of poor feature generalization and inadequate spatio-temporal modeling in unsupervised settings, this paper proposes STCU (Spatio-Temporal Change Understanding), a novel unsupervised framework that synergizes a domain-adapted Segment Anything Model (SAM) with advanced temporal modeling. The core contributions are threefold: First, we introduce Low-rank Adaptation (LoRA) to fine-tune the pre-trained SAM, effectively mitigating the domain discrepancy between natural and remote sensing images. This adaptation enables the generation of high-precision, spatially consistent change feature maps from bitemporal images without any supervision. Second, we design the Temporal Convolutional Gated Module (TCGM) within the STCU framework. The TCGM explicitly models spatio-temporal dependencies between dual-temporal features through multi-scale depthwise convolution and a gating mechanism, allowing it to capture both gradual transitions and abrupt changes while dynamically suppressing temporal noise. Furthermore, a multi-task loss function is devised to enhance feature disentanglement and joint optimization. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks (CLCD, JL1, and RSIPAC) demonstrate that STCU significantly outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods (e.g., SCM, FCD-GAN, Trans-MAD), achieving superior F1 scores of 53.69%, 60.37%, and 46.38%, respectively. The results confirm STCU's strong generalization capability and robustness in complex farmland environments, offering a practical and effective solution for large-scale agricultural monitoring.

title: Uncertainty Quantification in Detection Transformers: Object-Level Calibration and Image-Level Reliability
authors: Young-Jin Park, Carson Sobolewski, Navid Azizan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: DEtection TRansformer (DETR) and its variants have emerged as promising architectures for object detection, offering an end-to-end prediction pipeline. In practice, however, DETRs generate hundreds of predictions that far outnumber the actual objects present in an image. This raises a critical question: which of these predictions could be trusted? This is particularly important for safety-critical applications, such as in autonomous vehicles. Addressing this concern, we provide empirical and theoretical evidence that predictions within the same image play distinct roles, resulting in varying reliability levels. Our analysis reveals that DETRs employ an optimal “specialist strategy”: one prediction per object is trained to be well-calibrated , while the remaining predictions are trained to suppress their foreground confidence to near zero, even when maintaining accurate localization. We show that this strategy emerges as the loss-minimizing solution to the Hungarian matching algorithm, fundamentally shaping DETRs' outputs. While selecting the well-calibrated predictions is ideal, they are unidentifiable at inference time. This means that any post-processing algorithm—used to identify trustworthy predictions—poses a risk of outputting a set of predictions with mixed calibration levels. Therefore, practical deployment necessitates a joint evaluation of both the model's calibration quality and the effectiveness of the post-processing algorithm. However, we demonstrate that existing metrics like average precision and expected calibration error are inadequate for this task. To address this issue, we further introduce Object-level Calibration Error (OCE), which evaluates calibration by aggregating predictions per ground-truth object rather than per prediction. This object-centric design penalizes both retaining suppressed predictions and missed ground truth foreground objects, making OCE suitable for both evaluating models and identifying reliable prediction subsets. Finally, we present a post hoc uncertainty quantification (UQ) framework that predicts per-image model accuracy.

title: Meetings Calendar
authors: Frank Hegeler
abstract: Frank Hegeler, Jerome Castellon, Nancy Frost, George Laity, Pietro Romano, and Feipeng Wang

title: Active Cross-Task Knowledge Transfer Learning Framework for Semi-supervised Joint 3D Grounding and Captioning
authors: Yang Liu, Daizong Liu, Wei Hu, Baoquan Chen
abstract: 3D visual grounding is a fundamental task hindered by the requirement for large-scale, costly annotated data. To overcome this limitation, we address a challenging semisupervised setting for 3D grounding, where only a small fraction of text-object pairs are labeled. We propose a novel solution that leverages knowledge from a complementary task, 3D captioning, to learn effectively from abundant unlabeled data. Our main contribution is the 3D Cross-Task Teacher-Student Framework++ (3D-CTTSF++), which jointly learns grounding and captioning. Key novelties of our framework include: (1) a multi-teacher to single-student paradigm to distill robust and generalizable knowledge from limited labeled data; (2) a cross-task pseudolabel generation and filtering mechanism that uses the synergy between grounding and captioning to provide reliable supervision; and (3) an active sampling strategy to progressively incorporate high-quality pseudo-labels into the training set. Extensive experiments demonstrate that 3D-CTTSF++ achieves competitive performance against fully-and weakly-supervised methods, validating it as an effective solution to overcome the data scarcity issue in 3D vision-language tasks.

title: Computing Node Failure Prediction Based on Continuous-Time Dynamic Graph
authors: Binbin Huang, Teng Bao, Feiyi Chen, Lingbin Wang, Xunqing Huang, Yuyu Yin, Xiaoying Shi, Shangguang Wang, Shuiguang Deng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: The growth of large models demands multinode cooperation during training and inference processes. The computing node failures can interrupt these processes, subsequently causing information loss and prolonging the execution time. To reduce the prohibitively large overhead incurred by the computing nodes failures, the accurate prediction of computing node failure is vital, which can help to avert potential large overhead, service interruptions, and negative customer experiences. Existing solutions of computing nodes failure prediction mainly focus on utilizing state-of-the-art time-series models to enhance the performance of computing node failure prediction. However, on the one hand, they could not capture the causal relationship between device over-utilization and node failures; On the other hand, they fail to extract the complex spatial–temporal cascading correlations among computing node failure events. These limits can degrade the performance of computing node failure prediction. To address these above problems, this article makes an effort to focus on designing a continuous-time dynamic graphs-based computing node failures prediction (CTDG-NFP) scheme, to accurately predict in dynamic cluster environments. Specifically, the CTDG-NFP scheme first designs a novel multiple-dimensional feature-biased neighbor sampling method, which jointly considers CPU utilization-biased, memory utilization-biased, temporal-biased and spatial-biased, to sample relevant context. Then, the CTDG-NFP scheme extracts diverse computing node failure motifs by multiple-dimensional feature-biased-based long–short-path walk method and set-based anonymization method. Finally, the CTDG-NFP scheme adopts time encoder to encode these motifs, and thereby extracting the complex spatial–temporal correlations among computing node failure events. On this basis, contrastive learning is adopted to train the computing node failure prediction model. Extensive evaluations with various real-world failure traces demonstrate the CTDG-NFP scheme can achieve superior performance in terms of six widely used performance metrics compared with the SOTA node failure prediction methods.

title: Age-of-Information Aware Mobility-Based Vehicular-Fog Formation Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Seifu Birhanu Tadele, Binayak Kar, Frezer Guteta Wakgra, Madhusanka Liyanage
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Vehicular-fog computing (VFC) is an emerging approach aimed at providing low-latency services for IoT devices and intelligent traffic systems. In VFC, vehicles and roadside units (RSUs) work together to create a fog infrastructure, enabling the processing and storage of real-time traffic data at the network edge. However, vehicle mobility poses significant challenges for maintaining stable communication and network connectivity. Current studies often assume that all required information for forming a VFC network is fully accessible to every node. Additionally, fog devices are typically regarded as pre-configured and stationed at fixed locations near user groups, consistently delivering predefined services. However, variations in distance, location, speed, and direction between vehicles and infrastructure can disrupt vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication and compromise the reliability of fog formation. To address these challenges, we investigate dynamic fog formation involving mobile vehicles and RSUs in urban scenarios. Our approach emphasizes real-time RSU association and fog formation to ensure timely updates and maintain reliable V2I communication. We propose a solution utilizing deep reinforcement learning (DRL), specifically the dueling double deep Q-network (Dueling DDQN) algorithm. In our simulations, we employed the Gauss-Markov Mobility (GMM) model to evaluate its performance compared to DQN, DDQN, and Dueling DQN algorithms. The results demonstrate that Dueling DDQN outperforms the other methods, providing substantial improvements in maintaining the freshness of real-time system information and enhancing the reliability of vehicular-fog formation.

title: Consistency-Aware Local Path Planning for Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships Under Perception Uncertainty: A Field-Validated Framework_supp2-3677261.mp4
authors: Chenguang Liu
abstract: Ensuring consistent and reliable collision avoidance under perception uncertainty remains a major challenge for maritime autonomous surface ships (MASS). This paper proposes a lightweight and consistency-aware local path planning framework, called consistent Kalman virtual potential field (CKVPF), to improve decision robustness in dynamic and uncertain maritime environments. The framework combines virtual potential field-based planning with Kalman-filtered state estimation and introduces a historical consistency constraint to reduce decision fluctuations over time. CKVPF is implemented on a 45-meter-long MASS platform and tested through full-scale sea trials, with onboard real-time execution achieved using a Raspberry Pi 5. The experiments cover representative encounter scenarios, including head-on, overtaking, crossing and mixed-traffic, where the target ships exhibit unstable motion patterns. Results show that CKVPF maintains consistent decision-making and safely avoids collisions, achieving a 48.9% improvement in navigation path stability compared to baseline methods, while maintaining an average planning time of 177.8 ms. These findings demonstrate the method’s practical applicability and real-time performance for autonomous navigation under real-world uncertainty.

title: Consistency-Aware Local Path Planning for Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships Under Perception Uncertainty: A Field-Validated Framework_supp1-3677261.mp4
authors: Chenguang Liu
abstract: Ensuring consistent and reliable collision avoidance under perception uncertainty remains a major challenge for maritime autonomous surface ships (MASS). This paper proposes a lightweight and consistency-aware local path planning framework, called consistent Kalman virtual potential field (CKVPF), to improve decision robustness in dynamic and uncertain maritime environments. The framework combines virtual potential field-based planning with Kalman-filtered state estimation and introduces a historical consistency constraint to reduce decision fluctuations over time. CKVPF is implemented on a 45-meter-long MASS platform and tested through full-scale sea trials, with onboard real-time execution achieved using a Raspberry Pi 5. The experiments cover representative encounter scenarios, including head-on, overtaking, crossing and mixed-traffic, where the target ships exhibit unstable motion patterns. Results show that CKVPF maintains consistent decision-making and safely avoids collisions, achieving a 48.9% improvement in navigation path stability compared to baseline methods, while maintaining an average planning time of 177.8 ms. These findings demonstrate the method’s practical applicability and real-time performance for autonomous navigation under real-world uncertainty.

title: Decoupled Heuristic Multi-Vehicle Emergency Trajectory Planning for Sudden Obstacles
authors: Dengyu Xiao, Zhenyang Zeng, Chuan Tong, Mengdie Huang, Gang Wang, Jun Luo, Huayan Pu
abstract: The emergence of sudden obstacles can significantly reduce the feasible space and may induce locally non-convex or fragmented space, especially in densely clustered scenarios, making vehicle trajectory planning remarkably challenging. Current methods face computational bottlenecks when generating emergency trajectories under such tight real-time constraints. To address this issue, we decouple safety-critical guidance from trajectory optimization for suddenly appearing obstacles. Specifically, a novel unified nonpositivity quantification method based on vector cross-product consistency is introduced to numerically constrain non-convex regions and a heuristic risk metric is designed to guide the optimization of avoidance target. Additionally, a dynamic priority strategy is further designed to adaptively adjust the constraint dimensionality in real time, improving the success rate of emergency planning. Comparative evaluations with existing emergency planning methods demonstrate the superiority of the proposed approach in terms of success rate, planning time, and emergency trajectory length. Finally, several real-world multi-vehicle experiments validate the effectiveness and practical applicability of the proposed method.

title: A Gradual-Steepness Recurrent Neural Network for Rust Removal Motion Planning of Multiple Mobile Manipulators on Curved Surfaces
authors: Yuguo Li, Zhijun Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: To solve the rust removal planning problem for multiple mobile manipulators on curved surfaces, a novel Gradual-Steepness Recurrent Neural Network (GS-RNN) is proposed in this article. Firstly, the K-means algorithm and ant colony algorithm are used to generate collaborative rust removal paths for multiple manipulators based on the surface and rust removal requirements. Then, considering the end-effector tracking task and velocity constraints, the rust removal task for multiple mobile manipulators on the curved surface is formulated as a time-varying quadratic programming (TVQP) problem with equality and inequality constraints. Secondly, the steepening function is designed, the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) condition is improved, and the relevant theories and detailed derivations are given. Finally, the GS-RNN for solving this problem was obtained. Computer simulation experiments verify the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed method, and the results show that GS-RNN significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of precision and robustness.

title: Fusing Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic with Bidirectional RNNs for Uncertainty-Aware Time Series Forecasting
authors: Xi Yu, Haiqing Zhang, Mengji Yang, Yuanping Xu, Lei Mou, Daiwei Li
abstract: With the explosive growth of multidimensional data, uncertainty in time series forecasting has become increasingly prominent. Although deep learning models demonstrate strong predictive capabilities, they remain sensitive to data uncertainty, while fuzzy logic systems (FLS) offer interpretability but often suffer from predefined parameter sensitivity and high computational overhead. To address these challenges, this paper proposes the IT2-FLS-BiRNN framework, which combines the temporal feature extraction capabilities of Bidirectional Recurrent Neural Networks (BiRNNs) with the uncertainty quantification strengths of Interval Type-2 FLS (IT2-FLS). To overcome limitations related to membership function sensitivity and constrained generalization, a fuzzy membership learning strategy is introduced to enable a data-aligned transformation of input features into IT2 fuzzy sets. Moreover, to alleviate the considerable computational overhead commonly associated with fuzzy systems, a parallelizable IT2-FLS inference mechanism is developed, which leverages matrix-based parallel computation for efficient reasoning. To evaluate the effectiveness and robustness of the IT2-FLS-BiRNN framework, experiments are conducted on ten publicly available benchmark datasets with varying levels of noise and nonlinear characteristics. Experimental results show that the proposed method consistently achieves superior performance across multiple evaluation metrics and outperforms most baseline models. In addition, noise analysis further reveals that IT2-FLS-BiRNN maintains significantly lower prediction errors compared to competing methods, particularly under high noise conditions, thereby highlighting its robustness in managing uncertainty for time series forecasting.

title: Surface Electric Field Distribution Characteristics of Stator Slots in High-Voltage Generators
authors: Minghe Chi, Dayuan Xiu, Zhou Yang, Yu Feng, Qingguo Chen, Zhihang Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The electric field concentrates at the end of the stator slot of a high-voltage generator, which is prone to corona discharge. At present, there is a lack of accurate mathematical models to guide the design of anti-corona structure, which restricts the improvement of the generator voltage level. Based on the idea of infinitesimal elements, this paper constructs and analyzes the unit circuit model of the stator slot end insulation, puts forward the concept of the voltage division coefficient of the anti-corona structure, and clarifies the relationship between this coefficient and the resistance-capacitance ratio of the anti-corona layer. The study points out that by controlling the voltage division coefficient of the anti-corona structure at each point on the anti-corona layer and making the resistivity of the anti-corona layer increase proportionally along its length direction (with the increase of the distance from the start of the anti-corona layer), the ideal anti-corona effect can be achieved. Thus, by deriving the analytical distribution law of the resistivity of the anti-corona layer along the axial direction, a forward design criterion for achieving a uniform electric field is established. The effectiveness of the model is verified by finite element simulation. The anti-corona design method derived from this model effectively modulates the electric field strength on the stator slot surface without employing nonlinear anti-corona materials, while simultaneously minimizing the length of the anti-corona layer.

title: Revisiting Linear Distinguishing Attacks on SNOW 2.0 Stream Cipher
authors: Sudong Ma, Chenhui Jin, Qiuling He, Jie Guan, Ting Cui, Lin Ding
abstract: SNOW 2.0 is a word-oriented stream cipher that has been standardized by ISO/IEC 18033-4. At FSE 2006, Nyberg et al. proposed a linear distinguisher for SNOW 2.0 with absolute correlation 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−85:89</sup>, derived from four linear approximations of the Finite State Machine (FSM). When deriving the overall correlation, they assumed these four linear approximations to be mutually independent. However, they are in fact dependent because of a shared variable, which leads to the inaccuracy of the correlation calculation. Moreover, they impose the unnecessary restriction that all masks of the distinguisher must be identical, artificially limiting the search space and yielding inaccurate conclusions about the minimum number of active S-boxes. To resolve these contradictions, we first establish a general SNOW 2.0 linear distinguisher without any unnecessary restrictions. Secondly, we present and prove an exact formula to calculate the correlations of the general SNOW 2.0 distinguishers, thereby correcting the current best absolute correlation from 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−85:89</sup> to 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−86:14</sup>. Thirdly, we establish an Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model to search for the optimal SNOW 2.0 linear approximation trail, which will be used to derive both the optimal linear approximation trail and the minimum number of active S-boxes. The results prove that the maximum absolute correlation of the SNOW 2.0 linear approximation trail is no higher than 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−75</sup>. Consequently, if the maximum absolute correlation of the linear approximation trail is taken as the security measure, then SNOW 2.0 can guarantee the 128-bit security level against the linear distinguishing attack. Based on the MILP model, we prove that the minimum number of active S-boxes of the linear distinguisher is 12, not 14 as previously reported. Finally, we observe that the identical distinguisher masks tend to yield high absolute correlation. We therefore exhaustively enumerate this large class of distinguishers and find that the best absolute correlation remains 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−86:14</sup>. Then the time/data complexity of the linear distinguishing attack on SNOW 2.0 can be evaluated as 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">172:28</sup>. Additionally, our formula reveals another distinguisher whose true correlation is 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−88:37</sup>, while the result calculated by the original formula is 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−107:23</sup>. These results demonstrate that ignoring the dependency among approximations can lead to significant underestimation of the correlation.

title: Explainable Adaptive Path Reconfiguration for Obstacle Avoidance in UAV Networks Using Transformer-based Predictive Modeling and Bio-Inspired Optimization
authors: Farid Ud Din, Abdus Subhan, Atiq Ur Rehman, Ali Sayyed, Tahir Abbas, Zeeshan Abbas, Seung Won Lee
abstract: Critical challenges for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) networks in FANETs operation include collision avoidance, energy efficiency, and decision transparency in highly dynamic aerial conditions. This paper proposes an Explainable Adaptive Path Reconfiguration framework by integrating Transformer-based trajectory prediction, Secretary Bird Optimization Algorithm (SBOA), and SHAP-driven interpretability, enabling predictive, self-aware UAV navigation. The Transformer model predicts future UAV and obstacle trajectories under multi-head self-attention to proactively reconfigure before any potential collision risk. Then, the SBOA further optimizes the trajectories by minimizing a multi-objective cost function that jointly considers collision risk, energy consumption, and path deviation. It estimates the quantitative contribution of key features distance, velocity, and orientation-to each decision made, guaranteeing human-interpretable autonomy. Extensive simulations for diverse UAV densities and obstacle environments prove that EAPR achieves 96.2% collision avoidance, 82.8% energy efficiency, and 0.128 path deviation with improvements of 15%~25% over PSO, FHOA, and PSOA. Robustness tests further confirm resilience in case of communication loss and dense obstacle scenarios. This paper establishes a unified paradigm of predictive, optimized, and explainable UAV swarm navigation for safe and transparent autonomous operations in next-generation aerial networks.

title: Robust Hybrid Beamforming for Wideband mmWave MIMO ISAC With Hardware Impairments
authors: Yiyang Shen, Chenhao Qi, Shiwen Mao
abstract: We investigate a robust hybrid beamforming design for wideband millimeter wave integrated sensing and communications under stochastic hardware impairments (HWI), including the phase shifter quantization errors and true-time-delay (TTD) group delay fluctuations. We derive the closed-form expressions for the expectations of the autocorrelation matrix of the impaired beamforming matrix. Aiming at minimizing the Cramér–Rao bound (CRB) on the estimated target direction, we propose a hardware impairment robust hybrid beamforming (HWI-RHBF) scheme to jointly optimize the analog phase shifter network, TTD network and digital beamformer subject to the constraints of signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) of communication users and total transmit power of the base station (BS). Based on the penalty dual decomposition method, the HWI-RHBF scheme transforms the original problem into manageable subproblems for iterative optimization, where the CRB, SINR of users and transmit power of BS in each iteration are computed from the closed-form expressions of the autocorrelation matrix of the impaired beamforming matrix. Simulation results show the superiority of the proposed robust design over the existing schemes under HWI.

title: Design of a High-Contrast Cogging Torque Outer Rotor PM Machine with Continuous Adjustability for Integrated Robot Joint Drive
authors: Ling Qin, Jiyao Wang, Wei Xu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents the design methodology of an outer rotor PM machine with continuously adjustable cogging torque for integrated robot joint drive. The proposed machine under normal operation is programmed to have excellent electromagnetic performances as a conventional field modulation permanent magnet (PM) machine, with high electromagnetic torque and low torque ripple that provides smooth power production. Additionally, the proposed machine has a state of high cogging torque, during power-off condition, provides passive locking force to keep the shaft from rolling, which can reduce energy consumption. The cogging torque amplitude can also be adjusted continuously to a desired locking force level. With this design, the mechanical parking/holding brake can be eliminated together with its dust contamination, corrosion and abrasion concerns. This greatly reduces the maintenance need of a system, making it suitable for the application of integrated robot joint system. The evolution of candidate machine structure is shown in this paper, followed by an analytical evaluation of cogging torque generation. Then, the optimization process is employed to the candidate topology to determine the optimal electromagnetic and geometric design parameters. Finally, a prototype based on the optimal topology is accordingly manufactured and experimentally verified. The test result shows a high-contrast cogging torque range of 101% rated torque.

title: Performance Enhancement of Drowning Detection Using Synthetic Data
authors: Minju Kim, Jinhwan Koh
abstract: Drowning accidents remain a critical global safety issue, yet vision-based deep learning approaches are hindered by the scarcity of real drowning data due to ethical and legal constraints. This paper proposes a synthetic data–driven drowning detection framework that combines structured prompt-based image generation with a Residual-GAN to construct a balanced dataset of 8,400 synthetic images (400 prompt-generated seeds and 8,000 GAN-generated samples). A lightweight residual CNN (two Residual Blocks with Global Average Pooling) is then trained exclusively on synthetic data. To assess real-world applicability, we perform synthetic-to-real cross-domain validation using an independent test set collected from the Roboflow Universe public dataset platform (500 drowning and 500 non-drowning images) without fine-tuning. The proposed model achieves 98.0% validation accuracy and 94.3% test accuracy, demonstrating performance comparable to AlexNet (94.8%) and ResNet18 (94.9%) while maintaining a significantly more compact architecture. These results indicate that controllable synthetic data generation combined with lightweight residual learning can provide an effective foundation for drowning detection under real-data scarcity.

title: A Transformer-Enhanced Hybrid Physics-Informed Neural Network for Aircraft Aerodynamic Parameter Identification
authors: Yueji Wang, Yu Hu, Weizhi Chen, Yanshuo Zhang, Shi Yu, Qianchao He, Yunhao Li
abstract: This paper presents a novel hybrid framework that integrates a Transformer encoder and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) decoder with Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for aircraft aerodynamic parameter identification. Traditional methods, including Genetic Algorithm (GA) and Extended Kalman filter (EKF) techniques, often suffer from difficulties in limited incorporation of physical prior knowledge and precision of aerodynamic parameters in high-fidelity simulator data that closely approximates real flight data. To address these limitations, we propose a hybrid framework that combines the temporal modeling capabilities of Transformers with the physical consistency enforcement of PINNs. The proposed framework employs a Transformer encoder to process historical flight sequences and compress them into a comprehensive contextual representation from which an MLP decoder predict flight trajectories. Furthermore, this architecture is augmented with Fourier feature embeddings, which mitigate the spectral bias commonly observed in traditional MLPs while simultaneously enhancing the representation of multi-frequency dynamics inherent in flight data. To ensure physical consistency throughout the learning process, the aircraft’s longitudinal dynamics equations are embedded as soft constraints within a comprehensive multi-objective loss function. This loss function systematically incorporates multiple critical components, including data fidelity loss, physical loss, initial condition loss, and prior knowledge loss. Finally, an adaptive weighting strategy is implemented to dynamically balance these loss components during the training process, thereby ensuring stable convergence and optimal performance across different flight regimes. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves better precision than MLP-based PINN,GA and EKF baselines, highlighting its potential for high-fidelity aircraft modeling and system identification.

title: Dual-Population Cooperative Algorithm Integrating an LLM and an Evolutionary Algorithm for AUV Path Planning in Three-Dimensional Marine Environments
authors: Jie Wu, Zhiyong Wang, Liang Cheng, Yanjie Song
abstract: Existing AUV path planning methods still face limitations in modeling real three-dimensional (3D) maritime search and rescue (SAR) environments, multi-objective evaluation and safety constraint expression, and maintaining convergence stability in complex search spaces. To address these issues, we propose a dual-population cooperative algorithm that integrates a large language model (LLM) with an evolutionary algorithm (EA) for marine SAR tasks, named LLM_EA. Firstly, we integrate real seabed topography and 3D ocean currents to construct a unified navigation environment. A multi-objective evaluation framework is further designed to balance energy consumption, safety risk, and attitude stability. During the evolutionary process, LLM_EA performs parallel co-evolution between an EA population for numerical search and an LLM population for semantic reconstruction. A feasibility assurance and cross-population information exchange mechanism is designed to improve feasibility and efficiency, and a topological diversity preservation mechanism is introduced to mitigate topological collapse and maintain structural diversity. Comparative experiments show that LLM_EA consistently generates feasible trajectories under diverse geographical environments and complex constraints while achieving better overall performance and more stable convergence than multiple representative algorithms. Ablation experiments further confirm the contributions of the topological diversity preservation mechanism and the LLM strategy. This framework offers a reusable solution for efficient and robust AUV path planning in real-world 3D maritime SAR scenarios.

title: Photocurable Polysiloxane-Based Microfluidic Chip for Convenient Sample Partitioning and Digital Nucleic Acid Assay_supp2-3682335.mp4
authors: Tao Zhang
abstract: Digital nucleic acid assay, capable of absolute quantification with high accuracy, shows promising potentials in various fields. Among others, chamber-based digital assay (cDA) is one of the most important techniques. However, challenges in sample partitioning often cause a cDA chip to suffer from complicated design/fabrication or tedious surface modification. While the polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-based chip can partially solve this problem, its slow thermal curing process forms a serious obstacle in chip manufacturing. To address these issues, we herein propose a novel photocurable polysiloxane-based cDA chip (pcDA), which enables not only simple, rapid fabrication, but also convenient sample partitioning under either negative or positive pressure. The chip molding process can be completed in less than 1 minute, and sample partitioning in several minutes. Moreover, both the isothermal method (e.g., digital recombinase polymerase amplification, dRPA) and digital polymerase chain reaction (dPCR) have been achieved, with the proof-of-concept demonstration of E.coli. and S.aureus. detection showing satisfactory results, which features a relatively wide dynamic range of 0-5000 copies/μL and high sensitivity at 1 copy/μL level. It is therefore reasonably believed that the proposed pcDA may well address the current issues of cDA technique, providing a new and viable platform for precise digital nucleic acid assay.

title: A Novel DFT-Based Flexible RSMA Scheme for 6G Networks
authors: Kenza Abela, Ayoub Ammar Boudjelal, Shaima Abidrabbu, Hüseyin Arslan
abstract: Sixth-generation (6G) networks require multiple-access schemes that jointly provide high spectral efficiency (SE), low receiver complexity, and robustness in frequency-selective channels. Although rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) offers flexible interference management, its practical deployment is often limited by reliance on successive interference cancellation (SIC). This paper proposes a flexible RSMA framework in which the common stream is spread over configurable discrete Fourier transform (DFT) blocks, while private streams are transmitted using conventional orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM). By jointly tuning the DFT block structure and adopting a peak-aware power allocation strategy, the proposed design mitigates interference and reduces reliance on SIC. Simulation results demonstrate improved bit error rate (BER) performance while maintaining SE comparable to RSMA and NOMA under perfect channel state information (CSI), and significantly outperforming these baselines under imperfect CSI conditions.

title: A Dynamic Frequency Selection Module for Geometry-Aware Building Extraction in VHR Remote Sensing Image
authors: Yi Huang, Siyu Hong, Shaohua Lei, Hui Chen, Peijun Du, Jinlong Sun, Haitao Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Reliable measurement of building footprints, encompassing precise boundary localization and planar area estimation, is fundamental to urban applications using very high-resolution (VHR) remote sensing imagery. Conventional semantic segmentation pipelines optimize pixel-wise accuracy but often suffer from blurred edges and shape distortions, leading to biased geometric measurements. Although recent frequency-domain methods attempt to recover structural details, they typically rely on static filters that fail to adapt across diverse building topologies and complex background clutter. To overcome this limitation, we propose the Dynamic Frequency Selection Module (DFSM), a plug-and-play adaptive frequency selection module for geometry-aware building extraction. DFSM parameterizes the frequency cutoff into dynamically learnable, continuous variables, functioning as a content-aware filter. It adaptively modulates the spectral passband—widening it to suppress high-frequency noise for small, cluttered buildings, and narrowing it to effectively sharpen edge contours for large-scale structures. Subsequently, an attention-based fusion mechanism integrates these frequency representations with spatial context, optimizing the inherent trade-off between regional area integrity and boundary precision. Comprehensive evaluations on the ISPRS Vaihingen and WHU datasets demonstrate that DFSM consistently enhances both CNN- and Transformer-based segmenters. Beyond improving conventional regional metrics, DFSM elevates Boundary-Intersection over Union (Boundary-IoU) by 3%–4% and significantly reduces the 95% Hausdorff distance. Notably, DFSM outperforms explicit boundary-supervised baselines without requiring auxiliary edge annotations or multi-task loss tuning. By systematically tightening the 95% coverage intervals for geometric biases, DFSM provides a robust, architecture-agnostic solution for geometry-aware building extraction in remote sensing.

title: A Multi-Axis Convolution Network for Small and High-Aspect-Ratio Object Detection in Remote Sensing
authors: Feng Zhang, Jiaofeng Wang, Jie Mao, Chunru Dong, Qiang Hua, Jie Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing object detection (RSOD) is vital for tasks ranging from environmental monitoring to infrastructure analysis. However, due to the unique perspective and extensive spatial coverage of remote sensing images, feature attenuation and localization errors occur, posing significant challenges for reliable detection of small objects and High-Aspect-Ratio (HAR) objects in object detection tasks. To address these limitations, we propose the Multi-Axis Convolution Network (MACNet), a novel architecture designed to enhance feature representation for small objects and improve shape discrimination for HAR objects. MACNet introduces two innovative modules: the Large-Kernel Dual-Branch (LKDB) module, which utilizes large-kernel convolutions and parallel spatial and channel attention to enhance contextual understanding and mitigate feature attenuation of small objects, and the Direction-aware Convolution (DC) module, which employs dual orthogonal strip convolutions to capture distinctive shape patterns that is crucial for HAR objects. Additionally, we propose a Dynamically weighted KFIoU (DKFIoU) loss function to balance the contribution of center point loss and IoU loss. DKFIoU adaptively weights the center point loss and a Gaussian-approximated IoU based on object size and aspect ratio, thereby improving the detection of HAR objects and prioritizing small objects by reducing localization errors. Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that MACNet significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, particularly for small objects and HAR objects. Ablation studies provide valuable insights into the efficacy of our proposed components. https://github.com/ww66671/mac net.

title: Receding Horizon Optimization for Network-Balanced Placement of Multiple Stratospheric Airships in Dynamic Environments
authors: Yuanqiao Fan, Xiaolong Deng, Yanchu Yang, Xixiang Yang, Fangchao Bai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Stratospheric airships, as high-altitude platform stations, are potential to provide wide-area wireless communication in next generation networks. This paper addresses the deployment of multiple stratospheric airships in dynamic environments. It investigates the balance between the aggregate rate of user equipment (UEs) and the capacity of inter-airship links, and establishes a formal criterion as a network constraint. A receding horizon optimization framework with heuristic initialization is proposed to jointly optimize placement and airship-to-UE rates. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances throughput and capacity balance compared to Voronoi-based strategies, while maintaining compliance with restricted region constraints under dynamic wind conditions.

title: An Efficient Motion Parameters Estimation Method for Ground Maneuvering Targets in Wide-Swath MIMO-SAR
authors: Ling Qiao, Mingwei Shen, Guodong Jin, Di Wu, Daiyin Zhu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurately estimating the motion parameters of ground maneuvering targets in airborne wide-swath multiple-input multiple-output synthetic aperture radar (MIMO-SAR) is challenging under low pulse repetition frequency and strong clutter. Rather than resampling the periodic nonuniform sampling (PNS) sequence for parameter estimation, the proposed method confines spectrum reconstruction to clutter preprocessing and exploits the restored PNS structure to efficiently estimate radial acceleration and radial velocity. First, the azimuth degrees of freedom of the MIMO-SAR are leveraged to construct equivalent dual-channel data, and conjugate-symmetric difference operators suppress clutter, improving the signal-to-clutter-plus-noise ratio while preserving the PNS structure. Then, a parallel differential-phase model with a common slope and group-dependent intercepts is formulated, and a group-coherent generalized likelihood ratio test (GC-GLRT) estimator incorporating coherence-adaptive window selection is designed for robust estimation of radial acceleration. By treating radial velocity as a nuisance parameter through intra-group coherent integration and inter-group noncoherent combination, the original two-dimensional velocity–acceleration search is reduced to a one-dimensional acceleration search. Finally, after acceleration compensation, multi-delay differential-phase observations derived from the PNS structure are combined to form closure-phase observations, yielding an ambiguous radial velocity estimate. The velocity ambiguity is resolved using reference-delay observations together with a coherence-weighted decision rule, and the final estimate is refined through a weighted least-squares (WLS) fit to the residual phase. Simulation results show accuracy comparable to the generalized Radon-Fourier transform (GRFT) with substantially reduced computational complexity, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm.

title: Digital Twin Framework for Interpretable Aero-Engine RUL Prediction via Multi-Branch LSTM and Unity Visualization
authors: Anping Wan, Zengzhen Zhu, Khalil Al-Bukhaiti, Rui Yin, Jiantao Yuan, Xiaomin Cheng, Xiaosheng Ji
abstract: Aero-engines, as pivotal components in aviation, demand robust prognostics and health management (PHM) to mitigate degradation risks and optimize maintenance amid complex operational stresses. Conventional scheduled strategies often yield inefficiencies, prompting the integration of digital twins (DTs) for enhanced predictive capabilities. This study introduces a comprehensive DT-based aero-engine health management framework, stratified into physical, data, model, and application layers, augmented by a Unity-driven 3D visualization module for immersive virtual-real interactions. The physical layer captures real-time sensor data from turbofan components, feeding into the data layer for preprocessing, fusion, and lifecycle storage. The model layer employs a novel physically decoupled multi- branch long short-term memory (LSTM) network with attention fusion, segmenting sensor features (e.g., temperature, pressure) into specialized branches to discern degradation channels while dynamically weighting salient contributions. Outputs manifest in the application layer via interactive dashboards for RUL fore- casting, anomaly alerts, and maintenance simulations. Validated on the N-CMAPSS DS01 dataset, the model attains an R2 of 0.9376 and RMSE of 5.76 cycles, outperforming single-branch LSTM (R2 = 0.7306) and Transformer (R2 = 0.7810) baselines by leveraging physical priors for superior trend capture and interpretability. Unity integration enables real-time 3D mapping of predictions, fostering user-centric diagnostics. This framework advances PHM by harmonizing deep learning with DTs, boosting accuracy, usability, and decision-making in civil aviation. Limitations in multi-condition scenarios underscore avenues for future uncertainty modeling and lightweight adaptations. Note to Practitioners This digital twin (DT) framework advances aero-engine prognostics and health management (PHM) by delivering interpretable remaining useful life (RUL) predictions to combat inefficiencies in traditional scheduled maintenance, which costs aviation billions in downtime and overhauls. Structured across physical, data, model, and application layers, it processes real- time turbofan sensor data (e.g., temperature, pressure) to support proactive decision-making. The core multi-branch LSTM with attention fusion decouples features by physics (e.g., temperature branches), dynamically weighting degradation signals, such as turbine exhaust, for superior accuracy and transparency. On the N-C MAPSS DS01 dataset, it reaches R2 = 0.9376 and RMSE = 5.76 cycles, surpassing the single-branch LSTM (R2 = 0.7306) and Transformer (R2 = 0.7810) baselines by 20–28%. Benefits include 10-15% longer on-the-wing times and reduced inspections. Unity 3D visualization maps RUL onto interactive models, enabling engineers to identify anomalies, simulate maintenance, and receive alerts, bridging AI to actionable insights without requiring expertise. Deployable via edge cloud for avionics, it scales to variable flight sizes; future work will add uncertainty modeling for noisy data. This empowers predictive stewardship, boosting safety and economics.

title: MFAD: A Multimodal Feature Fusion-Enhanced Time Series Anomaly Detection Framework in Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Silin Peng, Yu Han, Lichen Liu, Jinlong Li, Ruonan Li, Zhaoquan Gu, Jie Liu, Xiaowen Chu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems (ICPS) are increasingly vulnerable to sophisticated attacks and operational disturbances that induce subtle and hard-to-detect anomalies, particularly in industrial edge environments. Existing anomaly detection methods often rely on sufficient labeled data and involve excessive computational overhead, hindering real-time detection and lightweight deployment. To address these challenges, we propose a Multimodal Feature fusion-enhanced time series Anomaly Detection framework (MFAD) in ICPS. MFAD enhances the representation of subtle anomalies by jointly modeling temporal dynamics and industrial characteristics through a unified multimodal feature fusion mechanism. Moreover, MFAD adopts a three-stage detection strategy with adaptive thresholding, which further improves robustness under varying operating conditions, while its lightweight overall architecture supports edge deployment. In addition, we provide the Industrial Gas Cyber-Physical System (IGCPS) dataset collected from real-world industrial operations. Experiments on ICPS benchmark datasets of varying scales, including IGCPS, PUMP, WADI, and SWaT, demonstrate that MFAD achieves an F1 score exceeding 96.7% with efficient resource utilization, validating its effectiveness for real-time detection and lightweight deployment in resource-constrained industrial edge environments. Note to Practitioners—This paper is motivated by the increasing need for reliable and efficient anomaly detection in Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems (ICPS), particularly deployed in resource-constrained industrial edge environments. Existing approaches often treat temporal and industrial features separately, rely on sufficient labeled data, and require substantial computational resources, which limits their applicability in real-world industrial settings. In contrast, the proposed MFAD provides a lightweight and practical solution that integrates multimodal feature fusion with robust semi-supervised detection mechanisms to effectively capture subtle anomalies in time series industrial data. The framework is designed with deployment feasibility that it offers strong detection accuracy, low latency, and efficient resource consumption suitable for industrial edge devices. The methods presented here can inform practitioners seeking to enhance the reliability and real-time performance of ICPS anomaly detection systems. Future extensions may focus on expanding MFAD for broader online industrial applications, integrating it with more edge platforms, and enabling large-scale distributed deployment.

title: A Selective Reprocessing Mechanism to Achieve End-to-End Data Consistency in Streaming Systems
authors: Felipe Cerezo, Carlos E. Cuesta, Belen Vela
abstract: In this work the challenge of end-to-end data consistency is addressed in the context of an industrial real-time Big Data project, as it cannot be achieved solely by using known tools or architectures. The main goal is to guarantee end-to-end consistency in streaming systems, and, to this end, we propose the design and implementation of a specific-purpose selective reprocessing mechanism. This mechanism involves scanning the real-time data stream and tagging data frames to detect both missing and duplicate information, allowing selective reprocessing of only the affected data. By applying this mechanism to data streaming in the context of our project, we have achieved end-to-end consistency in a short time with significantly low resource usage. It also enables us to know at any moment how far we are from achieving end-to-end consistency. Additionally, we have improved the system’s resilience by adapting the circuit breaker pattern to the streaming process. In conclusion, the selective reprocessing mechanism is effective in ensuring end-to-end data consistency in Big Data streaming projects. Resource usage is optimized, and any potential reprocessing is executed within a short time frame.

title: Reciprocity-Based Sensing for Bistatic ISAC
authors: Jinho Choi
abstract: We propose a reciprocity-based bistatic sensing framework for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, exploiting the asymmetry in angular resolvability between spatially separated nodes. By leveraging reciprocal transmissions, the approach enables nodes with limited angular resolution to resolve closely spaced targets, while matched-filter-based delay refinement facilitates reliable target pairing at the central unit (CU). Simulation results show that the reciprocity-based framework effectively suppresses ghost targets, enhances multi-target association, and achieves high-accuracy bistatic localization, even in scenarios with asymmetric node capabilities.

title: Comprehensive Design and Experimental Validation of Modular Multilevel Converter Based on IGCT-IGBT-Parallel Hybrid Switch
authors: Lvyang Chen, Xiangyu Zhang, Zihan Qu, Jiabin Wang, Lin Wang, Jinpeng Wu, Jiapeng Liu, Lei Qi
abstract: Insulated-gate bipolar transistor (IGBT) is widely employed in modular multilevel converters (MMCs) due to excellent switching performance, however, its further application is not satisfying because of large conduction power loss and high cost ratio. Apart from IGBT, recently integrated gate-commutated thyristor (IGCT) has also exhibited desired applicability in MMCs owing to its good conduction properties and small cost. Nevertheless, IGCT’s di/dt capability is limited, thus a clamp circuit is required in the MMC submodule, which will in turn result in increased complexity, power loss and cost. Moreover, its turn-<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small> capability is low, imposing a serious challenge for fault interruption in HIGH-voltage direct current (HVDC) power systems. To enhance the performance of MMCs, the IGCT-IGBT-parallel hybrid switch is introduced to combine the complementary characteristics of IGCT and IGBT, this article develops its comprehensive design method on control implementation and hardware integration. On this basis, prototype platform of the hybrid switch is set up, the 1.2 kV/5 kA and 2 kV/3 kA back-to-back power cycling and fault protection functional tests are conducted to verify its feasibility. By means of an extensive analysis, the hybrid switch is confirmed to show advantages in efficiency, reliability, and economy compared with standalone IGBT and IGCT especially in large-power low-frequency MMC applications.

title: Analysis of Active Damping Methods for Power-Synchronization Dynamics Considering Impacts of Different Inner Loops
authors: Liang Zhao, Xiongfei Wang, Zejie Li
abstract: This article evaluates active damping methods for power-synchronization dynamics in grid-forming (GFM) converters, including frequency-to-active-power (FtP), active-power-to-frequency (PtF), and q-axis-voltage-to-frequency (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">q</sub>tF) controls. Beyond stability-oriented damping analysis, their impacts on inertial response and damping effectiveness under different inner-loop control structures are systematically evaluated. It is revealed that when inner loops employ virtual-admittance-current control and open-loop voltage control, the PtF and V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">q</sub>tF methods share the same damping mechanism and exhibit equivalent characteristics. In contrast, under vector-voltage-control-based inner loops, a tradeoff emerges: the PtF method provides effective damping but prolongs the inertial-response settling time, whereas the V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">q</sub>tF method has a negligible impact on inertial response but offers limited damping in the low-frequency range. To address this tradeoff, a hybrid damping scheme combining PtF and V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">q</sub>tF is proposed, achieving effective damping while preserving inertial response capability. Simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: 400-GHz Concurrent Transceiver Pixels for Short Range FMCW Radar Imaging
authors: Goutham Murugesan, Muhammad Awais, Sarfraz Shariff, Frank Zhang, Wooyeol Choi, Kenneth K. O.
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: A 400-GHz concurrent transceiver imaging pixel with co-optimized output power, locking range (LR), and receiver noise performance is reported. The pixel integrates TX, RX, injection locking circuitry, and patch antenna within a (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda $</tex-math> </inline-formula>/2)<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> area, which makes the pixel more easily scalable to form focal plane arrays. The transceiver pixel is fabricated in the GF 22-nm FDSOI CMOS process with a chip area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$740\times 520$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m and achieves a measured peak effective isotropic radiated power (EIRP) of −14.8 dBm, a measured minimum DSB NF of 33dB, and an LR of 8.5 GHz while consuming only 63.2 mW of DC power at 0.8-V V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DD</sub>. The operation of the pixel with an FMCW injection signal is demonstrated over 8.4 GHz at a ramp rate of 174 MHz/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s. Using a module assembled with a PCB, including a pixel IC and a 6-cm-diameter Cassegrain reflector, radar imaging of a pair of corner reflectors at 1.85 and 2 m away with a cardboard cover is demonstrated. These design and characterization efforts were used to establish a design framework/flow for concurrent transceiver pixels.

title: A Comparative Study on Reaction Times using Visual, Audio, and Haptic Stimuli with Direct, Encoded or Dynamic Deliveries
authors: Fernando Hernandez-Gobertti, Raul Lozano, Ignacio Benito-Frontelo, Jaime Ruiz-Alonso, David Gomez-Barquero
abstract: Reaction times are critical for assessing user response efficacy in applications like virtual environments, assistive technologies, and real-time monitoring. This study examines reaction times across visual, audio, and haptic stimuli, categorized into signal path (direct vs. encoded) and task context (static vs. dynamic), among N = 44 participants. Participants were grouped by demographic factors, including age, gender, and education; and lifestyle factors, including videogame and computer use, and physical exercise. We first analyzed observed reaction times and then derived effective reaction times by subtracting measured processing latencies for each delivery condition. Haptic stimuli yielded the fastest observed responses in the direct and dynamic conditions and comparatively low variability across the full sample. After latency correction, encoded haptic produced the shortest effective reaction times, followed by encoded audio, whereas visual stimuli remained the slowest, particularly in the dynamic condition. Exploratory summaries by demographic and lifestyle subgroup suggested some descriptive differences, but these patterns should be interpreted cautiously because several subgroups were small and imbalanced. Overall, the results support haptic cues as a promising channel for rapid-response applications within the specific end-to-end systems evaluated here, offering valuable insights for designing future technologies.

title: CONVFORMER-LITE: A Lightweight CNN-Transformer Hybrid for Haze/Non-Haze Classification on a Pan-Asian Sentinel-2 True Colour Haze Benchmark Dataset
authors: Balla Pavan Kumar, Aravinth J, Sankaran Rajendran, Arvind Mukundan
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Earth observation plays a crucial role in environmental monitoring, disaster damage assessment, and sustainable resource management, among other applications. These tasks may be affected by atmospheric haze, which reduces the contrast in Sentinel-2 multispectral imagery (MSI) as well as other satellite MSI. Hence, haze detection is essential as an early-stage screening step to further enhance the performance of satellite imagery analysis. This paper presents a fast and reliable network called CONVFORMER-LITE, a lightweight Convolutional–Transformer for binary haze/non-haze image classification on true colour bands of Sentinel-2 MSI. The proposed model combines efficient ConvMixer-based spatial integration with a Safe Multi-Head Self-Attention 2D module, i.e., a token-budgeted multi-head self-attention block that applies full attention when the token count is within a preset budget, and otherwise reduces tokens via pooling (factor <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$p$</tex-math></inline-formula>) before attention and upsamples back to the original spatial size. To enable robust training and evaluation of the proposed network under strong geographic diversity, we introduce a Pan-Asian Sentinel-2 haze/non-haze benchmarking dataset, constructed from 100 locations using a standard physics-guided atmospheric-scattering model with cirrus-band-guided haze thickness control. The proposed <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ConvFormer-Lite</small> achieves near-perfect performance (Accuracy/F1-score <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 0.9998$</tex-math></inline-formula>, Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 1.0$</tex-math></inline-formula>) with 4.68 M parameters and 0.712 G multiply-accumulate operations, which outperforms several recent lightweight baseline models (e.g., <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CoAtNet-Lite</small> and <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EConvNeXt-Lite</small>). The complexity and runtime analyses reveal that <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ConvFormer-Lite</small> offers an excellent trade-off between accuracy and efficiency, which makes it a practical front-end classifier for haze-aware preprocessing in large Sentinel-2 processing pipelines. The implementation of our work is provided at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/kumarballapavan/CFL-H-NH-class-PAS2DB</uri>.

title: Adaptive Fast Clearing Algorithm Based Peer-to-Peer Transaction for Microgrid-integrated Distribution Network Considering Uncertainty
authors: Ye Tian, Yinliang Xu
abstract: Peer-to-peer (P2P) transaction has become an effective way to improve the economic efficiency of microgrids (MGs). However, existing studies neglect the impacts of electricity power-carbon-green certificate join cost (PCGC), energy-reserve co-optimization and uncertainty within MG on P2P transactions schemes. To fill this gap, this paper proposes a P2P transaction framework for interconnected MG, where carbon allowance-green certificate constraints, reserve constraints, joint chance constraints are employed respectively to capture PCGC coupling, reserve provisioning, and uncertainty impacts. An optimization-based piecewise-linear marginal utility function (MUF) is developed to characterize MG's price-responsive behaviors under multi-resource coordination. Furthermore, an adaptive fast clearing algorithm (AFCA) is designed with a network-fee-exempt non-iterative clearing scheme and a network-fee-incorporated oscillation-resistant iterative scheme to ensure computational efficiency and distribution network security. Numerical results demonstrate that the MUF effectively captures the impacts of internal reserve, network constraints, risk preference, and PCGC on inter-MG P2P trading behaviors. The AFCA significantly accelerates computation, with a 97.72% time reduction compared to the widely used alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) in a 5-MG system, and solves the 20-MG system in only 1.45 s with an optimality error of merely 0.59%.
