title: Performance Limits of Hardware-Constrained THz Inter-Satellite MIMO-ISAC Systems
authors: Haofan Dong, Ozgur B. Akan
abstract: Terahertz inter-satellite links (THz-ISL) offer unprecedented bandwidth for future space networks but face fundamental constraints from onboard power and thermal budgets. This paper establishes theoretical performance limits for MIMO Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems under per-element constant-envelope (CE) transmission constraints. We demonstrate that hardware distortions, specifically power amplifier nonlinearity, ADC quantization, and oscillator phase noise, impose an achievable-rate ceiling that cannot be overcome by increasing transmit power. A unified link budget framework integrates wideband beam squint, aperture pointing errors, and colored noise sources through a spectral consistency principle that ensures uncompensated phase noise is counted exactly once across communication and sensing analyses. The sensing bounds are derived via the Whittle-Fisher Information Matrix under a Constant Acceleration kinematic model with jerk noise, yielding closed-form scaling laws: uncompensated phase noise variance scales as α−1 while dynamic state-estimation error (DSE) variance scales as α−5 with pilot overhead α. Numerical results show divergent MIMO scaling: sensing precision improves with array size (RMSE ∝ 1/ √ NtNr), while the critical SNR exhibits scale invariance regarding array size, implying that the distortion-limited transition point stabilizes regardless of the array scale. The steep α−5 DSE scaling creates an operationally infeasible region at α < α∗ ≈ 0.10, where α∗ = (CDSE/CPN)1/4, a constraint-driven threshold under the adopted baseline for LEO operation. These findings provide design guidelines for hardware-efficient THz-ISL constellations.

title: Conceptualization of Multipurpose Testing Facility for Advanced Fluid Dynamics Investigations in Tokamak Divertor Modules
authors: Davide Caprini, Pierdomenico Lorusso, Mariano Di Bartolomeo, Selanna Roccella
abstract: A novel testing facility is being conceptualized to experimentally support the development of prototypical components for divertors in tokamak fusion reactors. The facility will be conceived to operate in a wide range of operating conditions, with the capability to reach high pressure (up to 150bar) and high temperature (up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$250~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C) conditions, enabling rigorous testing of the fluid dynamics and mechanical behavior of components. The primary focus of the facility will be the investigation of fluid dynamics, particularly around swirl flows and within the targets of plasma-facing units (PFUs). With the use of advanced diagnostic tools, including tomographic particle image velocimetry (Tomo-PIV) system, the facility will enable in-depth analysis of fluid flow behaviors. This will provide valuable insights into how fluids interact with complex geometries, helping to optimize the thermo-fluid dynamic performance of fusion reactor in-vessel components. In addition, the facility will support experimental campaigns focused on vibration and fluid–structure interaction (FSI) analysis. Flow-induced vibrations are critical factors that could potentially limit the service life of key in-vessel components in a nuclear fusion reactor, particularly those involved in the heat extraction system (e.g., the first wall, divertor, etc.). By employing high-sensitivity measurement systems, both contact (accelerometers) and noncontact laser Doppler vibrometer (LDV), the facility will enable the measurement and subsequent analysis of the vibrational spectrum and the dynamic interaction between fluids and solid structures (e.g., pipe-fluid-swirl) under various operating conditions, as well as the vibrational characterization of the components through experimental and operational modal analysis (EMA and OMA). These insights will enhance understanding of the mechanical stability and dynamic response of the components, contributing to their reliability and longevity. With its combination of cutting-edge diagnostic tools and experimental versatility, the facility will play a pivotal role in advancing research in fluid dynamics and structural mechanics for fusion technologies. The integration of these experimental techniques into a unified platform will foster interdisciplinary collaboration and enhance the efficiency of research efforts.

title: SAFA: Sequential Recommendation with Adaptive Sparse Attention and Frequency-Aware Encoding
authors: Wenming Cao, Hongjun Liu, Xujun Yang, Bing Li, Lu Wang, Zhiwen Yu, Man-Fai Leung
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Recommendation systems alleviate the issue of information overload via modeling user preferences from interaction sequences. Although self-attention based sequential models effectively capture long-range dependencies, they are susceptible to noise amplification in sparse sequences and over-smoothing of item representations, which obscures true user intent and reduces sensitivity to fine-grained behavioral changes. To overcome these challenges, we propose SAFA, a sparse sequential recommendation framework comprising: (1) an adaptive sparse attention mechanism that suppresses noisy interactions while preserving embedding diversity; (2) a frequency-aware encoder that decomposes interaction sequences into low-frequency components for long-term preference modeling and high-frequency components for short-term intent dynamics; and (3) a simplified focal loss that removes the class-balancing term while preserving the focusing factor, emphasizing hard-to-predict samples rather than class priors. Experiments on seven benchmark datasets demonstrate that SAFA consistently achieve state-of-the-art performance with average improvements of up to 3.77%, 4.10% and 4.25% in terms of HR@5, HR@10 and HR@20, respectively, and 4.30%, 4.78% and 4.58% in terms of NDCG@5, NDCG@10 and NDCG@20, respectively, over the best competing model. Ablation studies verify the importance of each component, with notable performance degradation upon removing the sparse attention or frequency-aware encoder. Overall, SAFA enhances sequential recommendation by improving robustness and discriminative learning under noisy and sparse conditions.

title: Energy-Efficient Federated Learning for IoT Networks With Massive MIMO-Enabled SWIPT
authors: Mohammad Mozafari, Maryam Hosseini, Pouya Hosseini, Abdulhamid Zahedi, Jamshid Abouei, Arash Mohammadi
abstract: This paper investigates energy-efficient federated learning (FL) in multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) edge-enabled Internet of Things (IoT) networks, where user equipments (UEs) are enabled with simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) capabilities. To jointly optimize communication and computation resources, a hierarchical optimization framework is proposed to minimize the total effective energy consumption per global FL round, while satisfying latency, power, frequency, power-splitting, and local accuracy constraints. By exploiting the time-scale separation between wireless resource allocation and learning accuracy adaptation, the resulting non-convex problem is decomposed into a short-term convexified subproblem for communication and computation resource optimization, solved via successive convex approximation (SCA), and a long-term subproblem for local accuracy updates, addressed using coordinate descent (CD). The proposed algorithm ensures convergence to a stationary solution with polynomial complexity, achieving significant computational savings compared to exhaustive search. Simulation results verify that the proposed framework achieves substantial energy consumption reduction and faster convergence compared with the benchmark schemes. Furthermore, results demonstrate that increasing the base station (BS) antenna array or the energy harvesting efficiency enhances the network sustainability and scalability of FL for energy-constrained IoT devices.

title: A Sensitive and Selective QCM Sensor Functionalized with Chitosan Biopolymer for the Detection of Linalool in Organic Sikkim Teas
authors: Rajiv Pradhan, Subhojit Malik, Amrik Basak, Subhajit Roy, Sumana Banerjee, Prolay Sharma
abstract: In this work, a facile and environmentally benign chitosan-functionalized Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) sensor is developed for sensitive Linalool detection in organic Sikkim teas. The proposed sensor exhibits a sensitivity of 1.2903 Hz·ppm⁻¹, with excellent linearity (R² = 0.991) over a concentration range of 10–1000 ppm, representing a measurable improvement over several previously reported polymer- and MOF-based QCM systems. A maximum steady-state frequency shift of −1411 Hz at 1000 ppm is observed, along with preferential selectivity toward Linalool attributed to specific analyte–film interactions. Dynamic response measurements indicate rapid and reversible sensing behavior. Repeatability, reproducibility and long-term stability studies demonstrate consistent sensor performance, including stable operation under periodic 300 ppm exposures over a six-month duration. Surface morphology and chemical functionality of the coating are confirmed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, supporting the proposed sensing mechanism. Real-sample analysis further demonstrates practical applicability, with QCM responses showing strong correlation with GC–MS results (R² = 0.9820) for Sikkim tea samples. These results highlight chitosan as an effective green coating material for reliable and sensitive QCM-based Linalool sensing.

title: Visual Environment Semantic Sensing Assisted OTFS Channel Estimation
authors: Weihao Wang, Jing Guo, Jingxuan Huang, Zesong Fei, Weijie Yuan, Tony Q. S. Quek
abstract: To meet the growing demand for communication capacity and address the scarcity of wireless spectrum resources, we propose a novel orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) channel estimation scheme enhanced by visual environment semantics. This approach establishes a theoretical foundation for semantic-assisted channel estimation by modeling the potential relationship between the wireless communication channel and its surrounding environment. Leveraging a computer vision-based adaptive environment semantic sensing framework, the system extracts and processes environment features to infer channel characteristics. To tackle the challenge of capturing small-scale fading solely through visual environment semantics, we design two new pilot structures and the corresponding channel estimation methods. These are tailored to maximize the utility of information derived from the environment while minimizing pilot overhead. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme outperforms the conventional channel estimation method in terms of spectral efficiency and robustness in high-mobility and low-SNR scenarios with fewer pilot symbols.

title: Design and Control of an Electromechanical Human&#x2013;Robotic Manipulator&#x2019;s Interface for Upper-Limb Rehabilitation_supp1-3684722.mp4
authors: ANDRE GONCALVES

title: Context-Aware Semantic Video Coding Using Pretrained Motion-Adaptive Models and Bidirectional Prediction
authors: Prabhath Samarathunga, Yasith Ganearachchi, Thanuj Fernando, Adhuran Jayasingam, Thushan Sivalingam, Nandana Rajatheva, Anil Fernando
abstract: The rapid growth of video traffic and high-resolution real-time services exposes fundamental limitations in both conventional hybrid codecs and fully learned video compression methods, particularly in scalability, computational efficiency, and temporal modeling capability. Existing semantic video coding approaches partially address these challenges by combining learned representations with standardized residual coding. However, their reliance on per-group-of-pictures online training and simplistic temporal prediction limits practical deployment and generalization. This paper proposes a context-conditioned semantic video coding framework that fundamentally redefines hybrid semantic coding by eliminating per-group-of-pictures training through a shared pretrained foundation model with motion-adaptive specialization. A hierarchical bidirectional prediction architecture is introduced to explicitly model long-range temporal dependencies, while motion-aligned contextual references generated via bidirectional frame interpolation provide semantic guidance for reconstruction. This context-conditioned decoding mechanism reduces ambiguity in latent interpretation and significantly improves temporal coherence and prediction accuracy. To further enhance coding efficiency, a differential semantic latent representation is proposed and entropy-coded using Deep Context-Adaptive Binary Arithmetic Coding, while a Versatile Video Coding-based residual pathway guarantees reconstruction fidelity. The overall system is formulated within a unified context-conditioned rate–distortion optimization framework, bridging semantic and conventional coding paradigms. Comprehensive evaluations across diverse benchmark datasets demonstrate consistent and significant rate–distortion gains, achieving an average Bjøntegaard delta rate reduction of 22.03% over the Versatile Video Coding Test Model and competitive performance relative to state-of-the-art learned codecs. These results establish context-conditioned semantic coding with pretrained models and hierarchical bidirectional prediction as a scalable and deployable direction for next-generation video compression.

title: Offline-Learned Visual Encoder for Privileged-to-Real Bipedal Locomotion
authors: Yan Luo, Liping Chen, Yuhao Song, Huang Chen, Jianwan Ding
abstract: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning framework for vision-based locomotion control of bipedal robots on complex, unstructured terrains, integrating privileged perception transfer, offline Transformer-based depth reconstruction, and historical observation encoding. During training, the critic utilizes dense privileged height samples to improve value estimation accuracy, while the actor operates on sparse observations consistent with the deployment phase, ensuring stable policy transfer from training to real-world application. The offline visual module converts depth images into structured height representations, significantly alleviating the dependence of online training on high simulation parallelism and computational resources. The historical encoder further enhances temporal modeling capabilities for dynamic events such as terrain transitions and contact delays. Validation results on the MuJoCo simulation platform and the TRON1 hardware platform demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing end-to-end visual policies and proprioception-based strategies across multiple performance metrics, exhibiting superior stability and adaptability. This framework offers a scalable new paradigm for achieving efficient and robust vision-based locomotion control without requiring high-precision localization or calibration.

title: Towards adaptive optimal personalization control of robotic hip exoskeleton assistance for individuals with paretic stroke_supp1-3685273.pdf
authors: Qiang Zhang
abstract: Neurological disorders, like stroke, can re duce the mobility and quality of life for millions of indi viduals across the world. As promising gait assistive de vices, powered lower-limb exoskeletons have gained wide attention for their potential to improve walking function for individuals with mobility impairments. However, due to the complex human-robot interaction, highly nonlinear dynamics modeling, and person-to-person variability, it is challenging to provide optimal customized assistance from these exoskeletons. In the current study, we showed that a reinforcement learning (RL)-based control framework pro vided adaptive and optimal personalized assistance across the hip joint to improve walking function for individuals with and without mobility deficits. We selected hip kinematic spatial gait symmetry as the control objective for five par ticipants with hemiparetic stroke, and found three optimal control parameters over an average of 201.7±36.1 sec. The kinematic spatial gait symmetry was defined as the joint excursion symmetry on both hip joints within the same gait cycle. By adding optimal assistance, we observed that the joint excursion during gait cycles at the affected hip joint was increased from 37.09° to 48.03° by 29.5% (p = 0.003) and the joint excursion gait symmetry was improved from 7.55% to-0.63% (p = 0.002) compared to transparent mode (no assistance). The findings suggest that the application of an RL-based assistance personalization control scheme to a wearable hip exoskeleton may positively impact gait rehabilitation training outcomes in a clinical population.

title: Suitability of Hollow-Core Fibre for Data-and-Power-Over-Fibre Systems
authors: Douglas McCulloch, Kyle Bottrill, Yongmin Jung, Gregory T. Jasion, Hesham Sakr, Francesco Poletti, Periklis Petropoulos
abstract: Power-over-fibre (PoF) can improve future network resilience by simultaneously delivering power and connectivity to remote equipment in situations where a supply based on copper cables is neither feasible nor appropriate. However, in standard silica fibres, the high optical powers have an impact on data signals due to nonlinearities, decreasing maximum data rates. Hollow-core fibre (HCF) as a transmission medium has a marked decrease in nonlinearity, and its applicability to PoF is studied here. Two experiments using 3.1 and 1.2km lengths of HCF are conducted to investigate the benefits of this fibre as a medium to carry high-power optical signals alongside high-throughput data signals. The study of diverse wavelengths highlights the versatility of HCF for power transmission.

title: Quantum-Assisted Multi-Output Gaussian Processes for Line Parameter Estimation in Power Systems
authors: Priyanka Arkalgud Ganeshamurthy, Kumar Ghosh, Corey O’ Meara, Giorgio Cortiana, Jan Schiefelbein-Lach, Antonello Monti, Ferdinanda Ponci
abstract: A Gaussian process (GP) is a powerful modeling method with applications in machine learning for various engineering and non-engineering fields. Despite numerous benefits of modeling using GPs, the computational complexity associated with GPs demanding immense resources make their practical usage highly challenging. In this article, a proof-of-concept demonstration of quantum-assisted multi-output Gaussian Process regression is presented, where the kernel matrix inversion during the training phase of GP is replaced by the Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd (HHL) algorithm, a well-known quantum algorithm for solving linear systems. The presented approach uses classical kernels, but employs quantum computing for the computationally expensive matrix inversion operations. To reduce the large circuit depth of HHL, matrix conditioning and a circuit optimization technique called Approximate Quantum Compiling (AQC) is implemented, which reduces the circuit depth from O(107) gates to approximately 250 gates. The quantum-assisted Gaussian process is then utilized for implementation of line parameter of electrical grids, a real-world problem adapted from our previous work. Using AQC, up to 13-qubit HHL circuit has been implemented for a 32×32 kernel matrix inversion on IBM Quantum hardware (IBM Auckland). The performance of quantum hardware implementation is compared against noiseless quantum simulators and classical computation results. Through this work the feasibility of implementing quantum-assisted GP on current Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) hardware is demonstrated and key bottlenecks for future improvements are identified. To the best of authors’ knowledge, this is one of the largest experimental implementations involving HHL algorithm for power system applications on IBM hardware.

title: An Integrated Transmission Scheme for One-Time Pad Encryption and Secret Key Generation
authors: Zheyuan Deng, Liang Jin, Xiaoyan Hu, Xiaoming Xu, Na Wei, Yuqing Liu
abstract: Traditional physical-layer secret key generation (PLSKG) schemes for the One-Time Pad (OTP) rely on a time-sharing approach, where key generation and OTP communication are separated, resulting in significant time overhead and low efficiency. To address this limitation, this letter proposes an integrated scheme, named PLSKG-OTP, that enables simultaneous key generation and data transmission by introducing random perturbations within the decision regions of the received constellation. To counter a powerful eavesdropper with prior knowledge, a composite security scheme is designed: artificial noise (AN) protects each raw key, while a cryptographic hash function (CHF) accumulates entropy across blocks, ensuring the asymptotic zero leakage security of the final encryption key. Simulations show the scheme reduces time cost to 26% of benchmarks and fulfills OTP requirements.

title: Remote Sensing Semantic Change Detection with Multi-dimensional Spatiotemporal Scanning
authors: Zhong Chen, Hanruo Chen, Haodong Mu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing semantic change detection (RSSCD), which identifies pixel-level ground semantic changes by analyzing multi-temporal data, is widely used in fields such as urban planning and disaster monitoring. However, Most of the current datasets only contain two temporal phases, which provide insufficient information, leading to overlook areas with subtle changes and mistakenly detect non-change areas with significant differences. Moreover, changes with diverse scales and directional distributions make it difficult to localize them accurately. Besides, class imbalance further degrades detection performance. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel Mamba-based multi-dimensional spatiotemporal scanning method. Firstly, temporal interpolation is applied to bi-temporal images, enabling high-dimensional spatiotemporal feature extraction through global interaction with linear complexity. Secondly, the shallow feature extraction module in the above model is improved and a set of diagonal scanning directions are added to better capture multi-scale and omnidirectional changes. Finally, a Consistency Dynamic Weighted Loss(CDW Loss) is introduced to mitigate class imbalance. The proposed method achieves significant improvements in the Separated Kappa (SeK) metric, a comprehensive indicator for semantic change detection, outperforming the current state-of-the-art method(ChangeMamba) by 17.67%, 4.54% and 20.80% on the Landsat-SCD, SECOND, and Hi-UCD datasets respectively, in the absence of the proposed CDW Loss.

title: Antiferroelectric-based Memcapacitor and Strategy for Non-destructive Read Operation by Endurance Recovery
authors: C.-H. Liu, K.-Y. Hsiang, F.-S. Chang, M. Januar, Y.-T. Chang, C. W. Liu, T.-H. Hou, M. H. Lee
abstract: The proposed antiferroelectric capacitive memory (AFE-FCM) achieves a symmetric capacitive memory window (CMW) of ~ 6×ε0 with non-destructive readout (NDR) at 0V. The asymmetric capacitance loop (ACL) operation achieves CMW with reduced drive voltage range -1.5V to +3V or -3V to +1.5V. The sensing circuit of FCM verifies a significant improvement in sensing voltage for AFE compared to FE. The read endurance with NDR remains beyond 10<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">9</sub> cycling. The NDR characteristic gradually transitions to destructive readout (DR) due to the program/erase endurance, which accumulates the imprint effect. The strategy will be proposed to eliminate DR and prolong endurance through a recovery scheme, with a 100-period demonstration. The proposed AFE-FCM boasts promising metrics, including high CMW, NDR, long endurance, a crossbar architecture, a simple structure, and treatment-free capabilities.

title: Effect of DC Superimposed Opposite-polarity Lightning Impulse on Insulation Performance of XLPE Cables
authors: Chengde Liu, Hang Su, Su Zhao, Bin Feng, Xiaolei Zhao, Shuai Hou, Yalin Wang, Mingli Fu, Yi Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: To investigate the effect of DC superimposed with opposite-polarity lightning impulse on the insulation performance of cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) cable, this paper built a dedicated experimental platform. Based on maintaining a stable DC voltage on XLPE film samples, opposite-polarity lightning impulses with different numbers of applications were applied. The results show that as the number of impulses increases, the DC breakdown strength of XLPE insulation decreases significantly. After 300 consecutive impulses, the DC breakdown strength decreases by 48% compared to that without impulse, and the dispersion increases. Isothermal relaxation current (IRC) tests indicate that as the number of impulses increases, the trap density of the material increases significantly, and the trap energy level deepens continuously. Space charge measurements further show that the dynamic behavior of space charge inside the material becomes more complex after multiple impulses. To reveal the physicochemical mechanism of the effect of impulse number on the dielectric properties of XLPE insulation, multi-scale characterization of the samples was carried out using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The results show that as the number of impulses increases, the initial thermal decomposition temperature and crystallinity of XLPE insulation decrease significantly, while the intensity of characteristic absorption peaks of aging functional groups such as carbonyl groups in the infrared spectrum continues to increase. Comprehensive analysis reveals that lightning impulses cause XLPE molecular chain breakage accompanied by generation of small molecular fragments. Meanwhile, the polarization loss caused by the periodic reversal of the electric field during the impulse process converts into thermal effects, thereby accelerating the thermal-oxidative aging reaction and promoting the generation of more aging products. Pearson correlation analysis further confirms a strong correlation between the evolution of trap characteristics and the degradation of breakdown performance, thus establishing a direct quantitative relationship between microstructural damage and macroscopic performance deterioration, and profoundly revealing the influence mechanism of the impulse accumulation effect on dielectric properties.

This article overviews the holistic approach needed to successfully develop insulation materials by examining specific insulation systems from recent research.

title: Structural Connectivity Modeling with Graph Curvature Features for Brain Tumor Analysis
authors: Suman Rekha Dip, Hemant Kumar Meena
abstract: This work presents a low-power FPGA-based biomedical sensing system for early detection of brain tumors using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). While MRI is a key medical sensing modality, existing deep learning–based diagnostic methods are computationally intensive and unsuitable for real-time, resource-constrained sensor platforms. To address this challenge, a graph signal processing (GSP) framework is proposed that models MRI pixel intensities as graph signals, enabling effective representation of the non-Euclidean spatial connectivity inherent to the MRI sensing process. Ricci curvature is employed to characterize local structural connectivity variations and tumor-induced asymmetries through curvature-driven graph features. The proposed sensor-aware framework is explicitly designed for edge-deployed MRI sensing systems and is implemented on a PYNQ-ZU FPGA platform to demonstrate measurable improvements in sensing-system efficiency. Experimental results on the BR35H and Kaggle-253 datasets achieve classification accuracies of 96.50% and 91.94%, respectively. The system requires only 0.0011 million parameters, achieves an inference latency of 3.85 s per image, and consumes 0.33 J per inference, demonstrating its suitability for real-time, energy-efficient medical sensing applications.

title: Spatial Downscaling of MODIS Land Surface Temperature Based on a Geographically Weighted Gradient Boosting Regression Model
authors: Mengyu Liu, Sheng Chang, Jie Cheng, Hong Chen, Jinlong Fan, Chunyan Zhang, Peng He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: As a fundamental indicator of surface thermal conditions, land surface temperature (LST) provides essential information for characterizing land–atmosphere exchanges and surface energy dynamics. Owing to sensor design constraints, satellite observations with high temporal resolution are typically limited to coarse spatial resolutions, making spatial downscaling of LST products essential for capturing fine-scale thermal heterogeneity and supporting detailed thermal environment analyses. In this study, a geographically weighted gradient boosting regression (GWRBoost) model is developed by integrating geographically weighted regression (GWR) with gradient boosting to explicitly characterize spatial nonstationarity and complex nonlinear interactions between LST and environmental factors. The normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI), normalized difference water index (NDWI), and digital elevation model (DEM) were incorporated to generate used as predictors to downscale high-resolution LST with the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) LST from a spatial resolution of 990 m to 90 m, with Landsat 8 LST serving as reference observations for validation. The proposed GWRBoost model was evaluated against the thermal data sharpening (TsHARP) method and the conventional GWR model. The results indicate that the GWRBoost model outperforms the comparative approaches, achieving an RMSE of 2.07 K and an MAE of 1.75 K. Additional site-based validation further demonstrates the stability and consistency of the LST downscaling outputs produced by the proposed model.

title: Hey, Robot! Don't Cut in Line: Designing Queuing Behaviors of Delivery Robots with Multiple Stakeholders
authors: Nayoung Kim, Hyochang Kim, Sunok Lee, Changjoo Nam
abstract: In situations where a delivery robot shares limited resources with people who are incidentally copresent in the same space, its behavior affects both those individuals and customers awaiting delivery. In this paper, we refer to such individuals as incidentally copresent persons (InCops). Most prior studies focus on a single stakeholder, leaving a gap for deployment in shared environments. We examined how robot behavior influences customers' perceived waiting time and the impressions formed by both InCops and customers, using a multi-stakeholder perspective. A key scenario involves a robot and an InCop meeting at an elevator door, where the robot performs a queuing behavior. We compared yielding, cutting in line, and first-in-first-out, along with four information types shown to customers. User journey maps captured qualitative insights. Results revealed that preferences for robot queuing behavior differed by stakeholder, with InCops responding primarily to norm compliance and customers relying more on system transparency to interpret waiting experiences. These findings suggest that socially aligned, humble conduct and visible intent are critical for delivery robots operating in resource-limited environments that balance fairness with efficiency.

title: Signal Processing Approach for LO Leakage Cancellation of IQ Modulators in a Wideband Stretch Processing SAR Sensor
authors: Ali Imanifar, Sadegh Samadi
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) is a remote sensing system capable of providing high-resolution imagery. Among the prominent features of this sensor is its ability to image under various weather conditions and throughout the day and night. These radar systems require extremely high bandwidth to achieve high-resolution imaging. Due to the limitations of digital components, the single-tone local oscillator structure in the receiver section has been replaced with the stretch processing structure. However, despite this advancement, the challenge of generating the high-bandwidth signal in the transmitter section remains. A common approach to increase transmission bandwidth is the combination of the real and imaginary part signals using IQ modulators. However, the use of IQ modulator introduces a number of challenges due to its LO (Local Oscillator) leakage, which increases noise level and creates false targets in the final image. This paper proposes a novel signal processing approach to address LO leakage cancellation of IQ modulators in a wideband stretch processing SAR system. This method achieves high accuracy and eliminates the need for additional RF circuits. In this approach, the leakage of receiver and transmitter channels are suppressed separately with a cancellation method using a matched filter within the stretch processing structure. The results obtained from simulated and real data experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively eliminates LO leakage effects of IQ modulators in wideband SAR sensor images.

title: Extraction of Equivalent Circuit Parameters for the Organic Photodetectors Based on Physical Model of Frequency-Dependent Capacitance–Conductance–Voltage Characteristics
authors: Sai Liu, Shun-Ye Yu, Rui-Peng Chen, Jia-Jie Liu, Miao Xu, Lei Wang, Wei-Jing Wu, Jun-Biao Peng
abstract: We propose a small-signal admittance model for the trap states of the organic photodetectors (OPDs). Unlike conventional models of the inorganic devices, our approach introduces a trap-related RC branch, enabling the effective decoupling of intrinsic, trap capacitances and the extraction of equivalent circuit parameters such as capacitance, conductance, and relaxation time of the trap. Experimental results on the PM6:Y6-based OPDs confirm that the model accurately predicts the capacitance and conductance over a wide range of biases and different frequencies, while traditional depletion-based models fail to predict the frequency-dependent behavior. The analysis indicates that trap capacitance dominates the low-frequency response and gradually decreases with increasing reverse bias due to trap filling, whereas the intrinsic capacitance remains nearly bias-independent as an inherent structural property. Beyond providing physical insight into the trap states, this model serves as a versatile tool for designing the high-speed and low-noise OPDs, with potential extensions to other organic and inorganic semiconductor devices in optical communication and biosensing systems.

title: HCSGRN: Hierarchical Cross-Attention and Structure-aware Graph Reasoning Network for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
authors: Chunlan Zhan, Wenhua Qian, Peng Liu, Hongli Wang, Shuang Luo
abstract: With the proliferation of user-generated multimodal content on consumer electronics platforms, including mobile applications and streaming media interfaces, accurately interpreting user sentiment, especially sarcastic intent, has become increasingly important. Multimodal sarcasm detection aims to identify sarcasm by capturing inconsistencies between textual and visual information, but semantic conflicts across modalities and overlooked unimodal cues remain major challenges. Existing methods achieve limited cross-modal fusion and often fail to capture subtle incongruities, restricting their ability to detect nuanced sarcasm. To address these challenges, we propose a Hierarchical Cross-Attention and Structure-aware Graph Reasoning Network (HCSGRN). Specifically, we first generate image captions using accompanying text to enhance sentiment cues and enrich visual semantics. To capture cross-modal consistency and inconsistency, we design a Hierarchical Cross-Guided Attention Module that integrates low- and high-level text features for semantic alignment and fine-grained interaction. Furthermore, we propose a Structure-aware Graph Reasoning Module, which constructs a graph based on negative similarity weights to model latent structural relationships and implicit semantic conflicts between text and image. Extensive experiments on two multi-modal sarcasm detection datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance, highlighting its potential for sentiment understanding, recommendation, and human–device interaction in consumer electronics systems.

title: Camera-Photodiode Fusion Based Cooperative Localization Algorithm for Multi-User Visible Light Positioning
authors: Yulan He, Xu Bao, Muyu Mei, Wence Zhang
abstract: Visible light positioning (VLP) enables high-precision indoor localization, but single-sensor systems do not scale well to multi-user settings: camera-based methods suffer from motion jitter, while photodiode (PD)-based approaches are vulnerable to ambient light and multipath effects. This paper proposes a camera-photodiode fusion-based cooperative localization algorithm (C-CPD): cameras extract geometric features of a circular luminaire for coarse positioning, and PD-light-emitting diode (LED) modules measure distances to cooperative visible light communication (VLC) units via received signal strength (RSS) to refine estimates, mitigating motion distortions. We derive the Cram´er-Rao lower bound (CRLB) to characterize performance, revealing impacts of focal length, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and cooperative VLC units. Simulations show 0.41 cm average error, and experiments demonstrate 3.24 cm real-world error, outperforming baselines in dynamic scenarios. This work highlights multi-sensor fusion and geometric feature exploitation for robust, high-accuracy VLP, with CRLB insights guiding system design.

title: Collaborative Multimodal Learning over Integrated Aerial-Terrestrial Networks under Adversarial Attacks
authors: Shaba Shaon, Dinh C. Nguyen, Dusit Niyato, H. Vincent Poor
abstract: With the rapid growth of intelligent aerial-terrestrial applications, enabling collaborative multimodal learning (CML) across heterogeneous data sources, such as aerial images from unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and time-series signals from ground edge devices (EDs), has become essential for achieving reliable intelligence beyond unimodal approaches. However, aerial-terrestrial CML systems face stringent latency requirements, limited energy and computation resources, and vulnerability to adversarial attacks, which are not jointly addressed in existing studies. This paper proposes a wireless aerial-terrestrial CML framework that integrates distributed UAVs and terrestrial EDs with modality-specific encoder training and multimodal fusion at a ground base station (BS). We formulate a latency minimization problem under energy, and security-aware constraints by jointly optimizing UAV trajectories and resource allocation, ED resource allocation, as well as resource allocation of the BS. The framework explicitly incorporates both passive eavesdropping and active interference attacks to ensure secure and robust aerial-terrestrial CML operation. To solve the resulting non-convex latency minimization problem, we develop a simple yet efficient iterative optimization algorithm to find a high-quality optimal solution based on successive convex approximation. Extensive simulation results with real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms existing training methods in terms of accuracy, loss, and convergence. Moreover, our joint optimization framework achieves up to 94.05% lower latency and stronger robustness against aerial adversaries compared with baseline schemes.

title: Admittance-Based Aggregated Modeling of Distributed Energy Resources With Proportional-Resonant Control for Large-Signal Transient Analysis in Nodal-Based EMT Simulators
authors: Arash Safavizadeh, Rahul Raman Ramesh, Seyyedmilad Ebrahimi, Juri Jatskevich
abstract: Distributed energy resources are increasingly integrated into power systems via power electronic converters, creating the need for more efficient electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations. This paper proposes a direct interfacing method for admittance-based aggregated models (ABAMs) of grid-following converter-interfaced resources (CIRs) with proportional-resonant (PR) control, aiming to enhance the efficiency of large-signal transient simulations in nodal-based EMT programs. The transfer-function-based Norton models are first developed for CIRs with heterogeneous parameters/ratings that are diversely distributed across the power grid. Then, Norton/Thévenin transformations are applied to combine them with collector lines and the impedance/admittance-based models of system components, such as loads. The aggregated transfer functions, represented as a Norton equivalent, are discretized and integrated into the overall network nodal equations to enable simultaneous solution. The proposed directly interfaced ABAM (DI-ABAM) uses a constant-conductance matrix, reducing computational cost and enabling the use of large simulation time-step sizes while maintaining high accuracy. The benefits of the proposed DI-ABAM are demonstrated through transient simulations in both offline (PSCAD) and real-time (RTDS) simulators.

title: Joint Quality Assessment and Example-Guided Tone Mapping by Disentangling Picture Appearance from Content
authors: Abhinau K. Venkataramanan, Cosmin Stejerean, Ioannis Katsavounidis, Hassene Tmar, Alan C. Bovik
abstract: The deep learning revolution has strongly impacted low-level image processing tasks such as style/domain transfer, enhancement/restoration, and visual quality assessments. Despite often being treated separately, the aforementioned tasks share a common theme of understanding, editing, or enhancing the appearance of input images without modifying the underlying content. We leverage this observation to develop a novel disentangled representation learning method that decomposes inputs into content and appearance features. The model is trained in a self-supervised manner and we use the learned features to develop a new quality prediction model named DisQUE. We demonstrate through extensive evaluations that DisQUE achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across quality prediction tasks and distortion types. Moreover, we demonstrate that the same features may also be used for image processing tasks such as HDR tone mapping, where the desired output characteristics may be tuned using example input-output pairs.

title: TNT: Breaking the Privacy Barrier of Federated Learning via Targeted Neuron Stimulation
authors: Wanqi Zhang, Kanye Ye Wang, Xiaobo Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Federated learning supports decentralized model training while preserving user privacy, yet shared gradients contain sensitive information that exposes systems to privacy attacks. Among these, data reconstruction attacks, where adversaries recover training samples from gradients, pose critical threats. However, existing methods are often limited to small batch sizes and rely on activation patterns that rarely occur naturally, reducing their practical applicability. To address these challenges, we propose a novel attack framework called Targeted Neuron Stimulation (TNT), which systematically generates adversarial perturbations during training to enforce activation patterns conducive to effective data reconstruction. TNT solves a constrained optimization problem to inject subtle, imperceptible noise into target training batches, enabling an honest-but-curious server to extract sensitive data from client gradients with high fidelity. Through extensive evaluations across five datasets, including facial recognition and medical diagnosis, TNT expands reconstructable batch sizes from 8 to realistic levels and accelerates attack processes by up to 10 times, achieving significantly higher reconstruction fidelity compared to existing methods. By exploring latent vulnerabilities in gradient-sharing mechanisms, this work highlights the privacy risks inherent to federated learning systems and calls for stronger defenses to safeguard sensitive user data.

title: Symptom-Aware Adaptation of CLIP for Few-Shot Medical Image Classification_supp1-3685174.pdf
authors: Wenxu Wang
abstract: Vision-language pre-training model yields powerful general-purpose representations, yet its direct application to medical images is hindered by two key gaps: (i) low sensitivity to fine-grained pathological cues and (ii) semantic misalignment with structured clinical terminology. We propose SITSA, a framework that adapts a frozen CLIP backbone for data-scarce medical tasks without full fine-tuning. SITSA introduces three synergistic components: (1) MedVisExtractor, a depthwise-separable convolutional adapter that recalibrates patch tokens to emphasize subtle lesions; (2) SymSemLoRA, which injects low-rank updates into the text encoder conditioned on large-language-model-generated prompts that encode texture, location, and shape attributes; and (3) Prototype-Guided Alignment, a cross-modal module that binds image patches and prompt tokens via shared learnable prototypes. Extensive experiments on COVID-QU-Ex, OCTMNIST, and DERMAMNIST demonstrate that SITSA surpasses thirteen state-of-the-art baselines in both few-shot and base-to-novel generalization, achieving the highest harmonic mean accuracy while keeping the backbone frozen. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/enfantsRichesDeprimes/sitsa</uri>.

title: NDVI Change-Guided Transformer-Mamba Hybrid Network for Spatiotemporal Fusion
authors: Yuyun Liang, Zhaozhao Zeng, Fan Li, Jun Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: High spatiotemporal resolution imagery is essential for dynamic earth observation. Efficiently fusing multisource imagery to obtain high spatial and temporal resolution surface information has become a core research problem in the field of remote sensing. However, existing spatiotemporal fusion methods have limited ability to model phenological dynamics and land cover changes across both short and long temporal intervals, resulting in suboptimal fusion performance under complex surface change conditions. To address this problem, this paper proposes an innovative dual-input normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) change-guided spatiotemporal fusion model, termed NDVI change-guided Transformer-Mamba hybrid network for spatiotemporal fusion (NCTM-STF). An NDVI change-guided feature enhancement module (NFEM) was constructed based on the Swin Transformer architecture, explicitly incorporating NDVI change information into the attention mechanism. Then, combined with a differential feature extraction module (DFEM) also constructed based on the Swin Transformer architecture, the proposed model could automatically focus on change regions while extracting differential features, thus enhancing the characterization of both short and long temporal intervals surface dynamic changes. Moreover, a channel-aware Mamba fusion module (CA-MFM) is designed to achieve feature fusion across both spatial and channel dimensions. Experimental results on the CIA and LGC datasets demonstrate that the fusion images of NCTM-STF consistently outperform existing methods under both two- and three-input settings in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations. Ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of each core module. Its advantages are especially evident in complex scenarios with significant phenological changes and drastic land cover variations, even under long-interval conditions, highlighting its superior spatiotemporal fusion performance.

title: FedSKD: Aggregation-Free Model-Heterogeneous Federated Learning via Multidimensional Similarity Knowledge Distillation for Medical Image Classification
authors: Ziqiao Weng, Weidong Cai, Bo Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative model training without direct data sharing. Model-heterogeneous FL (MHFL) enables clients to train personalized models with heterogeneous architectures, but existing methods mainly rely on centralized aggregation or require partially identical architectures, limiting scalability and efficiency. Current peer-to-peer (P2P) FL frameworks, though removing server dependence, have not been adapted to heterogeneous models and suffer from model drift and knowledge dilution. To address these challenges, we propose FedSKD, a novel P2P MHFL framework for medical image classification that facilitates direct knowledge exchange through round-robin model circulation, eliminating the need for centralized aggregation while allowing fully heterogeneous model architectures across clients. FedSKD’s key innovation lies in multidimensional similarity knowledge distillation (SKD), which enables bidirectional cross-client knowledge transfer at batch, pixel/voxel, and region levels for heterogeneous models in FL. This approach mitigates catastrophic forgetting and model drift through progressive reinforcement and distribution alignment while preserving model heterogeneity. Extensive evaluations on fMRI-based autism spectrum disorder (ASD) diagnosis and skin lesion classification demonstrate that FedSKD outperforms state-of-the-art heterogeneous and homogeneous FL baselines, achieving superior personalization and cross-institutional generalization. These findings underscore FedSKD’s potential as a scalable and robust solution for real-world medical FL.

title: NOMA-ISAC Enhanced Secure Short-Packet Transmission in IoE Networks
authors: Suyu Lv, Chang Liu, Meng Li, Rui Meng, Jingxuan Zhang, Xiaodong Xu
abstract: To facilitate low-latency and secure transmission in Internet of Everything, a secure short-packet transmission framework is proposed in an uplink non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA)-based integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system. A triple-functional base station is utilized, which simultaneously carries out the tasks of receiving short-packet messages, detecting potential eavesdroppers, and transmitting active jamming signals. The achievable secure short-packet transmission rate is developed to measure the security performance, where the practical cases of imperfect inter-functional and inter-device interference elimination are considered. To optimize the security performance by effectively leveraging sensing capabilities, a problem is formulated aiming at maximizing the secure short-packet transmission rate, while guaranteeing the sensing quality. A key challenge to solve this problem lies in the performance loss terms associated with decoding error and information leakage in short-packet transmission, which makes the optimization problem highly coupled and strictly non-convex. To tackle this challenge, an alternating optimization (AO)-based approach is devised to solve the formulated problem iteratively, where an approximation method is proposed to convert the secure short-packet transmission rate into a manageable form. The convergence and effectiveness of the proposed design are validated by simulation results, which reveal that i) the devised AO-based algorithm converges within a modest number of iteration times; ii) the proposed NOMA-ISAC-based security design outperforms other benchmark schemes, demonstrating the benefit of utilizing sensing capabilities to enhance security.

title: Hi-MAC: An Intelligent MAC for Heterogeneous Wireless Network
authors: Wenhui Li, Yibin Deng, Fengyuan Zhu, Jiazhen Lei, Linling Zhong, Zeming Yang, Bingbing Wang, Xiaohua Tian
abstract: When Internet of Things (IoTs) devices operate in the dense 2.4 GHz band, severe cross-interference with incumbent networks like Wi-Fi degrades total throughput. While cognitive radio (CR) can replace short-term contention with predictive access to improve long-term performance, existing designs ignore system delays, causing decision-execution mismatch and impractical deployment. We propose Hi-MAC, a gateway-assisted spectrum sharing system that uses machine learning for long-term channel access decisions. It generates an opportunity times list for future devices' transmissions, allowing devices to skip outdated slots and use valid ones despite delays. Key contributions include a low-latency end-to-end neural network with incremental learning, a signaling synchronization mechanism, and coordinated end-edge operations to mitigate hidden/exposed nodes. Implemented on software-defined radio-based gateways and IoT devices, Hi-MAC realizes at least 15% higher packet reception rate and 1.42-7.4<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$times$</tex-math></inline-formula> better delivery-loss ratio than other access solutions, demonstrating fewer transmission attempts and less interference to coexisting networks. It not only delivers near-optimal own throughput for IoT but also minimizes its impact on the throughput of incumbent networks. In two evaluated cases, the total throughput achieved by Hi-MAC is 131% and 121% of that of other methods with comparable performance. Besides, the prototype performs fast inference only in 1.93 ms, with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gt $</tex-math></inline-formula>95% time utilization per inference cycle.

title: FML-DGCN: Federated Multi-Label Learning Based on Dynamic Graph Convolutional Networks
authors: Shaocong Xue, Wenjian Luo, Zeping Yin, Jiahao Gu, Yamin Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Federated multilabel learning enables the collaborative training of multilabel classification models while preserving client privacy. Existing federated multilabel learning methods either fail to effectively capture label correlations, which significantly affects model performance in scenarios where labels are interdependent, or increase the risk of client privacy leakage due to the transmission of unnecessary client data. To this end, we propose FML-DGCN, a federated multi-label learning approach based on dynamic graph convolutional networks. In the local training phase, a dynamic graph convolutional module is designed and employed to generate label representations specific to the input image, with parameters efficiently optimized by our designed correlation alignment loss. Within the module, we employ a fully connected layer-based network to merge static label embeddings and image features for computing dynamic label graphs, instead of leveraging complex attention-based networks, making it suitable for federated learning environments with limited computing resources. In the federated aggregation phase, clients transmit only model parameters, without sharing any additional information such as scene knowledge, thus lowering the risk of client privacy leakage. Experimental results on four typical multilabel image classification datasets demonstrate the superiority of our approach.

title: Understanding of the Electrical Contact Performance Degradation of the Crimped Terminals under Thermal Cycling
authors: Wanbin Ren, Lizhi Sun, Chao Zhang, Ming Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: Crimped terminals are widely used in electrical and electronic systems, where long-term reliability is highly sensitive to environmental temperature variations. However, the physical mechanisms governing electrical contact degradation under thermal cycling remain insufficiently understood. In this work, the electrical contact performance degradation of crimped terminals with tin-plated multi-strand conductors under thermal cycling is systematically investigated. Thermal cycling experiments conducted between −40 °C and 85 °C reveal a progressive and irreversible increase in the overall electrical resistance. Decomposition of the resistance components shows a clear transition from conductor-dominated resistance at the initial stage to interface-dominated resistance as thermal cycling proceeds. Then, the scanning electron microscopy and energy-dispersive spectroscopy reveal localized fretting damage at the crimped interface. Subsequently, finite element simulations demonstrate that thermal expansion mismatch between the terminal and wire conductors induces cyclic axial fretting and radial relaxation at the crimped interface during thermal cycling. Finally, a degradation mechanism is proposed in which thermally induced relative motion, fretting wear, and oxidation act synergistically to govern the irreversible increase and instability of electrical resistance. These findings clarify the physical origin of contact degradation in crimped terminals under thermal cycling and provide guidance for improving the reliability of crimped connections operating in temperature-varying service environments.

title: Fully Adaptive CWT Framework for the Identification and Analysis of Sub-Synchronous Oscillations in Power Systems
authors: Hassan Ali, Ahmet Onen, Ahmed Badawi, Ahmed Abu-Siada
abstract: Sub-Synchronous Oscillations (SSOs) challenge the stability of modern power systems with high renewable and power-electronic penetration. This paper proposes a fully adaptive Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) framework that integrates Welch Power Spectral Density (WPSD)–guided scale selection with a two-stage Nonlinear Least-Squares (NLS) estimator for accurate frequency and damping identification. Three CWT-based strategies are developed: Adaptive CWT (ACWT) for computationally efficient mode extraction, Targeted CWT (TCWT) for enhanced separation of closely spaced modes, and Refined fixed-scale CWT (RCWT) for noise-robust tracking over a uniform frequency grid. Validation using synthetic signals and real-world Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU) data demonstrates that ACWT offers an effective trade-off between computational efficiency and estimation accuracy, TCWT provides superior localization for closely spaced modes, and RCWT delivers consistently robust performance under noisy conditions. Compared with benchmark methods—including Prony–AIC, VMD–Hilbert, EMD-Hilbert, and EWT–Yoshida—the proposed framework delivers improved accuracy, noise resilience, and real-time suitability for SSO monitoring in smart grids.

title: Identity-Invariant Feature Learning for Face Recognition across The Temporal Domain
authors: Pengfei Xu, Tong Yu, Jianyang Zhang, Yuanyuan Zhang, Songtao Guo, Zhihui Li
abstract: Non-invasive face recognition serves as a critical technique for individual biometric identification. However, facial features may change significantly over periods, which brings great challenges to face recognition methods relying on static facial features. To address this problem, we propose Contrast Adversarial Network (CA-NET), which is designed based on temporal contrastive learning and adversarial learning, for face recognition across the temporal domains. Specifically, We incorporated adversarial learning by training a period classifier against an improved feature extractor to further extract stable identity features. In contrast to prior methods that depend on time labels, CA-NET directly extracts identity features from facial images, breaking through the limitation of missing the time information in biological datasets. In addition, we introduced two novel contrastive losses (LTC Loss and GTC Loss), which allow efficient contrastive learning even with limited data, and significantly improve the model's ability to adapt to temporal changes. CA-NET is applied on face recognition of golden snub-nosed monkeys due to the significant changes in their facial features over short periods. We constructed seven periods of cross-temporal facial datasets, and designed temporal recursive experimental rules. The final experimental results show that CA-NET improves recognition accuracy by at least 13.57% over conventional models and 12.81% over existing cross-temporal models, which confirms the effectiveness of temporal contrastive learning and adversarial constraints in addressing facial feature variations, and a new direction for advancing cross-temporal face recognition technology is provided in this paper.

title: Modulated Model Predictive Control for Modular Multilevel Matrix Converter with Enhanced Steady-state Performance
authors: Zheng Gong, Ziyang Meng, Lianchang Zhang, Xiaojie Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Modular multilevel matrix converter (M3C) is a direct ac/ac frequency conversion technology, which exhibits the characteristics of modularity, scalability, and redundancy. Conventional finite control set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) applies only a single switching state per control period, resulting in a variable switching frequency and significant current tracking errors. In this paper, a modulated model predictive control (M2PC) strategy is proposed, which fixes the switching frequency through modulation technology and reduces the tracking errors in branch currents by operating double switching states. Thus, the steady-state performance of the system is enhanced with the superior dynamic-state performance of the conventional FCS-MPC reserved. In addition, aiming at the inherent computational delay of the digital controller, a two-step forward prediction delay compensation scheme for proposed M2PC is employed. Finally, the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method are verified by simulations and experimental results.

title: Vibrohaptic Design for Simulating High-Five Interactions at Virtual Talent Fan Meetings
authors: Takashi Ota, Ryo Eguchi
abstract: As the market for virtual talents (e.g., VTubers) continues to expand, fan meetings have emerged as crucial events for strengthening the connection between talents and their fans. However, the limited interaction time per fan presents a challenge, making it difficult to create a highly satisfying experience through conversation alone. To enhance engagement, non-verbal interactions such as high-fives are considered an effective means for creating emotional impact and improving fan satisfaction. This paper presents a systematic evaluation of vibrohaptic feedback strategies for simulating a high-five interaction with a virtual avatar, targeting real-world applications such as virtual talent fan meetings. We conducted a user study comparing multiple vibrotactile stimuli, such as standard vibrotactile feedback, asymmetric vibration intended to induce a pulling illusion, and tendon vibration intended to induce a kinesthetic illusion, along with variations in stimulation placement. Results indicate that the mere presence of vibrotactile feedback is more crucial for enhancing the experience than the specific stimulation type employed. Furthermore, applying feedback to the palm, either solely or in combination with the wrist, proved most effective. These findings offer valuable insights for designing physical interaction with virtual talents, thereby enhancing social engagement and fan satisfaction in future fan meeting events.

title: Power Allocation Game Between Multiple Radar System and Jammer for Target Tracking
authors: Bo Wang, Gangsheng Zhang, Haowei Zhang, Junwei Xie, Gang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper investigates a power allocation strategy for target tracking, focusing on the interaction between a multiple radar system (MRS) and a self-defense jammer. A closed-form expression of the Bayesian Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (BCRLB) is derived as a function and employed as the optimization metric. Within a Stackelberg game framework, power allocation models are formulated under two configurations—with the MRS and the jammer, respectively, acting as the leader. In both scenarios, the leader aims to optimize tracking accuracy under a total power budget, while the follower seeks to minimize power consumption while maintaining a predefined tracking performance threshold. To solve this problem, a hybrid iterative algorithm integrating Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions, particle swarm optimization (PSO), and cyclic minimization algorithm is proposed. Furthermore, the existence and uniqueness of the Stackelberg equilibrium are rigorously analyzed. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively balances tracking performance and power consumption.

title: PGRA: Physics Guided Rotation Adaptive Attention for Few-Shot SAR Target Recognition
authors: Binze Hu, Xiayang Xiao, Xu Zhang, Haipeng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar target recognition under few-shot conditions presents significant challenges due to azimuth sensitivity, insufficient utilization of physical priors, and limited generalization capability. To address these issues, this study proposes a novel Physics Guided Rotation Adaptive Attention mechanism that integrates electromagnetic scattering priors with rotation-equivariant attention modeling. The proposed PGRA consists of three key components: 1) Channel Interdependency Dynamic Encoder that purifies and recalibrates feature channels, 2) Multi-angle Rotation-Equivariant Encoder that constructs dual feature branches for image-driven and ASC-based physical representation using a rotational convolutional layer, and 3) Gated Physics-Semantic Modulator that aligns and fuses features across modalities through cross-attention and adaptive gating. By explicitly encoding rotation-aware responses and embedding physically interpretable structures, PGRA enhances both robustness and semantic discriminability in low-sample regimes. Experimental results on the MSTAR and SAR-UAV datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving up to 10.69% accuracy improvement under the OFA-3 few-shot setting while maintaining strong generalization under cross-view conditions. The source code used in this study is publicly available at: https://github.com/waluef/PGRA.

title: Towards Semantically Enhanced Representation Learning for Text-Based Person Retrieval
authors: Kun Chen, Xi Yang, Nannan Wang
abstract: Text-Based Person Retrieval (TBPR), which is a pivotal technology in the intelligent surveillance field, is aimed at retrieving target pedestrians based on free-form textual descriptions. While the existing methods attempt to align cross-modal features via multigranular interactions, their performance remains fundamentally limited by two core challenges: cross-modal semantic inconsistency and cross-modal semantic discriminability. To address these issues, we propose DSEE (Diversity Semantic Embedding Expansion), a novel framework for semantically enhanced representation learning. Unlike approaches that rely on constructing larger or more detailed datasets, DSEE establishes identity-centric cross-modal consistency through contrastive learning and generative synergy. The framework consists of two key modules: Bidirectional-guided Semantic Modeling (BSM) and Generative-driven Semantic Enhancement (GSE) modules. The BSM module constructs novel semantic embeddings by modeling similarity-based interactions between the image and text modalities. Specifically, it emphasizes identity-level similarity to guide the generation of enriched, discriminative semantic representations, thereby enhancing their semantic expressiveness and cross-modal alignment. The GSE module provides enriched semantic diversity through a generative text augmentation scheme based on visual inputs, while refining the semantic precision of the method via a dual-path attention mechanism that performs both intramodal refinement and cross-modal alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DSEE achieves state-of-the-art performance on major benchmarks across diverse scenarios. Our work provides an effective paradigm for advancing TBPR applications in real-world settings.

title: An AI-Enabled Federated Edge–Cloud Communication Framework for Dynamic Task Offloading and Network Anomaly Detection in IoT System
authors: Sirisha Rani Kolli, Md Zia Ur Rahman
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The rapid explosion of Internet of Things (IoT) devices is primarily reshaping modern communication networks. However, they introduce unique challenges in resource management, latency control, and secure data exchange across large-scale edge–cloud infrastructures. Conventional task offloading mechanisms fail to adapt effectively to highly dynamic network conditions and suffer significant communication overhead. This paper presents a task-aware federated learning framework for efficient computation offloading and anomaly detection in Internet of Things based mobile edge computing (MEC) environments. It integrates a hybrid deep learning architecture with adaptive federated learning to jointly optimize task execution decisions and network security. In contrast to the conventional approaches which treat offloading and intrusion detection as isolated entities, the proposed framework provides coordinated decision-making across edge and cloud resources and also reduces communication overhead. The model is evaluated to validate its performance and results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves up to 38–42% reduction in latency, 22–27% improvement in throughput, and nearly 30% reduction in federated learning communication overhead compared to state-of-the-art approaches. Moreover, the anomaly detection module achieves an accuracy of 99.61%, with very low false positive and false negative rates. This indicates that the model is superior in identifying malicious activity in IoT networks. These results highlight the efficiency of combining task-aware federated learning with intelligent offloading strategies for scalable, efficient, and secure edge computing systems.

title: OBIA: A Distributed Multi-Authority Service Identity Authentication Scheme for Online Banking
authors: Shuo Wang, Yifan Liu, Fan Feng, Yi Liu, Zhenpeng Liu
abstract: Existing online banking identity authentication protocols typically rely on centralized authorization centers or trusted third parties, which can lead to single points of failure, key escrow risks, and privacy leaks. Addressing multi-party collaboration scenarios under a trustless assumption, this paper proposes an Overseen-by-Multiple-Authorities Identity Authentication (OBIA) scheme tailored for electronic banking environments. This solution integrates attribute-based cryptography with distributed key generation (DKG) mechanisms across multiple authorization centers. Users derive complete personal keys from attribute-based private keys embedded with random factors, enabling implicit binding of identity and attributes. To support dynamic attribute changes and permission revocation, a hierarchical time-driven key update mechanism is designed. Combined with non-interactive zero-knowledge proofs (NIZK) and elliptic curve cryptography (ECC), this enables efficient, privacy-preserving authentication. At the data storage layer, an optimized multi-layer Merkle hash tree (MMHT) structure reduces blockchain storage and verification overhead. Security analysis demonstrates that the proposed scheme effectively resists forgery, replay, man-in-the-middle, and key compromise attacks. Experimental results show that compared to existing multi-authorization authentication schemes, this approach exhibits superior or comparable computational and communication overhead while significantly enhancing the system’s decentralization and auditability.

title: Optimal DNN Layer Placement for Industrial IoT: A Novel Multi-Choice Multidimensional Knapsack Approach
authors: Rajeev Pratap Singh, Kiran Kumar Pattanaik, Nihit Mohan Johari
abstract: In the resource constrained delay sensitive collaborative computing paradigms of Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), Deep Neural Network (DNN) splitting schemes are promising. Multi-application multi-task (MAMT) scenarios in AI enabled industries bring novel challenges in deciding the split criterion for DNN tasks as opposed to Single -application multi-task (SAMT). This work proposes a novel algorithm that takes into account the varying architectures of DNN tasks and model them as Multi-choice Multi-dimensional Knapsack (MMKP) problem. Layer profiling is used to create an initial greedy solution and to optimize it this work adapts epidemic theory on how a pathogen infection follows an optimal path to spread itself in determining the optimal split solution for MAMTs. The complexity of O(mn) signifies that our approach for MAMTs is efficient compared to that of SAMT with similar complexity. Results show that the proposed approach performs optimally and at par with the prevalent approaches for both SAMT and MAMT.

title: LIBPipe: Efficient Load Imbalance Pipeline Model Parallelism for Large Models Training
authors: Bin Liu, Zhonghao Zhang, Hengzhao Li, Zeyu Ji, Hongming Zhang, Keqin Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: With the increasing size of datasets and the expansion of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), the training process has become exceedingly time-consuming. Distributed training, specifically the Pipeline Model Parallelism (PMP) method, commonly mitigates this problem but suffers from bubble time delays. This paper proposes LIBPipe, a pipeline training framework that explicitly incorporates a load-imbalance method to reduce bubble time in PMP. Within LIBPipe, a model-unequal-partitioning method is designed from the perspective of load imbalance to reshape the pipeline execution pattern and significantly shorten idle periods during training. On top of this method, a performance-guided unequal-partitioning search algorithm is developed to efficiently identify near-optimal partitioning strategies under memory constraints. The paper theoretically proves the time efficiency of adopting load imbalance in pipeline models. Comprehensive experiments are conducted on an 8-GPU server to evaluate the efficiency of LIBPipe, using the IMDB and mini-ImageNet datasets as well as well-known models such as BERT and ResNet. The BERT-series models achieve a maximum throughput improvement of 60.3%, while the ResNet-series models achieve a maximum improvement of 74.1%.

title: Adaptive and Explainable Test Case Prioritization in Continuous Integration: A Systematic Review of Reinforcement Learning, Learning-to-Rank, and Search-/Combinatorial-Based Methods
authors: Srinivasa Rao Kongarana, Ananda Rao Akepogu, Palagiri Radhika Raju
abstract: Continuous Integration requires fast fault exposure under strict time budgets. Test case prioritization orders regression tests to expose failures early while respecting budget limits and operational constraints. A systematic review of 73 studies published during 2020–2025 synthesizes evidence on adaptive and explainable prioritization for CI, organized into learning-driven ranking (learning-to-rank, reinforcement learning, neural rankers, history-only models), multi-armed bandits for volatile test pools, and search/combinatorial optimization for selection and ordering. Adjacent evidence on compute-bounded input prioritization for AI/ML systems is summarized to extract transferable patterns in cost control and explanation. Results show a consistent cost–effectiveness gradient: history-only rankers and volatile bandits achieve strong early-fault yield with sub-second decision time, while structure-aware models provide additional benefit only when feature extraction is incremental and retraining is scheduled to limit model decay. Reinforcement learning performs best when reward design uses time-aware credit assignment and explicit memory to handle sparse failures. Multi-objective search reaches strong trade-offs but requires evaluation caps, cached orders, or pre-filtering by safe regression-test selection to satisfy CI latency. Many studies report effectiveness without synchronized reporting of end-to-end cost, flakiness settings, and safety metrics such as failure recall and precision. Outputs include a taxonomy of signals and method families, a PRISMA-auditable study selection flow, an evidence matrix capturing datasets, metrics, and overhead, a sensitivity analysis for the cost-reporting inclusion criterion, and a decision framework for selecting deployable approaches under CI constraints. A replication package provides the evidence matrix and aggregation scripts.

title: A Compact Dual-Polarized Frequency-Selective Metasurface Enabling Independent Functionalities With Reduced Backward-Scattering
authors: Muhammad Nasir, Slawomir Koziel, Anna Pietrenko-Dabrowska
abstract: A novel dual-polarized dual-functional frequency-selective metasurface (FSMS) with low backscattering characteristics is proposed by combining the polarization converter with a bandpass frequency-selective surface (BP-FSS). The FSMS structure consists of diagonally aligned metallic strip gratings (MSGs) on two substrate layers, and provides two independently tunable functionalities, including wideband reflective cross-polarization conversion (RCPC) in the C-band with a simulated polarization conversion ratio (PCR) exceeding 99%, and co-polarized bandpass transmission in the S-band with insertion loss less than −1 dB. The design concept is validated using an equivalent circuit model (ECM) cascading polarization converter and BP-FSS, as well as measurement of the fabricated prototype. Both the ECM predictions and measurements are in good agreement with the full-wave electromagnetic (EM) simulation. Besides, when the proposed structure is arranged in a chessboard configuration, the low backscattering reduction is achieved only within the RCPC range. Unlike previously reported dual-functional MSs, the proposed FSMS eliminates the need for an additional BP-FSS layer and a complete ground plane for the RCPC response. Owing to compactness and independent tunable functionalities, the proposed FSMS is well-suited for antennas, radomes, and communication applications.
