title: Identification and Observation of Spatiotemporal Evolution Features of Coalfield Fire Areas Using Landsat Time Series Decomposition
authors: Jinchang Deng, Bobo Shi, Yong Xue, José L. Torero Cullen, Liying Han, Zhengyang Qu, Jin Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Globally uncontrolled and persistent coal fires cause substantial resource depletion and long-term environmental and safety risks. However, their long-term detection is hindered by complex subsurface dynamics, combustion behaviour and insufficient data from traditional monitoring methods. This study developed an integrated framework for the dynamic characterisation of fire hazards. The approach combines time-series decomposition, abrupt change analysis, and robust regression, using Landsat-derived land surface temperature (LST) time-series data (2013-2024) for the Miquan coalfield, Xinjiang, China. The LST was first retrieved from Landsat thermal infrared imagery via the Radiative Transfer Equation model. A hybrid Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess (STL) and Breaks for Additive Season and Trend (BFAST) model then separated trend, seasonal, and residual components, enabling the detection and quantification of abrupt LST changes related to coal fire dynamics. Thermal trends and anomalies were further quantified using the Mann–Kendall test, Sen’s slope, and a novel Annual Anomaly Deviation (AAD) metric. Crucially, using RANSAC-based regression, persistent potential fire pixels were extracted and categorised into four frequency regimes, with high robustness to noise and data gaps. The resulting coal fire frequency maps achieved over 77% spatial accuracy relative to field records, demonstrating enhanced continuity and reliability in long-term coal fire monitoring. The framework provides a versatile, pixel-level, and analytical paradigm for understanding the spatio-temporal evolution of these challenging landscape phenomena.

title: An Integrated Inductor for Circulating Current Suppression in Two Parallel Interleaved VSCs
authors: Yuanmao Ye, Yunqi Ma, Bofei Shu, Haolin Chen, Miao Zhang, Lingling Cao
abstract: Parallel interleaved voltage source converters (PI-VSCs) have good performance in the terms of capacity enhancement and the output power quality improvement. However, a set of inductive filters needs to be installed between PI-VSCs to suppress the circulating current. This article presents a three-phase integrated inductor to replace this set of inductive filters. It is composed of six coils and one magnetic core. The core is formed by an outer toroidal and an inner Y-shaped sections, resulting in a simple and symmetrical structure. Mathematical model, theoretical analysis and comparative study are provided. It indicates that the integrated inductor has higher utilization of magnetic cores and it can be designed with smaller size than traditional schemes. Moreover, the proposed inductor has stronger common-mode circulation current suppression capability. This means that it can effectively suppress circulation current without the assistance of complex pulse width modulation algorithms. Its feasibility is effectively demonstrated by both simulation and experiment results.

title: Segmentation-Guided Accelerating Diffusion Model for Cardiac CT Motion Artifact Reduction via Limited-Angle Imaging
authors: Dianlin Hu, Zhan Wu, Lin Zhao, Guotao Quan, Shangwen Yang, Yikun Zhang, Huazhong Shu, Yang Chen
abstract: Coronary computed tomography angiography (CCTA) is a pivotal non-invasive imaging modality for diagnosing cardiac disease. However, due to the temporal resolution limitations, cardiac structures, specifically coronary arteries, may suffer from motion artifacts when CCTA is applied to patients with arrhythmias or high heart rates. Limited-angle CT (LA-CT) emerges as a promising alternative by significantly reducing the acquisition time, thereby mitigating the motion artifacts. Yet, LA-CT unavoidably leads to severe wedge artifacts, posing a significant challenge. Therefore, to reconstruct motion-free cardiac CT images while suppressing the wedge artifacts, we propose a Segmentation-Guided Accelerating Diffusion Model (SGADM) tailored for LA-CT imaging. While diffusion models have demonstrated exceptional performance in medical imaging, their extensive sampling procedures impose high computational costs, hindering clinical applicability. To address this issue, SGADM employs an innovative diffusion model that directly generates high-quality CT images. Moreover, SGADM adopts the diffusion perceptual loss to ensure data distribution consistency between two successive sampling steps. As a result, SGADM can provide satisfactory results in fewer than 10 steps. Additionally, SGADM incorporates segmentation guidance to enhance the spatial-positional accuracy of generated coronary arteries explicitly. Both quantitative and qualitative evaluations on simulated and real datasets reveal that the SGADM effectively restores high-quality CCTA images with minimal motion artifacts, highlighting its potential for clinical applications.

title: Hybrid Magnetic Lenses Framing Camera and Its Spatiotemporal Resolution Characteristics
authors: Qiuyan Luo, Zhuoming Du, Jinyuan Liu, Houzhi Cai, Lijuan Xiang
abstract: X-ray framing camera serves as an important ultrafast diagnostic instrument for inertial confinement fusion, Z-pinch fusion, synchrotron radiation, laser-plasma physics, and so on. To enhance the spatiotemporal resolution and image size, a framing camera based on pulse-dilation as well as hybrid magnetic lenses has been presented. The hybrid magnetic lenses consist of two short and four long magnetic lenses. The spatiotemporal resolution characteristics of the pulse-dilation framing camera are studied. While the photocathode (PC) is excited by single-pulse with slope of 3 V/ps, the camera achieves an 8 ps temporal resolution and 1.5 mm frame size along the cathode pulse direction. To improve the frame size, double cathode pulses are applied. The frame size is significantly improved, which is only limited by the cathode microstrip length of 60 mm. Furthermore, the temporal resolution is also improved to 4.3 ps. The hybrid magnetic lenses could achieve a high spatial resolution of 47.2 lp/mm with a difference of 1.6% within the cathode imaging diameter of 60 mm, and then a spatial resolution of 22.1 lp/mm is obtained for the camera. Moreover, the image relative distortion is 1.5% in the X-axis and 1.6% in the Y-axis.

title: From Tool to Teammate: Balancing Task-Oriented and Social Talk During Human–Robot Collaboration
authors: Chayan Sarkar, Rebecca Ramnauth, Kate Candon, Alexander Lew, Marynel Vázquez
abstract: As robots become more integrated into shared work environments, effective communication must balance clear task directives with social engagement to support both efficiency and user satisfaction. This letter examines how people perceive task-oriented versus social dialogue during human–robot collaboration and how they judge the appropriateness of switching between them. We discuss a pilot, in-person data collection and two follow-up online studies with an autonomous robot in a collaborative pizza-making task. The pilot assesses the technical feasibility of an autonomous robot generating distinct task and social dialogue styles. Next, with a larger participant sample, we evaluate the salience of the two talk types and their impact on perceptions of the robot as a collaborative partner. The second study explores judgments of talk type switching using video clips from the pilot, revealing how timing, task state, and user behavior influence perceived appropriateness. Together, these findings highlight nuanced human expectations for robot communication and inform the design of collaborative robots that can adaptively balance task-oriented and social talk in contextually sensitive ways.

title: A Study on the Adoption Mechanism of AI-Supported Learning Based on TAM and Social Cognitive Theory
authors: Liang Feng
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Education
abstract: None

title: Event camera-based digital autoradiography for detection of alpha and beta particle emissions
authors: K.L. Bertschinger, B.S. McDonald, L. Pagani, E.R. Siciliano, M.A. Zalavadia
abstract: Autoradiography is an established technique for imaging and quantifying ionizing radiation emission from samples, with applications that span medical imaging, nuclear forensics, and environmental monitoring. This study explores the potential of using an event camera to perform quantitative digital autoradiography of samples emitting alpha and beta particles. Event cameras mimic the human eye’s efficient architecture, detecting asynchronous changes in pixel intensity and providing capabilities such as low latency, high dynamic range, and sparse data representation. The event camera was swapped for a standard CMOS (complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor) camera and optically coupled to a scintillator screen and an image intensifier—key components of the ionizing quantum radiation detector (iQID) autoradiography system. Comparative measurements were conducted with the CMOS and event cameras in otherwise identical iQID configurations. Alpha and beta particles were imaged concurrently with the event camera, demonstrating a potential wider sensitivity and dynamic range than that of a CMOS camera. Furthermore, tests with high activity sources were performed to assess the count rate capability of event cameras. These advances highlight the potential of event-based imaging in radiation imaging and its broader implications for various critical fields, including scientific research, healthcare, and nuclear security.

title: Hybrid Rate-Splitting and Sparse Code Multiple Access (RS-SCMA): Design and Performance
authors: Minerva Priyadarsini, Zilong Liu, Kuntal Deka, Sujit Kumar Sahoo, Sanjeev Sharma
abstract: This paper proposes, for the first time, a hybrid multiple access framework that integrates the principles of rate-splitting (RS) and sparse code multiple access (SCMA) in an SISO downlink scenario. The proposed scheme, termed RS-SCMA, unifies the powerful interference management capability of rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) with the near-optimal multiuser detection of SCMA. A key feature of RS-SCMA is a tunable splitting factor α, which governs the allocation between the generic M-ary modulated common messages and SCMA-encoded private messages. This enables dynamic control over the fundamental trade-off between system sum-rate, bit error rate (BER), and the overloading factor. We develop novel transmitter and receiver architectures based on soft successive interference cancellation (SIC), incorporating message passing algorithm (MPA) detection and soft-symbol reconstruction. Furthermore, a unified analytical expression for the achievable sum-rate is derived as a function of the splitting factor α. The performance of the proposed RS-SCMA system is evaluated in terms of both BER and sum-rate. Simulation results confirm the superiority of RS-SCMA over conventional SCMA and multi-carrier RSMA, demonstrating its scalability and robustness even in the presence of channel estimation errors.

title: Near-Real-Time Multi-Parametric Quantitative MRI using Parallel Non-Cartesian 6D Spatial-Temporal Dictionary Learning Neural Networks_supp2-3685550.mp4
authors: Yiping P. Du
abstract: Objective: Multi-parametric quantitative MRI (qMRI) enables precise targeting during image-guided interventions such as deep brain stimulation. To address the demand for higher temporal resolution in multi-parametric qMRI of the brain, we propose an online pipeline for near-real-time quantitative MRI. Methods: The acquisition utilizes an alternating dual-flip-angle blipped multi-gradient-echo sequence with a stack-of-stars (SoS) trajectory, enabling efficient sampling while facilitating T1 mapping. K-space data are streamed to the Gadgetron framework for preprocessing and parallel non-Cartesian reconstruction. We extend the original 4D spatial-temporal dictionary learning neural networks (stDLNN), which exploit correlations across 2D space, time, and real/imaginary channels in dynamic MRI, to a 6D formulation for dual-flip-angle multi-echo complex-valued 3D reconstruction. The proposed 6D-stDLNN jointly exploits correlations across 3D space, echo, flip angle, and real/imaginary channels during reconstruction. Subsequently, quantitative calculations are efficiently performed on the GPU. Results: Prospective results from volunteer studies show good consistency (intraclass correlation coefficients > 0.9) between 3D multi-parametric maps from accelerated sampling and those from fully sampled references. In the slab-selective scan, the temporal resolution achieved was 8.1 s per frame, and the total algorithm runtime was below 5 s per frame. Conclusion: We present a near-real-time imaging pipeline that simultaneously provides 3D quantitative susceptibility mapping (QSM), R2* mapping, and T1 mapping, along with susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) and maximum intensity projection (MaxIP) images. Significance: This work demonstrates the feasibility of immediate multi-parametric quantitative assessment in the brain, indicating potential utility in time-critical applications and future interventional studies.

title: Contrastive Feature Reasoning for EEG Classification in Asynchronous BCI-Controlled Humanoid Robot_supp2-3678704.mp4
authors: xingwei zhao
abstract: Asynchronous brain–computer interfaces (aBCIs) provide a direct neural interface for decoding human intention, enabling practical brain-controlled interaction with humanoid robots. The key challenge, in aBCI-controlled robotics without explicit triggers, lies in the robust perception and accurate discrimination of control states from nonstationary electroencephalography (EEG) with fuzzy temporal boundaries. This study proposes a contrastive feature reasoning (CFR) framework for online EEG classification, which leverages a dual contrastive learning strategy to hierarchically model the spatio-spectral representation and temporal reasoning of EEG states. Specifically, template-EEG pairs are constructed to train a contrastive feature extractor via self-supervised contrastive learning, yielding cross-subject robust perception of spatio-spectral embedding features. Building on these embedding features, a contrastive feature reasoner is designed to perform temporal reasoning by evaluating contrastive similarity between current and historical features, coupled with relative-threshold discrimination for EEG state classification. Experiments on self-acquired EEG data from 12 participants, along with a humanoid robot control task involving six subjects, demonstrate an average cross-subject online control success rate of 97.33%, validating the effectiveness of CFR as a pivotal EEG classification component for aBCI-driven humanoid interaction.

title: FineCL: Exemplar-free Continual Learning for Fine-grained Remote Sensing Panoptic Segmentation
authors: Bo Yuan, Danpei Zhao, Tian Li, Wentao Li, Bowen Yang, Zhenwei Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Continual learning enables the model to continually adapt to new data and tasks. However, the catastrophic forgetting and semantic drift lead to severe model degradation, especially in fine-grained segmentation tasks. Besides, replay-based methods brings extra storage burdens and raises privacy concerns. To address these challenges, we propose FineCL, an exemplar-free continual panoptic segmentation method. Concretely, FineCL proposes an anchoring contrastive distillation (ACD) method to preserve model stability by constraining the semantic consistency. To tackle semantic drift in fine-grained incremental learning, FineCL proposes a dynamic memory bank (DMB) to enhance model plasticity via triplet prototype ranking loss for new knowledge integration. Without storing exemplar data, FineCL sets an uncertainty-guided pseudo-labeling (UGPL) strategy to generate high-confidence pseudo-labels for incremental training. The framework supports end-to-end training with multiple concurrent objectives. Extensive experiments on two fine-grained datasets validate the effectiveness of the proposed model. FineCL outperforms state-of-the-art method by maximum 6.6% improvement in long-step continual panoptic segmentation tasks.

title: IDEA: Automated Design Space Exploration for Visualization Design_supp1-3684920.pdf
authors: Nan Cao
abstract: Design spaces serve as a conceptual framework that enables designers to explore feasible solutions, yet their lack of computational formalization limits integration with AI-driven design automation. To address this, we introduce a structured design space model that formalizes design spaces with orthogonal dimensions and discrete elements, making them machine-interpretable and executable. Building on this model, we present IDEA, a fully automated design exploration framework to generate effective outcomes based on user requirements and design spaces. Specifically, IDEA leverages large language models (LLMs) for constraint generation, incorporates a constraint-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) algorithm to explore the space, and instantiates abstract decisions into domain-specific implementations. We evaluate IDEA in two design scenarios: data-driven article and standard visualization, supported by blind ratings, expert interviews, and quantitative experiments. Results demonstrate the IDEA's adaptability across domains and its capability to produce high-quality designs.

title: A Voltage-Source-Charging Series-Capacitor Boost Converter With Automatic Current Balancing, Constant and Low Voltage Stress Within Full Duty-Cycle Range
authors: Yang Qin, Dylan Dah-Chuan Lu, Yam Prasad Siwakoti
abstract: The conventional series-capacitor boost converter offers common ground among the input, output, and switches, and utilizes phase extension to enhance voltage gain while reducing current/voltage stress and input current ripple. However, under symmetrical control (i.e., equal duty cycles with a $\textbf{180}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$ phase shift) and $\boldsymbol{D} \lt \textbf{0.5}$ , it suffers from voltage gain reduction, increased voltage stress, and inductor current imbalance. This article reveals that the root cause of the above-mentioned problems lies in the series-capacitor voltage decreases from $\boldsymbol{U}_{\bf{in}}$ /(1–D) when D < 0.5. To overcome these limitations, this work employs a conventional boost converter with a gain of 1/(1–D) and positive output to charge the series capacitor, thereby forming a new family of two-phase voltage-source-charging series-capacitor boost converters and serving as an illustrative example. The operating principles under continuous conduction mode are analyzed in detail, the steady-state performance is characterized and extended to N-phase configuration, and small-signal models are developed along with controller design. A 500 W/50 kHz prototype with an input voltage range of 40–110 V and an output voltage of 400 V was built and tested. The results demonstrate that under symmetrical control, the proposed converter maintains a voltage gain of 2/(1–D) across the entire duty cycle range, with constant voltage stresses on all power components equaling half of the output voltage. Moreover, the proposed converter can automatically balance the input inductor currents without additional current sensors, and achieve a peak efficiency of 98.5% and a minimum efficiency of 96.9%.

title: SANR: Scene-Aware Neural Representation for Light Field Image Compression with Rate-Distortion Optimization
authors: Gai Zhang, Xinfeng Zhang, Lv Tang, Hongyu An, Li Zhang, Qingming Huang
abstract: Light field images capture multi-view scene information and play a crucial role in 3D scene reconstruction. However, their high-dimensional nature results in enormous data volumes, posing a significant challenge for efficient compression in practical storage and transmission scenarios. Although neural representation-based methods have shown promise in light field image compression, most approaches rely on direct coordinate-to-pixel mapping through implicit neural representation (INR), often neglecting the explicit modeling of scene structure. Moreover, they typically lack end-to-end rate-distortion optimization, limiting their compression efficiency. To address these limitations, we propose SANR, a Scene-Aware Neural Representation framework for light field image compression with end-to-end rate-distortion optimization. For scene awareness, SANR introduces a hierarchical scene modeling block that leverages multi-scale latent codes to capture intrinsic scene structures, thereby reducing the information gap between INR input coordinates and the target light field image. From a compression perspective, SANR is the first to incorporate entropy-constrained quantization-aware training (QAT) into neural representation-based light field image compression, enabling end-to-end rate-distortion optimization. Extensive experiment results demonstrate that SANR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art techniques regarding rate-distortion performance with a 65.62% BD-rate saving against HEVC.

title: Design of Synaptic Interconnection of a Sensory LIF Neuron in CMOS for High-Speed In-sensor Neuromorphic Systems
authors: M. A. Seenivasan, Prabir Saha, Shubhankar Majumdar, Gautam Srivastava
abstract: The sensory transduction process of the sensory neurons involves detecting a stimulus from thermal (heat), chemical (taste), or mechanical stimuli (touch) by the sensory receptors and converting a stimulus into an electrical signal (spikes). The sensory (pre-synaptic) neuron passes spike signals through gap junctions called synapses (synaptic interconnection) to the post-synaptic neuron, for high-speed transmission. Such synaptic interconnections are essential for neural communication, in-sensor computing, and signal transmission. This paper presents a method for designing a current-mode analog interconnect circuit that emulates a synapse and solves resistive/ conductive interconnect issues between neurons, compared to digital interconnect (AER) methods. In addition, the implementation of the current-mode interconnect is performed through an OTA-DPI circuit that improves power and energy efficiency because it operates at lower voltage swings with good noise margins. The proposed design offers a low-loss conduction path with plasticity spike intervals that emulate STDP learning. This approach is adaptable for high-speed synaptic interconnections in neuromorphic VLSI systems. The proposed model was simulated using Cadence GPDK 45nm technology, providing a post-spike interval estimation (t<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">post</sub> − t<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">pre</sub>), and emulates STDP, an excitatory synaptic current of 1.321μA, and 23.06fJ of energy per spike consumption with a 0.7V power supply.

title: Online Learning-based Task Offloading for Energy-Efficient Edge Vision Analytics under Latency Constraints in Indoor WLANs
authors: Ryangsoo Kim, Hark Yoo, Sung Chang Kim, Yonggang Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Edge computing enables resource-constrained mobile devices to execute deep neural network (DNN)-based vision analytics by offloading tasks to nearby edge servers via wireless networks. However, in practical indoor IEEE 802.11 WLAN environments, the effectiveness of edge-assisted offloading is highly sensitive to time-varying link quality caused by device mobility, multipath fading, structural obstacles, and multi-user contention. In this paper, we develop an adaptive edge-assisted vision analytics framework that minimizes per-frame energy consumption while satisfying a strict latency constraint. Motivated by measurement-driven characterization of the link-quality–performance relationship on a real WLAN testbed, we explicitly formulate an RSSI-conditioned task offloading problem and design a lightweight online learning algorithm that operates without prior statistical knowledge. By integrating a penalty-based latency-aware decision rule with a sliding-window upper confidence bound (SW-UCB) mechanism, the proposed policy adapts to stationary and interval-wise non-stationary network conditions, with regret analysis guaranteeing sublinear performance loss. Extensive experiments on a real indoor WLAN testbed demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly reduces long-term energy consumption, strictly controls latency violations under mobility-induced NLoS and multi-user contention, and incurs negligible runtime overhead.

title: i3DV: Intelligent 3D Volumetric Video Coding Standard and Platform
authors: Jiayu Yang, Luyang Tang, Jiahao Wu, Jie Liang, Feng Gao, Ronggang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: 3D volumetric video enables users to freely navigate and observe scenes from any position and view direction through interactive operations, delivering more authentic and immersive viewing experiences. To address the issues of 6DoF interaction, photorealistic rendering quality of virtual viewpoints and efficient compression, the Audio Video Coding Standard Workgroup of China (AVS) is developing an intelligent 3D volumetric video coding standard named i3DV. The standard proposes a paradigm shift for 3D volumetric video, taking the advantages of implicit differentiable representation models for intelligent volumetric video reconstruction and coding. This paper provides an overview of the technical features and characteristics of the i3DV standard. In addition, this paper provides a report on the primary efforts of the reference software platform and its coding efficiency. The exploration experiment shows that the platform achieves high-quality virtual viewpoint rendering while significantly compressing the data size of volumetric video.

title: Toward Battery-Free Airflow Monitoring: Groove Enhanced Piezoelectric Wind Energy Harvester for Underground Mines
authors: Huili Zhang, Binghao Li, Mahmoud Karimi, Serkan Saydam, Mahbub Hassan
abstract: Underground mines require continuous airflow monitoring, yet cabling or frequent battery replacement for distributed sensors is costly and hazardous. In this study, we present a piezoelectric wind energy harvester (PWEH) that also functions as a self-powered sensor for monitoring ventilation airflow conditions in underground mines. A shallow groove machined into the windward face of a D‑shaped bluff body boosts harvested power by 29.3% at a wind speed of 4.5 m/s compared with an ungrooved benchmark. The resulting voltage waveform supports two embedded sensing functions without external power: (i) ventilation fault diagnosis based on a stacking ensemble that integrates one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D CNN), random forest (RF) and transfer-learned AlexNet classifier, enhanced by manifold‑based data augmentation, to identify 16 fault scenarios in a laboratory mine ventilation layout with up to 97.4% accuracy; and (ii) wind speed estimation using Random Forest that delivers a median mean absolute error below 0.12 m/s and worst‑case error under 0.5 m/s across 1.5–5.5 m/s airflow speeds, matching the performance of commercial anemometers. Laboratory trials in a scaled mine ventilation layout validate these capabilities across 16 duct networks with fan speeds ranging from 200–700 rpm, while energy measurements show the device produces a root mean square power output of 5.2 μW at a 4.5 m/s wind speed, which is sufficient to run a Bluetooth Low Energy-based SensorTag for fog data transmission every 50 seconds. By combining wind energy harvesting and airflow sensing in a single device, the proposed PWEH offers a battery-free and low-maintenance path to safer and smarter Mine Internet of Things deployments.

title: Autism Action Dataset (AAD): A Comprehensive Video Benchmark for ASD Behavior Recognition Using Deep Learning
authors: Md. Arif Shahriar, Md. Zasim Uddin, Md Rafiul Islam Rafi, Most. Farhana Akter, Fady Alnajjar, Md Atiqur Rahman Ahad
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by impairments in social interaction, communication, and behavior. Accurately recognizing the behaviors of children with ASD is crucial for diagnosis and early intervention. Traditional clinician- or caregiver-based approaches are often time-consuming, subjective, and delayed. In contrast, computer vision, along with modern deep learning (DL)-based methods, offers promising alternative solutions. However, a significant obstacle for this purpose is the lack of large-scale, publicly available datasets of ASD-related behaviors. To address the gap, we present the Autism Action Dataset (AAD), a novel publicly available video dataset including 202 manually annotated videos with seven distinct ASD-related action classes: Jumping, Arm Flapping, Joint Attention, Spinning, Head Banging, Head Shaking, and Imitation. These videos were collected from diverse YouTube sources to ensure variability in environment and subject condition.We evaluated the dataset using several state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN)-based action recognition benchmarks, including CNN-LSTM, Inflated 3D (I3D), SlowFast, and Expandable 3D (X3D), as well as transformer-based attention benchmarks such as Video Vision Transformer (ViViT) and Multiscale Vision Transformer (MViT). Additionally, we evaluated pre-trained models fine-tuned on our dataset, as well as models trained from scratch under 5-fold stratified cross-validation. Among fine-tuned pre-trained methods, X3D achieved the best performance, reaching an accuracy of 84.18% along with a 79.82% F1-score. When trained from scratch, I3D achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 64.29% with a 59.29% F1-score. These results demonstrate both the value of the AAD dataset as a publicly available benchmark and the efficacy of the I3D and X3D methods for ASD behavior analysis. They provide a solid foundation for future research and the development of assistive diagnostic tools. The dataset, metadata, and corresponding benchmark result will be publicly available at https://github.com/reyadhasan605/Autism-Action-Dataset.

title: An Analytical-Computational Framework for Electromagnetic Scattering by A Buried Anisotropic Gyrotropic-Coated Conducting Sphere
authors: Xinchen Liu, Youlin Geng
publish: IEEE Journal on Multiscale and Multiphysics Computational Techniques
abstract: By combining the addition theorem of spherical vector wave functions (SVWFs), the image method, the T-matrix method, and the recursive algorithm, this paper develops an efficient analytical-computational framework for electromagnetic scattering by a buried anisotropic gyrotropic-coated conducting sphere. The transmitted electromagnetic field from a vertically incident wave acts as the initial excitation for the buried object and is expanded using the SVWFs. By applying the boundary conditions, the scattered fields of the anisotropic gyrotropic-coated conducting sphere are obtained and further interact with the ground. The multiple reflections between the buried object and the ground are modeled through the image method and the addition theorem of SVWFs in an iterative framework until the field on the ground converges. Unlike previous studies focusing on single-point evaluation, the proposed method analyzes the field distribution over the entire ground plane. Numerical results are presented and show excellent agreement with the FEKO simulations, thereby validating the accuracy and robustness of the proposed framework.

title: IR-SPC:Image Restoration with Starting Point Controlled Diffusion Model
authors: Cheng Yang, Lijing Liang, Shuo Liu, Lei Zhang, Zhixun Su
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: This paper proposes a starting point controlled (SPC) diffusion model for general-purpose image restoration, which directly models the image degradation process as a diffusion process. The key idea of the SPC approach is to transform a high-quality image into a degraded counterpart, which is a low-quality image with added Gaussian noise as the starting point for the reverse process rather than pure Gaussian noise. Then, by simulating the corresponding SPC reverse-time process, we can restore the original high-quality image from its low-quality image without requiring any task-specific prior knowledge. Crucially, the IR-SPC method has a closed-form solution, allowing us to compute the true posterior probability and learn it with a neural network. Furthermore, we introduce a maximum likelihood objective to optimize the reverse trajectory that stabilizes the training and improves the restoration results. Additionally, we also propose optimized training and sampling algorithms to improve the computational efficiency and algorithmic consistency of the IR-SPC framework. Extensive experimental results show that our proposed method achieves highly competitive performance in quantitative comparisons of perceptual metrics, setting a new state-of-the-art perception metrics on restoration tasks.

title: A Hybrid Controller for Constant Current and Constant Voltage Mode in a Single-Stage Power Factor Corrected Wireless Power Transfer Converter
authors: Saman Rezazade, Mohammed Agamy
abstract: Wireless power transfer (WPT) systems are used in numerous applications, including electric vehicle charging, where high power capability, constant-voltage (CV) and constant-current (CC) regulation, high power factor (PF), low total harmonic distortion (THD), and high efficiency are required. This article proposes a single-stage WPT architecture that integrates an AC–DC power factor correction (PFC) front end with a resonant converter by sharing the active power switches and control hardware. Compared with conventional two-stage topologies, the proposed topology reduces circuit complexity, component count, power loss, and cost. A hybrid control scheme is developed to regulate the DC-link voltage and the DC output current/voltage. Specifically, a variable duty ratio controller regulates the DC-link voltage and shapes the input current in continuous conduction mode (CCM), while the DC output is regulated through variable switching frequency with a capacitive output filter. By coordinating duty ratio and switching frequency, the controller maintains CC/CV operation over a wide load range, eliminating the need for two independent controllers in conventional implementations. To verify closed-loop stability, a small-signal model is derived and the dynamics are evaluated using eigenvalue analysis. CCM operation reduces input current peaks and simplifies high-frequency filtering requirements, enabling improved THD, reduced output filter size, and higher overall efficiency.

title: Seed-Layer-Free Electroplated Copper Metallization of SHJ Cells Enabled by Chemical Modification of Transparent Conductive Oxides
authors: G. Rodrigues Lopes, S. Bastide, Y. Bourlier, J. Jourdan, W. Favre
abstract: This work presents a seed-layer-free wet-chemical surface treatment enabling direct copper (Cu) electroplating on indium-based transparent conductive oxides (TCOs), particularly indium tin oxide (ITO), for silicon heterojunction (SHJ) solar cells, without the need for a seed or barrier layer. The process involves two sequential chemical baths: an ammoniacal copper complex bath (B1) promoting Cu(OH)<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> chemisorption, followed by a borohydride-based reducing bath (B2) that converts Cu<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2+</sup> to Cu<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sup> and catalyzes the formation of metallic indium (In<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sup>) particles at the surface, enabling adhesion at the TCO interface. Structural and chemical analyses (SEM, EDX, XPS) confirmed the formation of Cu and In species, while electrical (dark I–V) and mechanical (shear strength) tests showed reduced contact resistance and strong adhesion for optimized treatment durations. Interfacial TEM-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ \mu$</tex-math></inline-formula>EDX analysis further reveals that the chemical treatment induces localized modification of the TCO near the surface, while a continuous oxide layer remains beneath the plated copper. The proposed method offers a cost-effective, low-temperature, and easy-to-implement alternative to conventional metallization processes for SHJ technologies.

title: Self-derived Motion Features from sEMG for Inferring 3D Forearm Trajectories
authors: Bangyu Lan, Dezhi Sun, Izadyar Tamadon, Kenan Niu
abstract: Surface electromyography (sEMG) records the activation patterns of muscles. It has been widely studied for human motion estimation in wearable robotics and human-machine interaction. Most existing methods focus on mapping sEMG signals to intermediate kinematic variables (joint angles, torque, or intended motion). Inferring 3D trajectories directly is challenging, as it generally requires additional motion features from other sensing modalities. Unlike the previous works, we proposed a method to derive extensible motion features from sEMG to infer 3D motion trajectory. To validate, an in-vivo experiment was performed to collect nine different typical forearm motions for ten subjects. The results showed that the self-derived motion features improved the motion estimation performance by an average of 10% to 15% across evaluation metrics. This revealed the closed relationships of the derived motion features, and their contributions to the 3D trajectory. The results showed the potential for deriving more critical motion features from sEMG within a single framework, which assists in an in-depth understanding of forearm motor control and improves the human motion trajectory inference in future robotics applications.

title: Single-Unit 3-Bit and 4-Bit Reflection-Type Phase Shifters With Flat Phase Responses
authors: Xiaojie Zhang, Yun Liu, Zhuo Li, Minghai Pan
abstract: Digital phase shifters (DPSs) are essential for phased-array systems, but the conventional cascaded architecture for multibit implementation suffers from a large footprint, high insertion loss (IL), and accumulated phase errors. This article presents single-unit multibit reflection-type phase shifters (RTPSs) based on the star-shaped switchable reactance block topology, which enable multiple phase-shifting bits within a single circuit. Each star-shaped structure consists of a connecting transmission line with N parallel open-circuited stubs selectively connected via PIN diodes. The core innovation is leveraging a strong linear relationship between input susceptance and phase shift. This linearity allows the use of the superposition principle, enabling all 2N phase states to be generated through simple switch combinations. Crucially, to simultaneously achieve accurate linearity at the center frequency and excellent phase flatness across the operating band, an adaptive gradient descent (AGD) algorithm is developed for parameter extraction. For validation, 3-bit (11.25°/22.5°/45°) and 4-bit (5.625°/11.25°/22.5°/45°) prototypes are fabricated and measured. A 4-bit design with an extended range (11.25°/22.5°/45°/90°) is also simulated, which further validates the capability of the star-shaped structure to achieve a large phase shift range. The measured results show excellent agreement with simulations, achieving maximum in-band phase errors below ± 5.5°, the figure of merit (FOM) better than 0.017 dB/°, return loss (RL) greater than 14 dB, and Root-Mean-Square (RMS) phase errors under 3.35° over 10% fractional bandwidth. Owing to the single-unit structure and the use of significantly fewer elements and switches, the proposed single-unit multibit RTPSs exhibit highly compact dimensions and lower IL.

title: Improved Prenatal Cardiac Anomaly Detection Using Enhanced Vision Transformer
authors: Vinita Yerande, D. N. Sonawane, C Y Patil
abstract: Noninvasive fetal electrocardiography (fECG) provides an important approach for monitoring fetal cardiac health during pregnancy. However, abdominal ECG recordings are often contaminated by maternal ECG components and various noise sources, making reliable fetal ECG extraction and analysis challenging. This study proposes Antenatal_VIT, an enhanced Vision Transformer framework designed for prenatal cardiac anomaly detection from fetal ECG signals. The proposed architecture extends the standard Vision Transformer by incorporating domain-specific adaptations for one-dimensional fetal ECG signals, including 1D temporal patch embedding, overlapping patch extraction to preserve beat continuity, learnable positional encoding for temporal ordering, morphology aware linear projection, and global self-attention for long-range cardiac rhythm modeling. Initially, fetal ECG signals are extracted from abdominal recordings using Independent Component Analysis while preserving important morphological characteristics. The recovered fECG signals are then analyzed using conventional machine learning classifiers as well as the proposed an enhanced Vision Transformer Antenatal_VIT framework. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed Antenatal_VIT model achieves 98.1% sensitivity, 97.5% specificity, and 98% classification accuracy, outperforming traditional classifiers including KNN, SVM, Decision Tree, and Random Forest. These results highlight the potential of transformer-based architectures for improving automated fetal ECG analysis and supporting early prenatal cardiac anomaly detection.

title: Modelling and Control of a 225 KW Grid-Interactive Bidirectional Fast DC Charger for Electric Vehicles
authors: Ali Chrakie, Moustapha El Hassan, Nagham El Ghossein
abstract: This paper presents the design and simulation-based evaluation of a 225 kW grid-interactive bidirectional fast DC charger for electric vehicles operating in both grid-to-vehicle (G2V) and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) modes. The proposed charger is developed in MATLAB/Simscape and integrates a two-level PWM AC/DC converter, a bidirectional DC/DC stage, an LCL-filtered grid interface, a regulated 1000 V DC-link, and a lithium-ion battery pack operating under CC and CC–CV control. The battery model and supervisory state-of-charge signal are reused from a previously validated electro-thermal and EKF-based battery framework and are embedded here within a charger-level architecture to study the coupled behavior of the battery, converter, and grid interface. Under nominal operation, the charger achieves near-unity power factor and low grid-current total harmonic distortion of 0.55% in G2V mode and 0.26% in V2G mode. The study also examines charger behavior under bidirectional mode transitions and disturbed-grid conditions including source-harmonic injection, voltage sag, frequency deviation, and reactive-power command tests. The results show stable bidirectional operation, preserved DC-link regulation, and acceptable current quality under both nominal and non-ideal conditions, providing a charger-level simulation reference for future real-time and experimental validation.

title: TriFuse-Net: A Tri-Branch PET/CT Fusion Pyramid Network Enhanced by Lesion-Guided Structural-Metabolic Attention for Lung Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis
authors: Yuyu Liu, Jieqin Lv, Fangfang Yang, Huiqin Wu, Xiang Pan, Li Wang, Han Bai, Shunfang Wang, Lijun Lu, Wenbing Lv
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Diagnosis and prognosis of lung cancer via PET/CT imaging have long been major clinical concerns. However, existing multimodal approaches often focus on feature aggregation rather than cross-modal interactive collaboration, failing to capture the structural-metabolic correlations and multi-scale synergy essential for characterizing complex lesions. Therefore, this study proposes TriFuse-Net, a tri-branch PET/CT fusion pyramid network (FPN) enhanced by lesion-guided structural-metabolic attention (LSMA) to improve both diagnosis and prognosis prediction tasks. The model is composed of two identical unimodal branches (PET/CT) and one pyramid branch with an interacting channel and spatial attention. The pyramid structure enables bidirectional multiscale feature extraction and fusion, capturing both local details and global semantic information of lesions. Comprehensive experiments validated the model's superiority across three clinical tasks. TriFuse-Net achieved a C-index of 0.747 for progression-free survival (PFS) prediction, showing improvements of 14.7% and 11.0% over ResNet-CT and ResNet-PET, respectively. Additionally, the clinical-integrated model (TriFuse-Net-Cli) achieved AUCs of 0.947 for differentiating lung cancer from tuberculosis and 0.937 for identifying lymph-node metastasis. Ablation studies further confirmed the essential contributions of both FPN and LSMA. In summary, the proposed framework demonstrates that integrating multi-scale structural-metabolic relationships significantly enhances diagnosis and prognosis in lung cancer.

title: Unified LoRA-Fine-Tuned LLM Framework for Interpretable Ship-to-Shore End-to-End Communication Latency Prediction in Inland Waterways
authors: Quan Liu, Zeting Xu, Kezhi Wang, Yangzhe Liao, Yi Han
abstract: Accurate and interpretable ship-to-shore end-to-end (E2E) communication latency prediction is important for safe and efficient inland waterway transportation. However, existing methods suffer from data sparsity, heterogeneity, and limited interpretability. Large language models (LLMs) show strong reasoning capabilities, but they usually perform poorly in numerical latency prediction. In this work, we propose a unified LLM-based framework that integrates high-dimensional environmental data representation, physically consistent data augmentation via a conditional diffusion model (CDM), and a tabular to question-answer transformation for low-rank adaptation (LoRA)-based LLM training. This unified framework achieves enhanced latency prediction performance while providing explicit environmental attribution and faithful natural-language explanations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms representative baselines across multiple metrics, reducing the mean absolute error by approximately 42% while providing reliable and interpretable predictions.

title: A Micromanipulation System for Spatial Single-Cell Isolation Using a 5-DoF Configuration-Adaptive Remote-Center-of-Motion Mechanism_supp3-3682556.mp4
authors: Hao Mo
abstract: Spatially resolved single-cell analysis of plant root tissues requires isolating target cells while preserving their original three-dimensional tissue context, which remains challenging due to microscopic size, structural fragility, and dense spatial distribution. This article presents an automated micromanipulation system featuring a five-degree-of-freedom remote-center-of-motion based micromanipulator with a lockable variable-stiffness joint, enabling configuration-dependent workspace transformation and precise tool-target alignment under constrained environments. A picoliter-resolution piezo-actuated pump provides gentle aspiration, while a YOLO-based detection network combined with confocal Z-stack scanning enables three-dimensional target localization. Experiments demonstrate a workspace expansion from 30° to 107°, an absolute positioning accuracy of 3.1 μm, a 95.5% isolation success rate, and an average pickup time of 8.7 s per cell. The proposed system enables accurate and repeatable isolation of spatially distributed protoplasts (2–30 μm in diameter) from densely populated root cap tissues, providing a practical foundation for automated and spatially tracked single-cell isolation in plant biology.

title: Fine-Grained Knowledge Concept Recommendation With Noise-Augmented Graph Contrastive Learning
authors: Huiru Yang
abstract: Students face massive learning resources on Massive Open Online Course (MOOC) platforms, making it difficult to rapidly identify knowledge content that matches their individual needs. Although recommendation systems are widely used to alleviate information overload and support personalized learning, most existing MOOC recommendation methods focus on the course level and fail to meet students’ needs for fine-grained knowledge concepts. To address this issue, this paper proposes a fine-grained knowledge concept recommendation algorithm based on noise-augmented graph contrastive learning (FGKCRec). FGKCRec constructs a heterogeneous information network and defines meta-paths to capture the associations between students and knowledge concepts from multiple perspectives. It further employs an attention mechanism to adaptively fuse multi-path semantic information and introduces noise-augmented cross-layer contrastive learning by injecting controlled perturbations into the propagation layers of LightGCN, thereby enhancing the robustness of node representations. Finally, it jointly optimizes the Bayesian Personalized Ranking loss for recommendation and the InfoNCE loss for contrastive learning to accomplish the knowledge concept recommendation task. The experimental results on the MOOCCubeX dataset show that FGKCRec outperforms multiple mainstream baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Diff-IR: A Non-Autoregressive End-to-End Framework for Generative Image Retrieval Via Diffusion Models
authors: Jian-Xin Ren, Ming-Bo Zhao, Zhao Zhang, Jicong Fan, Han Yan, Xuejiao Zhao
abstract: Diffusion models have recently shown remarkable generative and modeling capabilities across vision and audio domains. Motivated by this progress, we propose Diff-IR, a novel generative image retrieval framework that reformulates retrieval as a non-autoregressive sequence generation task. Instead of the conventional two-stage pipeline—feature extraction followed by similarity search—Diff-IR directly encodes each query image into a compact discrete sequence whose code uniquely matches database images. This end-to-end design eliminates explicit similarity computation. In Diff-IR, high-level semantics are first extracted via a pre-trained CLIP encoder. The query features and a noise sequence are then processed by a Transformer-based denoising network, which gradually reconstructs the target discrete representation. Experiments on three public benchmarks demonstrate that treating retrieval as a generative task with diffusion not only improves accuracy but also offers efficient, fully end-to-end retrieval. Our results highlight the strong potential of diffusion models for next-generation retrieval systems.

title: Optimal Energy Shaping and Force Amplification Framework for Task-Agnostic, Biomimetic Ankle Exoskeletons
authors: Katharine Walters, Gray C. Thomas, Robert D. Gregg
abstract: Task-agnostic controllers forpartial-assist lower-limb exoskeletons aim to reliably mimic biological torque while seamlessly adapting to changing movement patterns. However, current approaches relying on hidden state estimators or neural networks lack explainability and safety guarantees, while force amplification methods risk instability with an inherent trade-off between sensitivity and robustness to control inputs. Energy shaping control uses a kinematic model-based framework to provide predictable, stable assistance, though its traditional passive form limits biomimetic performance. Previous work relaxed the strict passivity requirements to improve biomimicry but reduced the stability guarantees. This paper presents an optimization-based extension of the energy-shaping control framework that combines the stability benefits of energy shaping with the intuitive biomimicry of force amplification. Our framework enables controlled trade-offs between sensitivity to changing human impedance and high performance through adjustable cost contributions of force amplification and model-based terms. We provide theoretical guarantees of closed-loop stability to an invariant set under human joint impedance control, supported by empirical validation of stability characteristics of an ankle exoskeleton under varying controller passivity constraints. A study of ten able-bodied participants using bilateral ankle exoskeletons demonstrates that the biomimetic controller reduced biological ankle torque by 19.1% across various activities of daily life.

title: Systematic Abductive Reasoning via Diverse Relation Representations in Vector-Symbolic Architecture
authors: Zhong-Hua Sun, Ru-Yuan Zhang, Zonglei Zhen, Da-Hui Wang, Yong-Jie Li, Xiaohong Wan, Hongzhi You
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: In abstract visual reasoning, monolithic deep learning models suffer from limited interpretability and generalization, while existing neuro-symbolic approaches fall short in capturing the diversity and systematicity of attribute and relation representations. To address these challenges, we propose a systematic abductive reasoning model with diverse relation representations (Rel-SAR) in vector-symbolic architecture (VSA) to solve Raven’s progressive matrices (RPM). To derive attribute representations with symbolic reasoning potential, we introduce not only various types of atomic high-dimensional (HD) encodings that capture numeric, periodic, and logical semantics, but also the structured HD representation (SHDR) for the overall grid component. For systematic reasoning, we further propose novel numerical and logical relation functions and perform rule abduction and execution in a unified framework built upon these relation representations. Experimental results demonstrate that Rel-SAR achieves significant performance improvements on RPM tasks. By synergistically combining HD attribute representations with symbolic reasoning, Rel-SAR enables systematic abductive reasoning with both interpretable and computable semantics.

title: Automatic Modulation Classification for Composite Radar Signals using Enhanced Time-Frequency Image Processing
authors: Ganghyuk Jeon, Geonho Song, Dongyeong Kim, Dongweon Yoon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Automatic modulation classification (AMC) plays a crucial role in non-cooperative contexts, a significance further amplified in contemporary cognitive radios, electromagnetic war fare, and spectrum surveillance with the advent of composite radar signals. Recently, deep learning (DL) has emerged as a prominent technology for AMC across various types of signals. This paper proposes a DL-based AMC for composite radar signals using enhanced time-frequency image (TFI) in non-cooperative contexts. To achieve this, we first identify the informative region (IR) within the TFIs of the received radar signals through a sequence of processing steps. This process differentiates key signal components, mitigates the influence of extraneous noise, and improves separability across modulation schemes. We then classify the modulation schemes of the composite radar signals by DL models extracting localized features from the IR, and analyze their classification performance. To validate the proposed method, we mathematically analyze the effect of the enhanced TFI processing on the classification. Furthermore, through comprehensive computer simulations on various DL models, we demonstrate that the pro posed method effectively classifies 37 types of single and composite radar modulation schemes, outperforming conventional approaches in terms of classification accuracy.

title: LRIG: A GAN-Based Lunar Rock Integrated Generator for Enhanced Robotic Autonomous Navigation
authors: Yihua Wei, Ting Yuan, Kuochu Chang, Aasem N. Alyahya, Chuankai Liu, Claudio M. Farias, Jiuchao Qian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The study presents the Lunar Rock Integrated Generator (LRIG), a cutting-edge generative adversarial network (GAN)-based framework, for generating realistic 3D lunar rock models with a wide spectrum of diversity. LRIG addresses the challenges of data insufficiency and unique geological characteristics of the lunar environment by integrating texture and geometry synthesis into a unified GAN architecture. The frame work incorporates various key innovations, such as a shared network backbone and a texture-geometry fusion module, to ensure consistency in both texture and geometry. To effectively and efficiently adapt Earth-based robotic AI approaches for uncommon remote Moon environments, LRIG introduces generic lunar rock modeling of high fidelity to seamlessly support exploration tasks such as lunar autonomous navigation (LAN). An experimental study demonstrates superior system performance of LRIG over existing state-of-the-art approaches in various metrics. The comparative and ablation results further validate the effectiveness of LRIG and highlight its potential for enhancing extraterrestrial exploration (including LAN) via high-fidelity planetary 3D surface reconstruction.

title: Greenland Ice Mass Change: Identifying the Drivers Behind the Recent Deceleration in Ice Mass Loss
authors: Qianyu Xiao, Jianli Chen, Yufeng Nie, Dongju Peng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: We derive Greenland ice mass changes from the Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment (GRACE) and GRACE Follow-On (GFO) mascon solutions and thirty Input-Output Method (IOM) combinations, and conduct a comprehensive comparison for the period 2002–2018. Using mascon solutions as constraints, we identify an optimal IOM combination to estimate the linear and quadratic trends for Greenland ice mass change, which enables us to extend the analysis to 2023. The IOM-estimated ice mass loss rate of −257.7 ± 19.8 Gt·yr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup> closely matches estimates from the three mascon solutions (−258.9 ± 13.2, −264.1 ± 12.0, and −271.8 ± 12.0 Gt·yr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>, respectively), provided by the Center for Space Research (CSR), Goddard Space Flight Center (GSFC), and Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL). A deceleration in ice mass losses is observed across all datasets, with rates of 3.2 ± 3.8, 4.5 ± 2.2, 3.8 ± 1.9, and 6.5 ± 2.2 Gt·yr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup> for the IOM combination, CSR, GSFC, and JPL mascon solutions, respectively. Partitioning results suggest that surface mass balance (SMB), solid ice discharge (SID), and peripheral glaciers' mass balance (PGMB) contribute about 41%, 50%, and 9%, respectively, to the total loss, with the deceleration mainly originating from SMB. Further decomposition of SMB reveals that runoff anomaly accounts for 136% of SMB-related ice mass loss, with 37% offset by positive precipitation anomaly. The observed deceleration in Greenland ice mass loss that has only been evident in recent years is temporally coherent with the quadratic increase in cumulative snowfall anomaly (4.1 ± 1.6 Gt·yr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup>).

title: Traffic Flow Prediction in Intelligent Transportation Systems Using LSTM with Attention Mechanism
authors: Hossein Pakparvar, S. Mojtaba Matinkhah, Arman Jamshidi
abstract: Accurate short-term traffic flow prediction is essential for Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) to support operational decisions such as congestion management, adaptive control, and route guidance. This paper targets ITS researchers and practitioners who require accurate yet deployable forecasting models for freeway monitoring. We propose an LSTM-based predictor augmented with a temporal attention mechanism that adaptively weights past observations to emphasize the most informative time steps under changing traffic regimes. The method is evaluated on real-world, 5-minute resolution freeway data from the Caltrans Performance Measurement System (PeMS) in a single-sensor setting, using both a widely used benchmark period (2016) and a recent period (2024) to assess robustness over time. Experimental results show that the proposed LSTM+Attention consistently improves prediction accuracy over a standard LSTM and several reference baselines. On the 2016 dataset, it achieves MAE = 3.8967 and MSE = 28.6424 (improvements of 0.69% and 2.07% over LSTM, respectively), and on the 2024 dataset it attains MAE = 5.1170 and MSE = 55.0752 (0.03% and 0.20% improvements). These results indicate that temporal attention provides a practical accuracy gain while maintaining millisecond-level inference latency, making it suitable for near-real-time ITS applications.

title: From the Editor
authors: N/A
abstract: This issue features two articles, the first of which is “Practical Experience with a Thermal Classification Test According to IEC 60034-18-31 for Type II Machine Insulation Systems,” by Hans Bärnklau et al. from VEM Sachsenwerk, Dresden, Germany, and Technische Universität Dresden, Dresden, Germany. This article examines the practical challenges of qualifying alternative insulation systems for high-voltage rotating machines in light of potential restrictions on anhydride-based materials under REACH regulations. The article discusses the implementation of the accelerated thermal aging test according to IEC 60034-18-31, highlighting its significant spatial, temporal, and resource demands despite being termed “accelerated.” Based on experimental experience, the authors analyze how test voltage level, mechanical conditioning, and specimen design—particularly strand number and overhang length in bar-type specimens—strongly influence mechanical stress and aging behavior. They demonstrate that variations in specimen geometry can lead to noncomparable results and may artificially accelerate time to failure when elevated thermal aging alone is not feasible. The article provides valuable practical insight for manufacturers and test laboratories navigating insulation system requalification under evolving regulatory and technical constraints.
