title: Evaluating Large Language Models in Engineering Education: A Multi-Course Assessment Across Bloom’s Taxonomy
authors: Ghazal Barari, Debarati Basu, Yuetong Lin, Jorge Ortega-Moody
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-5, Claude, and Grok are increasingly incorporated into engineering education, yet their effectiveness on discipline-specific cognitive tasks remains insufficiently characterized. Engineering learning relies heavily on conceptual understanding, multi-step problem solving, and higher-order reasoning, making it essential to evaluate how LLMs perform across these contexts. This study investigates the performance of three LLMs across four undergraduate engineering courses, Thermodynamics, Computer Programming, Systems and Control, and Circuits, using multiple-choice questions aligned with Bloom’s Taxonomy. We examine differences in accuracy and reasoning quality across cognitive levels, compare zero-shot and context-assisted prompting, and benchmark LLM performance against human learners in Computer Programming. Forty-eight instructor-designed questions (12 per course) spanning all Bloom levels were administered to each model. Responses were evaluated using accuracy, a rubric-based reasoning score, and a Composite Performance Index capturing completeness, efficiency, and quality of support. Human performance data were collected through an IRB-approved assessment with undergraduate students. Results show that Claude demonstrated the strongest overall performance and consistency, GPT-5 excelled in analytical reasoning, and Grok exhibited variable accuracy with limited higher-order reasoning. Providing contextual information improved GPT-5’s performance but had minimal impact on Claude or Grok. In Programming, Claude and GPT-5 outperformed human participants across all Bloom levels. These findings suggest that LLMs can support engineering learning when paired with instructional strategies emphasizing verification and critical evaluation. However, persistent limitations at higher cognitive levels highlight the importance of guided, responsible integration, supported by a practical instructional guide for effective LLM use across engineering tasks.

title: Stochastic Geometry Analysis of Uplink-Downlink Interference Asymmetry for Urban UAV-RC Networks
authors: Donggu Lee, Sung Joon Maeng, Ismail Guvenc
abstract: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) have emerged as a flexible platform for providing coverage over challenging environments, particularly for public safety and surveillance missions in urban areas. However, deploying the UAVs in dense urban areas introduces unique challenges, most notably asymmetric uplink (UL, remote controller to UAV) interference due to a higher chance of line-of-sight (LoS) interference at the UAV. In this letter, we propose a stochastic geometry (SG) framework to tractably analyze the large-scale asymmetric interference in urban areas. We incorporate a log-Gaussian Cox process (LGCP) model to capture the spatial correlation of the interference field in both UL and downlink (DL) as a function of the UAV altitude and the two-dimensional (2-D) distance between the remote controller and UAV. To quantify the UL and the DL interference asymmetry, we also define the UL-DL interference asymmetry ratio characterizing the interference disparity between the UL and the DL. Our numerical results demonstrate that the UL-DL interference asymmetry ratio increases and the channel capacity decreases as the UAV altitude and 2-D distance increase.

title: FairWeight: Private Fairness-aware Aggregation using Model Interpretation based Weightage in Federated Learning
authors: Harsh Kasyap, Ugur Ilker Atmaca, Carsten Maple, Nic Lane
abstract: Federated learning (FL) has been adopted as a promising solution for collaborative privacy-preserving machine learning. FL provides independence over the training data, but this can lead to statistical heterogeneity, which may result in issues such as bias and class imbalance. Thus, a global model, even with high accuracy, may be prone to discrimination and perform well only for certain sensitive attributes and classes. Global accuracy is not a perfect measure of the model's effectiveness; it should be reported in terms of balanced accuracy and fairness. Existing solutions attempt to mitigate bias using local or global debiasing techniques. However, local debiasing results in poor performance with increasing heterogeneity, and global debiasing is either designed only for achieving fairness, ignoring balanced accuracy, or costly in terms of finding an optimal solution. We present a novel model interpretation based bias mitigation aggregation scheme in FL, called FairWeight. It calculates the aggregation weightage by considering both fairness and loss for biased samples from the minority class. After optimizing the combined loss for prediction and fairness, the important weights responsible for bias and misclassification are calculated locally between clients. These weight indices are shared using secure aggregation to calculate the final aggregation weightage. Further, a secure aggregation scheme is devised that penalises biased weights across clients, rather than punishing the global model. FairWeight is evaluated on five real-world datasets and compared against five state-of-the-art fairness-aware aggregation schemes. The results provide empirical evidence that FairWeight achieves both balanced accuracy and fairness, with reduced computation and communication overheads.

title: Frequency-Spatial Semantic Decoupling for Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning
authors: Chaoyong Peng, Fan Wu, Wenwei Song, Zhixue Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: This paper proposes a Frequency-Spatial Semantic Decoupling Network (D3-Net: Dual-Domain Semantic Decoupling) for remote sensing image change captioning, aiming to effectively model semantic differences between bi-temporal images through explicit frequency–spatial feature decoupling. Specifically, D3-Net decomposes image representations into the frequency and spatial domains to enhance change-aware feature learning. A Frequency Domain Processing (FDP) module transforms features into the Fourier domain to capture global structural variations and large-scale change semantics, while a Spatial Domain Processing (SDP) module equipped with dense residual convolutions focuses on local structural details and fine-grained textures. The complementary representations from both domains are further refined via a cross-attention–based difference modeling mechanism. In addition, a Multi-Scale Dual-Domain Fusion (MSDF) module integrates multi-scale change features while suppressing irrelevant visual disturbances, thereby strengthening semantic consistency in change description generation. Extensive experiments conducted on the LEVIR-CC and Dubai-CC datasets demonstrate that D3-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance. On the LEVIR-CC dataset, the proposed model achieves 65.68% in BLEU-4, 40.74% in METEOR, 75.82% in ROUGE-L, and 140.52% in CIDEr, yielding improvements of +3.96% in CIDEr and +1.45% in S*m over the leading competitive method. Furthermore, visualization analysis indicates that D3-Net accurately captures genuine change regions while effectively suppressing pseudo-changes, maintaining high descriptive accuracy even under complex environmental conditions.

title: Fault Current Minimization and Fault Ride-Through Control of Triple Active Bridge DC-DC Converters Using Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Shubham Dhiman, Shrivatsal Sharma, Subhashish Bhattacharya
abstract: The fault ride-through (FRT) capability of triple active bridge (TAB) converters is crucial for maintaining reliable operation in DC Microgrids (MGs) during short-circuit faults (SCF). This paper proposes an optimization-based FRT control strategy for TAB DC–DC converters operating under DC SCF conditions. A generalized harmonic approximation (GHA) based steady-state fault model is utilized to characterize port current behavior during fault operation and to enable control-oriented evaluation of fault current stress. Based on this model, a particle swarm optimization (PSO) based triple phase shift (TPS) modulation strategy is proposed that minimizes cumulative RMS currents across all converter ports and maintains the required power transfer between healthy ports during FRT operation. The PSO algorithm is implemented as a background routine during normal operation to obtain optimized TPS modulation parameters. During fault conditions, the controller applies these pre-calculated settings directly, ensuring rapid response while preserving deterministic real-time control without additional CPU burden. Through simulation and experimental results, it is shown that proposed method has superior performance compared to existing methods using single phase shit (SPS) and double phase shift (DPS) modulations. The proposed strategy confirms its effectiveness in improving TAB converter fault resilience and operational continuity.

title: Edge Distributed Dynamic Line Rating through On Line Learning of Radial Basis Function Neural Networks for Temperature Prediction
authors: Danilo Pau, Emanuele Ogliari, Maciej Sakwa, Jinfei Han, Simone Tognocchi
abstract: In the context of energy transformation and a growing supply of renewable energy, additional flexibility of transmission lines is required. Dynamic Line Rating provides a solution to this question by allocating additional capacities in the power lines depending on the actual weather conditions. Modern implementations of Dynamic Line Rating are typically based on costly on-line sensors or unreliable weather stations mounted on pylons. This paper proposes a novel solution to the Dynamic Line Rating, by implementing a tiny machine learning approach suitable for integration into the sensor and a real-time decision algorithm for the detection of potential new allocation spots. The proposed forecasting methodology is based on a self-learning radial basis function model, that that forecasts the temperature of the conductor 5-, 10-, and 15 minutes into the future. The model can be accommodated on a cheap microcontroller device and is suitable for on-device self-calibration at the desired retraining frequency (RF). The forecasting results are promising, achieving mean absolute errors below 0.3 °C in the best test scenarios, a clear improvement over a naive persistence benchmark, and competitive performance when compared with the enhanced proposed Echo State Network (ESN). Subsequently, an algorithmic non-parametric Change Detection Test is executed on top of the forecaster to determine the possible points of change for dynamic allocation of the Over Head Line capacity, qualitatively and quantitatively demonstrating the potential capacity expansion compared to traditional Static Line Rating.

title: Fast GPU Inference with Unstructured-Pruned Sparse DNN Architectures
authors: Masahiko Ando, Keita Yamane, Takashi Oshima
abstract: Achieving fast and accurate deep neural network (DNN) inference on edge devices requires both aggressive model compression and efficient execution pipelines. This work introduces a sparsity‑aware compilation framework that integrates unstructured pruning with a hybrid SparseRT/TensorRT optimization flow. Using SynFlow‑based pruning at initialization, we produce models with over 99.9% sparsity while avoiding layer collapse. The resulting sparse networks are compiled into TensorRT engines through custom SparseRT kernels, fully eliminating CPU–GPU data‑transfer overhead and enabling end‑to‑end sparse execution. Applied to explainable deep one‑class classification (XDOC) for anomaly detection on MVTec AD dataset, our method achieves sub‑1‑ms inference on edge GPUs while preserving over 90% AUC accuracy. Replacing VGG11‑BN with RepVGG and incorporating CutPaste‑augmented learning further increases robustness under extreme pruning, achieving a parameter budget of 0.4% with a 0.98‑ms inference time. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining unstructured pruning, architecture selection, and compiler‑level integration for ultra‑low‑latency edge‑AI inference in post‑5G digital‑twin applications.

title: Model-free adaptive control-based electrical stimulation modulation system for upper limb bi-joint function
authors: Xiaoyan Shen, Yujie Gu, Wenxue Feng, Huanqing Zhao, Yuqi Zhang, Wennian Shao, Zhen Wu, Yuhua Zhao, Xiongheng Bian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) is a rehabilitation technique that helps restore or improve motor function by activating muscles through the distribution of electrical signals to nerves or muscles. However, because of the nonlinear and time-varying characteristics of muscle feedback to external electrical stimulation, achieving highly accurate real-time control is difficult. To precisely control the angles of the elbow joint and wrist joints of the upper limb simultaneously, this study adopted a model-free adaptive control (MFAC) algorithm to design the controller and improve the single-joint algorithm to achieve multi-joint control. A model-free adaptive control (RPPD-MFAC) algorithm with an improved pseudo-partial derivative estimation formula was adopted. The FES system designed based on this algorithm can track the trajectory of the reference joint angle. The algorithm effectively reduces the impact of the initial value of the pseudo-partial derivatives (PPD, a key parameter in the dynamic linearization of the MFAC algorithm) on the control performance such as the convergence rate and tracking accuracy. The system has successfully completed functional tests on both healthy individuals and hemiplegic patients, which is of great significance for the recovery and reconstruction of upper limb motor function.

title: Compress-Align-Detect: onboard change detection from unregistered images
authors: Gabriele Inzerillo, Diego Valsesia, Aniello Fiengo, Enrico Magli
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Change detection from satellite images typically incurs a delay ranging from several hours up to days because of latency in downlinking the acquired images and generating orthorectified image products at the ground stations; this may preclude real or near real-time applications. To overcome this limitation, we propose shifting the entire change detection workflow onboard satellites. This requires simultaneously solving challenges in data storage, image registration, and change detection with a strict complexity constraint. In this paper, we present a novel and efficient framework for onboard change detection that addresses the aforementioned challenges in an end-to-end fashion with a deep neural network composed of three interlinked submodules: (1) image compression, tailored to minimize onboard data storage resources; (2) lightweight co-registration of non-orthorectified multi-temporal image pairs; and (3) a novel temporally invariant and computationally efficient change detection model. This is the first approach in the literature combining all these tasks in a single end-to-end framework under the constraints dictated by onboard processing. Experimental results compare each submodule with the current state of the art and evaluate the performance of the overall integrated system in a realistic setting on low-power hardware. Compelling change detection results are obtained in terms of F1-score as a function of compression rate, sustaining a throughput of 0.7 Mpixel/s on a 15 W accelerator.

title: PointGrad: A Unified Spatially Varying Tensor Gradient Operator for Point Cloud Segmentation
authors: Rina Bao, Kannappan Palaniappan, Yunxin Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate representation of geometric shapes and structures plays a pivotal role in facilitating the recognition and segmentation of point clouds, particularly in remote sensing applications involving irregular, sparse and large-scale data. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), a widely used method for encoding geometric relationships among points through edge operators within a latent space projection. However, prevailing graph operators of point cloud within GNNs have not been explicitly tailored to incorporate directional gradient operations, which are crucial for capturing essential structural information, therefore, failed in challenging cases. To address this, we introduce a novel graph convolution operator, termed the Point Gradient (PointGrad) operator. PointGrad is meticulously crafted to effectively capture directional tensor gradients of individual points, subsequently mapping them into a high-dimensional, multi-scale tensor space. The PointGrad operator adeptly encodes diverse orders of partial derivatives, thereby comprehensively encapsulating multi-scale geometric shape details and curvature characteristics. Integrating the PointGrad operator into graph convolutions over distributed, irregular point sets helps capture pivotal structural particulars within the point cloud across varying spatial and feature scales to empower efficient learning. Furthermore, through the strategic stacking of PointGrad operators, we establish a family of deep networks known as PointGradNet. The comprehensive experimental analysis demonstrates the effectiveness and generalization of the proposed PointGrad operator across a range of benchmark datasets from varying in spatial scale, data sources, and task granularities, including urban scene airborne lidar point cloud segmentation, indoor scene semantic segmentation, and object part segmentation. Experiments on remote sensing benchmarks, including the IEEE DFT4 and COU2024 LiDAR datasets, demonstrate that PointGrad significantly improves segmentation accuracy and robustness in complex geospatial environments, providing a powerful, interpretable tool for scene understanding in earth observation and geoscience analytics. Codes is available at https://github.com/baorina/PointGradNet.

title: Intelligent Coordination and Control of Distributed Energy Resources, OLTC and SCB in Smart Grid Using X-DNN
authors: Tolulope David Makanju, Ali N Hasan, Thokozani Shongwe
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: The introduction of distributed energy resources (DERs) into distribution networks has caused a lot of challenges, such as power flow imbalance, voltage fluctuation, and operation control of voltage regulation devices. The optimization techniques used in power systems to address these issues have disadvantages, such as slow computational speed. To this end, we developed a two-stage control strategy for modern power system networks, wherein an Explainable-Deep neural network (X-DNN) controller was developed and trained using the output responses of centralized multi-objective AC optimal power Flows. The first stage involved the use of offline optimization methods by embedding a controller to generate optimal set points of the grid-connected inverter (GCI) and voltage-regulating devices' responses under the uncertainty of Photovoltaic (PV) power output and loading conditions, forming the training dataset for the X-DNN model. The trained X-DNN controller was deployed for real-time control, and the simulation of this approach was tested on the IEEE 69-bus system with a high penetration of PV grid-connected inverters (GCI), and compared with particle swarm optimization (PSO) and the genetic algorithim (GA). The results confirm that the X-DNN controller offers robust and efficient regulation, capable of maintaining system voltage stability by reducing the average voltage deviation by 34.7% and 56.69% compared to GA and PSO, respectively, and by 7.47% and 14.52% in active power losses for GA and PSO, respectively. Moreover, the X-DNN controller offers faster computational performance, making it a promising framework for smart power networks.

title: Corridor Design and Separation Definition in Advanced Air Mobility: Systematic Literature Review
authors: Evgenii Vinogradov, Debashisha Mishra, Mariam Ali Askar Alobeidli, Jamal Khaled Al Ali, Ahmed Saleh Alshehhi, Jennifer Simonjan, Enrico Natalizio
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) uses electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) vehicles to address urban congestion and emissions. However, corridor design, operation management, and separation standards remain underexamined for safe high-density operations. This paper applies the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines to systematically review relevant literature from IEEE Xplore and Web of Science, focusing on publications from 2010 to 2024. A Context, Intervention, Mechanism, and Outcome (CIMO) framework guided the development of research questions. After screening 2,039 journal and conference papers, 62 articles met the inclusion criteria. The findings reveal a lack of integrated corridor design approaches, limited operational strategies, and reliance on standards originally designed for conventional aviation. A unified corridor design and separation definition frameworks and taxonomies are proposed to address these shortcomings, informing future investigations and operational frameworks for safe, efficient eVTOL operation deployment in urban settings.

title: CACS: Climate-Adaptive State-of-Charge Control for Off-Grid Photovoltaic Systems
authors: Thais Ribeiro, Bruno Guazzelli Batista, Maycon Leone Maciel Peixoto, Bruno Tardiole Kuehne
abstract: Off-grid photovoltaic (PV) systems rely on battery banks to maintain supply continuity under solar intermittency. However, intensive cycling can accelerate aging and increase total system cost. This paper proposes the Climate-Adaptive State-of-Charge Control (CACS), a strategy that dynamically adjusts the effective lower state-of-charge (SOC) limit using a climate index <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$r(t)$ </tex-math></inline-formula> computed over a 24 h horizon, making discharge more conservative when low generation is expected. CACS was evaluated via hourly simulations using 2014–2024 climate data for Maria da Fé–MG, Brazil, considering a PV–maximum power point tracking (MPPT)–battery–inverter–load architecture and a full <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2^{3}$ </tex-math></inline-formula> factorial design (control strategy, maximum depth of discharge DoD<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\max }$ </tex-math></inline-formula>, and sizing). Performance was assessed using energy, reliability, and battery-stress indicators. Results indicate that DoD<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\max }$ </tex-math></inline-formula> and sizing dominate supply and reliability. When the control effect is isolated via pairwise comparisons, CACS does not increase mean annual served energy and incurs a limited supply penalty, while consistently reducing annual throughput, indicating lower cyclic stress. Therefore, CACS acts as an operational moderator and is particularly suitable for regimes with larger design margins in which battery preservation is prioritized.

title: A Fast-Locking Phase-Locked Loop With Joint Phase and Frequency Compensation Technique
authors: Pin-Cheng Chen, Wei-Teng Wang, Tai-Cheng Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: A fast-locking phase-locked loop (PLL) with a joint phase and frequency compensation technique is presented. With the proposed maximum phase error detector (MPED), the accumulated phase error can be well compensated right after frequency acquisition to improve the settling time of the PLL. The proposed PLL operating at a 0.9-V supply power was fabricated using a 28-nm CMOS process. The total settling time can be improved by 78% with a 200-ppm settling accuracy. The output frequency of the PLL is operated at 10GHz with a power consumption of 2.76mW.

title: A Single-Stage Single-Coil Wireless Multi-Channel Adiabatic Supply Stimulation System for Multiple Source Current Steering Deep Brain Stimulation
authors: Hyun-Su Lee, Kyeongho Eom, Joonghoon Kang, Hojae Chon, Taewoo Kim, Jaesoon Joo, Taekyung Kim, Young-Min Shon, Hyung-Min Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Circuits and Systems
abstract: This paper presents a single-stage single-coil wireless multi-channel adiabatic supply stimulation (M-CASS) system that generates four separate adiabatic supplies to independently regulate four constant stimulus currents, without using power-hungry current sources, regardless of stimulus current and load impedance. A wireless power distributor, combined with an AC-DC quadruple-output current regulator, independently controls four stimulus channels while receiving AC power from a single wireless link. A channel-optimized size control technique and a wide adaptive offset control scheme are employed to enhance the peak AC-to-stimulation efficiency in wireless adiabatic stimulation. The 2.72 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> 0.25-μm CMOS M-CASS demonstrates four independently controlled stimulus currents with four adiabatic supplies over a 6.78-MHz resonant link. Furthermore, M-CASS achieves a peak AC-to-stimulation efficiency of 82.2% when steering four 2-mA currents and maintains approximately 80% efficiency even when the four channels experience different impedances and currents. These features of M-CASS using a single coil provide a compact and energy-efficient solution for multiple-source current-steering (MSCS) deep brain stimulation (DBS) applications.

title: Duty Ratio Based Model Predictive Flux Control With Direct Switching-State Selection for Induction Motor Drives
authors: Kaif Ahmed Lodi, Adil Mohammed, Jamal Yousuf Alsawalhi, Khaled Ali Al Jaafari, Noureddine Harid, Abdul R. Beig
abstract: This article presents a low-complexity model predictive flux control (MPFC) scheme for induction motor drives that achieves the ripple-reduction benefits of duty-ratio-based MPFC with a reduced computational burden. The inverter switching state is obtained directly in the three-phase (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{abc}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) frame from the signs of resistance-compensated per-phase stator flux errors, and the adjacent active vector is determined by a one-bit leg toggle that minimizes projection loss. A constant-projection boundary then selects either the zero vector or the adjacent active vector as the secondary voltage vector in each sampling interval. Duty ratios are computed from the scalar projections, and the selected vectors are applied symmetrically, resulting in two commutations per sampling interval and a near-constant switching frequency. The proposed MPFC algorithm is validated against both simulation and experimental results.

title: Towards Robust and Fair Partial Label Federated Learning Service
authors: Sen Fu, Wei Bao, Zhengjie Yang, Xinyi Sheng, Yichen Guo, Yuqin Liu, Bing Bing Zhou
abstract: Machine learning services require accurately annotated datasets for AI training, which are often expensive and difficult to obtain. Weakly supervised learning, particularly Partial Label Learning (PLL), reduces costs by requiring only candidate label sets. However, most existing PLL works focus primarily on improving predictive performance, often neglecting potential security threats under such weakly supervised conditions. In this work, we study these threats within Federated Learning (FL), a distributed paradigm enabling privacy-preserving machine learning services. Specifically, we extend PLL to Partial Attribute Partial Label Learning (PAPL), where each sample is associated not only with partial labels but also with partial attributes, better reflecting real-world service environments involving uncertain or incomplete data collection. We then propose three innovative adversarial attacks that exploit attribute-label correlations and mixup strategies to compromise the fairness of models in FL services. To mitigate these threats, we propose FairFedPAPL, a gradient inversion-based defense mechanism that reconstructs representative pseudo-datasets to approximate each client's local distribution, enabling accurate identification of adversarial participants. Our analytical and empirical results demonstrate that the proposed attacks significantly compromise model fairness, while our defense effectively identifies attackers, preserving robustness and fairness in weakly supervised FL.

title: SARPCNet: Spaceborne SAR Feature Extraction Network Coupled with Phase Characteristics
authors: Xin Li, Yan Huang, Mingjun Deng, Taoyang Wang, Guo Zhang, Yin Yang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The feature extraction task for spaceborne SAR image matching faces the challenge of maintaining stable feature performance under different imaging conditions. The existing feature extraction networks mainly rely on amplitude domain features, which are easily affected by scattering and speckle noise in complex terrain. However, the phase characteristics of spaceborne SAR images have structural invariance and strong noise resistance, which can more reliably retain the geometric details that are often lost in the amplitude domain. To address this issue, a feature extraction network coupled with SAR phase characteristics is proposed, which combines the amplitude information and phase information of SAR images. The amplitude feature maps of SAR images are first extracted through a shallow feature extraction module. Subsequently, a phase fusion module is employed to couple the shallow features with phase information, thereby enriching the spatial semantic representation and enhancing structural sensitivity. The fused multi-dimensional SAR features are then further encoded and represented via a deep feature extraction module. In addition, phase consistency is incorporated in the form of a loss function to improve the stability of phase feature extraction. The loss function is computed based on the residual matrix between the feature maps before and after registration. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a matching accuracy better than 1.5 pixels, outperforming current state-of-the-art SAR image matching algorithms, while yielding several-fold improvements in the number of correctly matched points compared with the competing methods. The robustness and dependability of the suggested method for SAR image feature extraction are confirmed by these results.

title: OIRS-assisted VLC Channel Optimization Against UAV Blockage Based on Two-Stage Machine Learning Framework
authors: Hanyang Shi, Chen Xu, Song Zhang, Gan Luo, Yan Zhao
abstract: The dynamic blocking caused by unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) severely reduces the channel reliability of visible light communication (VLC), where conventional multipath diversity is unavailable. To address this, we propose an optical intelligent reflecting surface (OIRS)-assisted robust VLC framework against UAV blockage. To simulate and quantify blockage, we establish a unified channel-optical field-blocking model integrating: (i) a Gaussian-approximated optical power distribution for OIRS mirror units, derived from far-field-filtered Lambertian emissions; (ii) a probabilistic blockage model using Poisson point processes to quantify UAV-induced blockages. We formulate the optimization of light field distribution on OIRS as a dynamic optimization problem to maximize the user’s received power under blockage constraints by adjusting the deflection angle of VLC access point (AP). We propose a two-stage machine learning approach to solve this dynamic optimization problem. In the offline stage, we employ greedy combinatorial optimization to precompute diverse OIRS beam configurations for building a configuration database. In the online stage, we first detect occluded mirror units using sparse recovery, and then dynamically adjust the beam aiming center based on real-time conditions through database guidance selection. The simulation results show that our solution can significantly improve the actual anti-blocking performance under high-density mirror unit configuration compared to static state, random enable, and gradient descent.

title: Vacuum Induced Transparency, Vacuum Rabi Splitting, and Exceptional Points in a Quantum Emitter Near a Graphene Nanodisk
authors: George E. Paspalakis, Adamantios P. Synanidis, Vasilios Karanikolas, Andreas F. Terzis, Ioannis Thanopulos, Emmanuel Paspalakis
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: This work studies the absorption spectrum of a two-level quantum emitter coupled to a photonic environment modified by the plasmon modes of a graphene nanodisk. Using macroscopic quantum electrodynamics and a Lorentzian mapping, the system is described within an effective cavity quantum electrodynamics framework. Analytical expressions are derived for the linear susceptibility, and three distinct spectral regimes are identified. In vacuum induced transparency, destructive quantum interference opens a narrow transparency window in the absorption spectrum. In vacuum Rabi splitting, coherent energy exchange between the emitter and the plasmonically modified photonic mode produces a well-resolved doublet. When plasmon losses are large, the required interference is suppressed, leading to the absence of induced optical transparency. It is shown that the transitions between these regimes are strictly determined by the exceptional points of the system's effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian, and a complete phase diagram is provided as a function of the emitter-nanodisk distance and the free-space decay rate. The results highlight graphene nanodisks as a highly tunable platform for the dynamic control of quantum interference, offering potential applications in reconfigurable nanophotonic spectral switching and transparency-window engineering.

title: Optimization of All-Optical Phase-Controlled Phase Modulators
authors: Te-Chih Sung, Chia-Hua Chan, Chii-Chang Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: In this work, we aim to optimize the all-optical phase-controlled phase modulators that incorporate lasers, optical directional couplers, optical amplifiers, and phase shifters. Two types of the systems are studied: linear type and sinusoidal type in which the phase of the output light beam of the systems follows a linear and a sinusoidal dependence on the phase difference between the input light and the control light. Using the inverse design approach based on the 2-layer multilayer perceptron through the pseudoinverse of matrix, the coupling ratio of directional couplers and the phase delay of the phase shifters of the systems can be obtained. The standard errors of phase and power between the output modulated light and the target output light are less than those of our previous reports. The non-linear phase-controlled phase modulator could be applied in optical neural networks as an activation function.

title: IDEA: Automated Design Space Exploration for Visualization Design_supp2-3684920.mp4
authors: Nan Cao
abstract: Design spaces serve as a conceptual framework that enables designers to explore feasible solutions, yet their lack of computational formalization limits integration with AI-driven design automation. To address this, we introduce a structured design space model that formalizes design spaces with orthogonal dimensions and discrete elements, making them machine-interpretable and executable. Building on this model, we present IDEA, a fully automated design exploration framework to generate effective outcomes based on user requirements and design spaces. Specifically, IDEA leverages large language models (LLMs) for constraint generation, incorporates a constraint-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) algorithm to explore the space, and instantiates abstract decisions into domain-specific implementations. We evaluate IDEA in two design scenarios: data-driven article and standard visualization, supported by blind ratings, expert interviews, and quantitative experiments. Results demonstrate the IDEA's adaptability across domains and its capability to produce high-quality designs.

title: DebriCLIP: Dual-Prior Guided Few-Shot Segmentation of Urban Pavement Debris
authors: Xinxiao Wang, Luyue Sun, Sijia Hu, Yanli Yang, Kailang Hao, Ming Gao, Jian Pu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Robust segmentation of urban pavement debris is essential for intelligent road-cleaning systems. Few-shot segmentation offers a promising solution under limited annotation, but existing methods struggle with cluttered backgrounds and irregular debris structures. We propose DebriCLIP, a dual-prior guided framework for few-shot segmentation of urban pavement debris. The framework leverages two complementary mechanisms: the dual visual prior (DVP) and the adaptive textual prior (ATP). DVP enhances visual cues by modeling foreground and background similarities separately, then fusing them to generate the final visual prior. This helps focus on relevant areas while suppressing background noise. ATP aligns visual features with textual semantics using learnable prompts, refining pixel-level alignment and improving segmentation accuracy. A global consistency loss is introduced to stabilize training. We construct the first urban pavement debris few-shot segmentation dataset, UPD, using a hybrid annotation pipeline to reduce labeling costs. Experiments on UPD, PASCAL-5<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sup>, Cityscapes, and ADE20 K validate DebriCLIP's effectiveness. It achieves state-of-the-art results on UPD, with mIoU improvements of 4.6% (1-shot) and 6.9% (5-shot) over the baseline. DebriCLIP also achieves a competitive 76.4% mIoU on PASCAL-5<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sup> and demonstrates superior generalization on ADE20 K and Cityscapes, confirming its strong applicability across both domain-specific and general few-shot segmentation tasks.

title: Cross-Domain Fault Diagnosis of Aero-Engines Under Imbalanced Source and Few-Shot Conditions
authors: Ying Zhao, Hao Wang, Changyi Xu, Chao Zhang
abstract: Intelligent fault diagnosis of aero-engines is persistently challenged by the scarcity of fault samples and severe class distribution imbalance. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel fault diagnosis method termed the cross-domain few-shot fault diagnosis under imbalanced source (CDFI). By leveraging a meticulously designed joint learning objective, this method effectively overcomes performance degradation caused by data scarcity, class imbalance, and domain shift. Specifically, an adaptive weighting mechanism (AWM) is proposed to dynamically adjust class weights based on the learning state, effectively mitigating bias from dominant classes to ensure minority features are fully mined. In addition, a dual-objective contrastive learning (DOCL) loss is introduced to collaboratively facilitate intra-domain structure preservation and cross-domain alignment, yielding robust discriminative and domain-invariant features. Furthermore, a random composite data augmentation (RCDA) strategy is employed to augment scarce target samples via multi-dimensional signal synthesis, thereby enhancing data diversity and training stability. Validations on both simulated aero-engine and real-world physical datasets confirm that CDFI outperforms advanced baselines, maintaining high diagnostic accuracy on hard-to-classify samples under domain shift.

title: A Multidimensional Assessment of Embodied Interaction in Large-Scale Full-Body Interactive Environments_supp2-3682920.pdf
authors: Sena Beste Ercan
abstract: As Mixed Reality (MR) systems evolve toward richer, full-body interactive experiences, understanding how spatial configurations shape user behavior becomes essential for designing usable, engaging, and cognitively coherent environments. This paper presents the design and evaluation of three MR mini-games developed to investigate key embodied interaction properties: (1) territoriality and social distance, (2) proprioception and kinaesthesia, and (3) navigation and wayfinding. Implemented across four differently scaled MR footprints, the games were tested with participants from three age groups, children, adolescents, and young adults, to assess how interaction space size influences bodily coordination, spatial negotiation, and social behavior. Using a multimodal analysis approach, we analyze behavioral patterns that emerge at different scales, revealing how users' interaction strategies change based on space. Our findings contribute to the human-centered design of MR systems by offering a behavioral lens on how scale influences user experience, embodiment, and interaction quality. The study informs future applications in education, social interaction, and rehabilitation, where adaptive MR design is critical for inclusive and effective engagement.

title: Multimodal Integration of A Novel Gait State Time Interval Signal Generation Method and Insole Sensor Data-based Body Intelligence: Application in Parkinson's Disease
authors: Hao Li, Illa Baryskievic, Anatoliy Baryskievic, Viktar Tsviatkou
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Multidomain and multimodal identification of the walking gait-cycle states is important for detecting and monitoring locomotion disorders such as Parkinson's disease (PD). We propose a novel multizonal clustering and multi-level thresholding method based on analyzing multizonal plantar load distribution for generating a discrete gait-state time-interval (GSTI) signal to improve PD diagnosis accuracy and the effectiveness of rehabilitation through personalized strategies. Multidomain analysis of the GSTI signal shows a novel coupled I.Baryskievic-H.Li bio-oscillator interpreted as a GSTI-derived signal-level oscillatory signature that may be associated with a central nervous system (CNS)-related locomotor rhythm organization. The bio-oscillator consists of two interconnected oscillations with distinct resonant spectral peaks at specific natural frequencies and phase coupling (nonlinearity) between two frequency components. We propose a multidomain feature level of layered Integrative Body Intelligence (IBI) framework to identify lower and higher-order interactions between gait cycle states. The proposed multimodal data level of IBI involves the proposed acoustic and visual biofeedback based on a novel acoustic harmonic plantar pressure model and a 3D gait state portrait of the GSTI signal used for walking gait monitoring and personalized rehabilitation assessment in PD. Experiments on a publicly available PD plantar-insole dataset show that the Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) model based on the selected multidomain (time-interval, spectral, and bispectral) feature subset achieves classification accuracy (94.44%), and offers a trade-off between model complexity and performance for PD recognition. This result suggests that it is possible to accurately diagnose early-stage PD through merely testing patients' GSTI signal.

title: Fact or Fake? Assessing the Role of Deepfake Detectors in Multimodal Misinformation Detection
authors: A S M Sharifuzzaman Sagar, Mohammed Bennamoun, Farid Boussaid, Naeha Sharif, Lian Xu, Shaaban Sahmoud, Ali Kishk
abstract: In multimodal misinformation, deception usually arises not just from pixel-level manipulations in an image, but from the semantic and contextual claim jointly expressed by the image–text pair. Yet most deepfake detectors, engineered to detect pixel-level forgeries, do not account for claim-level meaning, despite their growing integration in automated fact-checking (AFC) pipelines. This raises a central scientific and practical question: Do pixel-level detectors contribute useful signal for verifying image–text claims, or do they instead introduce misleading authenticity priors that undermine evidence-based reasoning? We provide the first systematic analysis of deepfake detectors in the context of multimodal misinformation detection. Using two complementary benchmarks such as MMFakeBench and DGM4, we evaluate: (i) state-of-the-art image-only deepfake detectors, (ii) an evidence-driven fact-checking system that performs tool-guided retrieval via Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and engages in deliberative inference through Multi-Agent Debate (MAD), and (iii) a hybrid fact-checking system that injects detector outputs as auxiliary evidence. Results across both benchmark datasets show that deepfake detectors offer limited standalone value, achieving F1 scores in the range of 0.26–0.53 on MMFakeBench and 0.33–0.49 on DGM4, and that incorporating their predictions into fact-checking pipelines consistently reduces performance by 0.04–0.08 F1 due to non-causal authenticity assumptions. In contrast, the evidence-centric fact-checking system achieves the highest performance, reaching F1 scores of approximately 0.81 on MMFakeBench and 0.55 on DGM4. Overall, our findings demonstrate that multimodal claim verification is driven primarily by semantic understanding and external evidence, and that pixel-level artifact signals do not reliably enhance reasoning over real-world image–text misinformation.

title:
Development of a Non-enzymatic Potentiometric and Voltammetric Lactate Sensor Enhanced by ZnO/Co
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>4</sub>
                    /MB/SnO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    on Flexible Printed Circuit Board
authors: Yu-Hsun Nien, Xin-Han Chen, Jung-Chuan Chou, Chih-Hsien Lai, Po-Yu Kuo, Po-Hui Yang, Yu-Wei Chen, Lian-Sheng Jiang
abstract: A non-enzymatic lactate sensor is successfully developed by integrating ZnO/Co₃O₄/methylene blue (MB)/SnO₂ composite onto flexible printed circuit board (FPCB). ZnO provides a wide bandgap and surface area for adsorption, Co₃O₄ contributes p-type semiconducting behavior to promote charge separation, and MB serves as an effective redox mediator to accelerate electron transfer. Therefore, these materials form a highly responsive and stable sensing interface suitable for both the potentiometric and voltammetric detection modes. The sensor demonstrates good selectivity against common interfering biomolecules typically found in physiological fluids, ensuring reliable lactate detection in complex environments. In addition, the device remains consistent performance over an extended operational lifespan, confirming its mechanical and electrochemical stability for long-term use. The lactate sensor demonstrates a broad linear detection range (0.01–100 mM), with a high sensitivity of 21.9 μA/decade and excellent linearity (R² = 0.999), highlighting its strong potential for accurate and reliable lactate monitoring across a broad concentration spectrum.

title: Extending the optical absorption in a lumped element meander structure to far-infrared wavelengths
authors: Shekhar Chandra Pandey, Shilpam Sharma, Anudeep Singh, Utkarsh Pandey, S. S. Prabhu, Bhaskar Biswas, Sona Chandran, M. K. Chattopadhyay
abstract: Superconducting radiation detectors typically exhibit detection and single-photon sensitivity limited to the mid-infrared wavelength range. Extending their detection capabilities into the far-infrared range (>10 μm) requires careful selection of substrate materials and detector geometries. The overall detection efficiency of such detectors is inherently linked to their absorption and coupling efficiencies. In this study, the resonator geometry and its absorption efficiency were estimated using electromagnetic simulations in CST Microwave Studio simulation software for a lumped-element meander structure. Simulations were performed over the 12–50 μm wavelength range, which corresponds to the Infrared Free-Electron Laser (IR-FEL) at RRCAT, Indore. The absorption in the meander inductor was significantly influenced by the substrate material and thickness, as well as the impedance matching between the detector structure and the incident photon medium. The results indicate that SiO2 and diamond substrates are well-suited for developing lumped element kinetic inductance detectors (LEKID) in the above wavelength range. Optimised meander geometries on diamond substrates demonstrated absorption efficiencies of up to 95% for narrow bandwidths and greater than 50% for wide bandwidths. Based on the simulations, a 30-pixel LEKID structure was fabricated by electron-beam lithography on a 500 μm SiO2-coated Si substrate. The resonator was made from a 20 nm-thick Ti40V60 alloy thin film. Optical characterization was also performed through simultaneous transmission and reflection measurements of the 30-pixel LEKID. Simulation studies demonstrate that the designed LEKID structure is well-suited for detecting far-infrared wavelengths above 10 μm, offering high absorption efficiency.

title: MAC Protocol for ISAC in Underwater Acoustic Networks with Dynamic Target Monitoring-Localization-Tracking
authors: Zhihui Duan, Xiaoxiao Zhuo, Liang Tang, Yu Zhao, Fengzhong Qu
abstract: This paper proposes a multiple access control (MAC) protocol for integrated sensing and communication in underwater acoustic networks (ISAC-UANs) with dynamic target monitoring, localization, and tracking, where some ISAC nodes are deployed to collaboratively communicate and detect targets via integrated underwater acoustic signals. The proposed integrated underwater sensing and communication (USAC)-MAC protocol focuses on addressing the challenges of packet collisions and resource contention in the dual-function network. Specifically, the protocol is divided into three stages: target monitoring, target localization, and the target tracking phase. The target monitoring phase is carried out to initialize the network topology and search for detection targets. The conflict-free concurrent transmission constraints are established based on topological information to enable conflict-free and efficient data transmission. After detecting the target, the protocol transfers into the target localization phase, which localizes the target applying the time of arrival (TOA) method. We model the optimization problem to minimize the Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) and employ the particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm to solve it under the constraint of the geometric position of nodes. During the target tracking phase, we enhance target detection accuracy by dynamically selecting detection node clusters based on predicted target trajectories. Concurrently, we schedule packet transmission times to minimize the communication frame length. Thereby, a joint optimization problem for the CRLB of localization and the frame length of communication is established and addressed by the simulated annealing (SA) algorithm, ensuring detection precision while reducing communication latency. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed protocol exhibits superior performance with higher throughput, shorter frame length, shorter information update times, and more precise detection accuracy.

title: Finetune Chiplet Design Floorplan via KuanNet
authors: Xiaomeng Wang, Zhen Zhou, Yang Yi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Chiplet-based architectures require efficient through-silicon via (TSV) assignment to optimize interconnect performance and system integration. Unlike traditional 3-D integrated circuits, heterogeneous chiplet systems demand coordination across dies with varying sizes and functionalities, creating exponentially complex solution spaces that challenge existing optimization methods. This article introduces knowledge-unified attention neural network (KuanNet), a multiagent reinforcement learning (RL) framework integrating echo state networks (ESNs) with attention mechanisms for chiplet TSV assignment. The key innovation is a knowledge-unified architecture with temporal-static decomposition: temporal features shared across agents are processed through both ESN reservoirs and skip connections, while static features remain agent-private, enabling coordinated decisions with temporal memory and spatial awareness. Building on multiagent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG) with K-head attention critics, KuanNet demonstrates superior optimization performance over the state-of-the-art baseline across standard benchmark circuits of varying scale and complexity. Ablation studies validate individual component contributions of the KuanNet architecture.

title: Stents, Smart Stents and Their Importance for Wireless Biomedical Applications
authors: Deepti Sharma, Toni Björninen
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: A stent is a renowned choice for treating coronary artery disease and other diseases related to artery or vessel blockages. It is a meshed, tube-like supporting scaffold inserted into arteries or vessels where blood flow has become critically restricted to restore normal blood flow. The progressive success of stents has led to the emergence of wireless smart stents. They integrate sensors to perform wireless monitoring of the artery or blood vessel for any obstruction of the blood due to in-stent restenosis (ISR). Additionally, the wireless interface also assists in the wireless powering of the stent. The therapeutic uses of smart stents to cure ISR, explored by many research groups, are also discussed. Particularly, we focus on different types of sensors and wireless technologies used for sensing and wireless transmission of arterial health and overview the limitations of various sensing and wireless technologies of smart stents. Finally, the emerging future technologies that are envisioned to improve present smart stent technology is reviewed.

title: Joint Optimization of AoI and Energy Harvesting in Air-Ground Integrated MEC Based on Multi-Objective Evolutionary Reinforcement Learning
authors: Qixun Yang, Fuhong Song, Huanlai Xing, Xu Chen, Zhiwen Xiao, Xi Yu, Qingwei Meng, Mingsen Deng
abstract: This paper investigates the joint optimization of the age of information (AoI) and energy harvesting (JOAE) problem in an air-ground integrated mobile edge computing (MEC) system, where an unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) functions as a wireless information transfer platform or a wireless power transfer platform during its flight mission. The JOAE problem is defined as a multi-objective optimization problem, aims at minimizing the average AoI of intelligent devices and maximizing the average energy harvested by them, simultaneously. The problem is highly challenging due to its conflicting objectives, making it difficult to solve using conventional single-objective optimization approaches. Based on proximal policy optimization, we propose a multi-objective evolutionary reinforcement learning approach that includes two-performance improving strategies. The first strategy is the Pareto trajectory guiding that adopts imitation learning to refrain the reinforcement learning process of an agent from escaping local optima. The second strategy is genetic fine-tuning which performs genetic operations to fill the gaps in the coverage of the non-dominated solution set. The experiment results show that the proposed approach obtains many outstanding non-dominated solutions and strikes a better balance between objectives compared with several algorithms.

title: CLIP-SA: CLIP-Guided Semantic Alignment for Generalizable Face Anti-spoofing
authors: Hui Ma, Ajian Liu, Shuai Xue, Yunfeng Diao, Dashuang Liang, Hailun Liang, Yanyan Liang, Jun Wan, Xun Yang, ZhenZhen Hu, Zhen Lei
abstract: Domain Generalization of Face Anti-Spoofing (DG-FAS) is increasingly becoming an important basis for model applications. Previous methods usually endeavor to project a domain-invariant feature space, which may not necessarily exist or be extremely difficult to explore. In this work, borrowing a solution from the large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) to replace direct editing of visual features, we propose a novel CLIP-Guided Semantic Alignment (CLIP-SA) for DG-FAS, which can guide visual feature alignment for generalization by leveraging the smoothness of the dense space of textual features. Specifically, in the textual branch, we design a Fine-Grained Prompt Template (FGPT) consisting of two language concepts, i.e., content and category, as supplements to visual features. Thus, the generalization of visual features can be improved by enhancing the model's understanding and association of content and category semantics. In the image encoder, we introduce a learnable Domain-Aware Adapter (DA-Adapter), which acts as a parameter-efficient transfer learning (PETL) method to transfer the pre-trained CLIP for the FAS task. Furthermore, the DA-Adapter contains an attention-based pooling layer that aggregates domain-invariant semantics as adaptive features for the output visual features. Finally, Our CLIP-SA demonstrates strong robustness to physical attacks on cross-domain benchmarks (ICMO, SCW), while also achieving excellent generalization against digital adversarial perturbations and on multiple Deepfake datasets.

title: An Improved Coregistration Strategy Integrating Local Iterative Pixel Offset Tracking and Weighted ESD for Sentinel-1 TOPS Mode DInSAR Measurement in Non-Stationary Scenarios
authors: Huaishuai Wang, Hongjun Song, Jili Wang, Yulun Wu, Heng Zhang, Xuxin Zhang, Lianshuo An
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Sentinel-1 (S1) satellites, operating at C-band in Terrain Observation by Progressive Scans (TOPS) mode, provide unprecedented opportunities for measuring large-scale tectonic motions using Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR). However, the azimuth-varying Doppler centroid imposes stringent coregistration requirements, for which the Enhanced Spectral Diversity (ESD) method is typically employed to achieve the required high-precision azimuth coregistration. In practical applications, however, the ESD method is inevitably affected by various disturbances, especially by pronounced large-scale and non-stationary azimuth deformation. Such disturbances not only degrade the coregistration accuracy of the ESD method but also cause phase jumps at burst edges, thereby complicating the subsequent phase unwrapping. To address these issues, this study proposes an improved coregistration strategy that integrates the Local Iterative Pixel Offset Tracking (LI-POT) and Weighted ESD (WESD) to mitigate the effects of azimuth deformation and enhance the robustness of the ESD method. Furthermore, the Coherence Gradient Consistency (CGC) indicator is introduced to embed the proposed method into the standard processing workflow, enabling automated selection of appropriate coregistration strategies. The effectiveness of the proposed method was validated using S1 TOPS repeat-pass data acquired over four representative regions. By applying the proposed method, the phase jumps observed in the conventional processing were effectively corrected, demonstrating its superior accuracy and stability in complex deformation scenarios.

title: Mapping different soliton state in the automatic spatiotemporal mode-locked fiber laser based on convolutional neural network
authors: Haoxue Qiu, Qiwen Bao, Yong Yao, Yu Yang, Linguang Guo, Songting Li, Chengda Ge, Jinwei Zhang, Xiaochuan Xu, Jiajun Tian, Jianan Duan
abstract: A novel approach combining deep learning (DL) model with traditional mode-locked techniques is proposed to achieve the dissipative solitons (DSs) and noise-like pulses (NLPs) automatic spatiotemporal mode-locked fiber laser (MLFL). By utilizing the convolutional neural network (CNN), mapping between the rotation angle of the electric polarization controller (EPC), the output spectrum with different states of solitons and the complex beam profile is first established. Analysis of the training and testing loss curves demonstrates that the DL model effectively learns the latent spatial-spectral features necessary for accurate prediction. When supplied with random beam profiles, predicting the corresponding spectral characteristics and the rotation angle of the EPC almost perfectly matches with the experimental results. The proposed DL model enables the accurate reproduction of the rotation angle of the EPC and output spectrum with different types of soliton, and facilitating the automatic tuning of EPC can enhance stable mode-locked state in the experiment, thereby promoting the progress of the automatic fiber laser system.

title: Construction and Post-Failure Reconstruction of Virtual Backbone Based on Regional Risk Difference in Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Jingyu Gan, Chen Guo, Chongxiang Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: In wireless sensor networks (WSNs), virtual backbones (VBs) are widely employed to address issues such as energy constraints and broadcast storms. WSNs are typically modeled as unit disk graphs (UDGs); a VB for data transmission is determined based on the construction of a connected dominating set (CDS) in the graph. Since sensor nodes may fail due to accidental damage or energy depletion, it is necessary to construct a CDS with fault tolerance. In fact, under the influence of complex terrain, significant altitude differences, and environmental perturbations caused by multiple factors, application scenarios frequently have significant differences in failure risk between nodes in different regions. Based on this observation, we optimize the network structure by constructing different CDS types in regions with varying risk factors, introducing the concept of a regional risk difference connected dominating set (RRD-CDS) tailored for heterogeneous hazard levels. In this paper, we enhance network robustness by constructing (k,m)-CDS in high-risk regions, while reducing the number of CDS nodes by building a global (1, 1)-CDS for other regions, thereby designing the RRD-CDS algorithm. When failures cause the RRD-CDS to lose its properties as a CDS, we design a reconstruction algorithm to restore the fault tolerance of RRD-CDS. Simulation results verify the effectiveness of both the RRD-CDS construction algorithm and the RRD-CDS reconstruction algorithm.

title: Bipolar DC Bus Fluctuation Suppression of Electrolytic Capacitorless Vienna Rectifier for EV-Charging Stations
authors: Jiarui Wang, Gaolin Wang, Binxing Li, Guoqiang Zhang, Jianbing Chen, Jiangtao Yu, Dianguo Xu
abstract: Due to varying charging power and rates, electric vehicle (EV) charging stations require Vienna rectifiers to supply different DC-link voltages, and the reduction of capacitance leads to the increased bipolar DC bus voltage fluctuation and affects the grid-side power quality. A weighting fuzzy adaptive model predictive control (WFA-MPC) strategy is proposed to achieve synergistic suppression of the bipolar voltage fluctuations and the input current harmonics. The extreme components of the neutral point (NP) current are analyzed in conjunction with the clamping modes to characterize the voltage vector restricted areas of the conventional finite control set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) under the unbalanced DC voltage. To suppress input current distortion and the bipolar voltage fluctuations, the NP current control capability is increased by optimizing the finite set of voltage vectors in the restricted areas. Furthermore, a weighting fuzzy adaptive strategy is utilized to adjust the weight factor online according to the input current and unbalanced DC voltage errors, and the precise weight factor range is determined by the Pareto optimality. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed strategy is verified on an electrolytic capacitorless Vienna rectifier platform.

title: Near-Real-Time Multi-Parametric Quantitative MRI using Parallel Non-Cartesian 6D Spatial-Temporal Dictionary Learning Neural Networks_supp1-3685550.pdf
authors: Yiping P. Du
abstract: Objective: Multi-parametric quantitative MRI (qMRI) enables precise targeting during image-guided interventions such as deep brain stimulation. To address the demand for higher temporal resolution in multi-parametric qMRI of the brain, we propose an online pipeline for near-real-time quantitative MRI. Methods: The acquisition utilizes an alternating dual-flip-angle blipped multi-gradient-echo sequence with a stack-of-stars (SoS) trajectory, enabling efficient sampling while facilitating T1 mapping. K-space data are streamed to the Gadgetron framework for preprocessing and parallel non-Cartesian reconstruction. We extend the original 4D spatial-temporal dictionary learning neural networks (stDLNN), which exploit correlations across 2D space, time, and real/imaginary channels in dynamic MRI, to a 6D formulation for dual-flip-angle multi-echo complex-valued 3D reconstruction. The proposed 6D-stDLNN jointly exploits correlations across 3D space, echo, flip angle, and real/imaginary channels during reconstruction. Subsequently, quantitative calculations are efficiently performed on the GPU. Results: Prospective results from volunteer studies show good consistency (intraclass correlation coefficients > 0.9) between 3D multi-parametric maps from accelerated sampling and those from fully sampled references. In the slab-selective scan, the temporal resolution achieved was 8.1 s per frame, and the total algorithm runtime was below 5 s per frame. Conclusion: We present a near-real-time imaging pipeline that simultaneously provides 3D quantitative susceptibility mapping (QSM), R2* mapping, and T1 mapping, along with susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) and maximum intensity projection (MaxIP) images. Significance: This work demonstrates the feasibility of immediate multi-parametric quantitative assessment in the brain, indicating potential utility in time-critical applications and future interventional studies.

title: Analysis of Visible-Light Image and Flashover Voltage on the Cap-and-Pin Insulator With Fan-Shaped Pollution Distribution
authors: Mochammad Wahyudi, Tumiran Tumiran, Noor Akhmad Setiawan
abstract: Previous models have examined the relationship between insulator pollution level and flashover voltage, as well as between pollution levels and image-based features. This study investigated the potential correlation between visible-light images and flashover voltage. Porcelain cap-and-pin insulators were contaminated using a modified fan-shaped pollution distribution technique with a low-volume medium-pressure spray gun. Four pollution levels were produced by varying the ash–salt concentration (0.02–0.12 g/mL) and the number of spray gun trigger actuation (2-12). After ultrasonic humidification at 90% relative humidity for 90 min, the AC flashover tests were repeated at each pollution level until ten flashover voltages were obtained. Insulator images were captured under controlled conditions, including illumination (6,000 lm, 6,500 K), exposure parameters (ISO 500, f/16, 1/10 s), imaging geometry (1.3 m distance, 11.5° downward tilt, maximum optical zoom), and in-camera sRGB color space. Image processing involved selecting the fan-shaped area, cropping, background removal, conversion to RGB and HSV stacks, and histogram computation. Pearson’s correlation analysis showed that the histogram peak displacement between the dry and wet conditions of the pollutant layer was the most representative image feature with respect to flashover voltage. The RGB histogram peak displacement exhibited a higher correlation level than HSV, with the red component showing the greatest correlation coefficient (0.82). Furthermore, multivariable linear regression based on RGB histogram peak displacement yielded a coefficient of determination of 0.915. These results indicate that visible-light images can serve as non-contact predictors of the insulator pollution level and flashover voltage.

title: Nurturing Young Generation With Duality of Digital Intelligence and Human Wisdom
authors: Bing J. Sheu, Ching-Hsien Hsu
abstract: Advanced computers and versatile digital devices have dramatically changed the work environment for young generation in 21<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">st</sup> Century. Large language model-based artificial intelligence (A.I.) software packages such as ChatGPT and Gemini were introduced in 2022 and 2023, respectively. Teachers and students increasingly rely on A.I. software in teaching and learning processes. How to collaborate with A.I. software but remain high autonomy is very crucial to the young generation. First, education in engineering college is revisited that reveals the key to enable each discipline: civil engineering, mechanical engineering, electrical engineering in semiconductor field, computer science information engineering, and chemical engineering materials science. The key lies in increasing one extra dimension for more alternative solutions. Spectrum of computation ranges from analog processing, via digital computing, human brain processing, toward quantum computation. Artificial intelligence systems have brought significant challenges to young generation: both as a threat and as an opportunity. The students ought to be nurtured to think in higher dimension and to extend thinking from particle space to wave space. Mastering the duality of digital intelligence and human wisdom is the key for young generation to excel in 21<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">st</sup> Century.

title: Collision Location and Severity Vary in Different Automated Driving Technologies: Evidence from Real-world Accident Data Analysis
authors: Boyang Huang, Chunjiao Dong, Penghui Li, Dikang Meng, Xuedong Yan
abstract: The effectiveness of perception and decision-making technologies varies across complex and dynamic traffic environments, leading to differences in collision risks among automated vehicles (AVs). However, the previous studies mainly focused on investigating the effects of conventional human vehicle-road-environment factors on AVs collision, leaving the effects of automated driving technologies-related factors unknown. To fill up this gap, this study incorporated four additional automated driving technologies-related factors, including automation level, perception type, speed selection strategy, and regional development level into the existing influencing factor system, and verified them by the random forest algorithm. The restructured variable system demonstrates a 10% improvement in descriptive efficacy compared to the traditional one. Moreover, considering the connection between the AV accident severity and the collision location, a nested logit model was constructed to explore the key factors affecting the accident severity and collision location. Additionally, to address unobserved heterogeneity, a random parameters logit model was developed. Finally, a comparative analysis was conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of the two logit models in AV accident analysis. The results from 1,725 AV accidents revealed that AV accidents frequently occurred in highly developed regions, and over 40% AVs demonstrated impulsive speed selection strategy. The significant influencing factors identified by the two logit models were largely consistent. The outcomes of both models indicated that autonomous driving systems (SAE Level 3) and multiple sensors are the influencing factors of lateral and rear end collisions, and impulsive strategy increases the risk of injury in AV accidents. Moreover, the effects of autonomous driving systems, unmanned/remote-controlled driving, and conventional vehicle turning on rear-end non-injury AV accidents followed normal distributions. These findings suggest that different “perception-decision-control” technologies in automated driving have varying impacts on collision location and severity. Automakers should take these characteristics into consideration to optimize automated driving technologies and ensure AV safety.

title: Deep Multi-View Clustering via Cluster-Semantic Guidance
authors: Jinrong Cui, Xiaohuang Wu, Wai Keung Wong, Linlin Tang, Sen Xu, Jie Wen
abstract: Deep multi-view clustering aims to exploit the rich semantic information contained in heterogeneous multi-view data to uncover the underlying relationships among samples. However, existing deep multi-view clustering models often overlook intercluster separability and the effective integration of semantic information across views, resulting in insufficient feature discriminability and consequently limited clustering performance. To address the above issues, this paper proposes a novel deep multi-view clustering method via cluster-semantic guidance. We separate clusters to enhance inter-cluster discriminability, while incorporating a knowledge distillation mechanism to ensure cluster stability and facilitate the learning of clustering-friendly representations. Furthermore, by aggregating sample-level semantic information, the model is guided to follow a cluster-oriented learning strategy that promotes the extraction of discriminative features, thereby strengthening the sample representation capability. Our method effectively learns discriminative and clustering-friendly representations, guiding the model to acquire distinctive feature embeddings from a cluster-oriented perspective. Our comprehensive experiments across datasets of varying scales confirm the model’s effectiveness, showing superior clustering performance over existing state-of-the-art methods.
